This MJIYA 8X HANDHELD READING MAGNIFIER (for Kids and Seniors, Non-Scratch Quality Glass Lens, Shatterproof Design; 75mm, Orange) is budget-priced (about $7 on Amazon), and gives excellent value for the money. The magnifier is a little heavy, but that is because the glass lens is so large. The large lens means that you see a larger magnified area than you can with most inexpensive magnifiers.

The 8X magnification is a good feature, too. When you hold the magnifier at the correct focal length, you get a very clear, large image of print for reading. The lens shows little distortion, except as expected around the outer edges.

This STUDENTSZONE ELASTIC STRING FOR MASKS, SEWING (164 Yards (150M), 1/8" (3MM) Width, Black) is thin, but softly stretchy. I don’t know how durable it is.

I don’t think that it’s wide enough for the ear loops on handmade masks that are intended to be sold at retail. However, it works fine for family masks, or for nebulizer face mask straps, where it can be replaced frequently or as needed.

The 164-yard roll contains a lot of soft elastic string!

This DMHIRMG ZIPPER REPAIR KIT (with Zipper Pliers; Zipper Pulls, Zipper Stops for Clothing, Luggage, Backpacks) is a budget-priced (about $10 on Amazon) zipper repair kit. It includes (1) pincer pliers for removing slider stops; (2) an assortment of silver, brass, and black replacement sliders and slider stops; (3) loop-style zipper pulls; (4) an emergency sewing kit; and (5) a seam ripper.

Some, but not all, of the sliders are numbered so that you can match them to the slider you are replacing. Most of the sliders are the larger ones that will fit the bigger zippers you find on backpacks and similar items. The slider stoppers do NOT include the type of lower-edge stoppers that you find on sweatshirts--the kind where you insert a tube-like stopper into the slider.

To install the slider stops, you will need regular pliers (preferably needle-nose pliers), in addition to the included pincer pliers. The pincer pliers have short handles, like most craft-jewelry-making tools.

The kit provides pretty good value for the price. However, it does NOT include any instructions for making zipper repairs; and the tiny stops are packaged in the same bag as the big sliders. (You have to carefully sort the sliders and stops.) I didn't count the sliders and slider stoppers, but you get an assortment that's pretty close to the product page photo. Some of the sliders have two tabs (for reversible garments).

There are lots of YouTube videos available that explain how to make zipper repairs. One good one comes from the outdoor clothing outfitter, REI (try Googling for “REI zipper repair”).

VOGUE KNITTING: THE ULTIMATE QUICK REFERENCE is a “lite” version of the big, coffee-table-book-sized [[ASIN:1942021690 Vogue Knitting: The Ultimate Knitting Reference]]. It contains the technical how-to pages from the classic reference (the ones with the clear step-by-step drawings of stitches and yarn positions). However, the illustrations are just photographically reduced in size to fit an 8¼” x 7½” page, which makes the text on the pages harder to read. With the reduction, the book is still fairly large for a knitting-bag reference.

The big book has always been one of the very best, most comprehensive knitting references available. (The absolute best, in my opinion, is [[ASIN:0895774674 Montse Stanley’s out-of-print Knitter’s Handbook]].) This new, slender, travel version of the big book contains the following chapters: Basic Techniques (e.g., casting on, increases and decreases, stranded knitting); Advanced Techniques (e.g., entrelac, brioche knitting); Understanding Instructions (e.g., schematics, gauge); Correcting Errors (e.g., twisted stitches, adding lifelines); Finishing (e.g., blocking, seaming); Designing (e.g., taking measurements, short-row shaping); Embellishments (e.g., knitting with beads); and Tables and Tools (e.g., knitting needle sizes, yarn weights).

A nice feature is the heavy cardboard back flap that features a knitting stitch gauge (for counting your rows and stitches in a gauge swatch), and a needle gauge (for sizing your loose knitting needles). The book includes a short Bibliography and a complete Index.

If you already own the classic reference, you don’t need this “Quick Reference” unless you want it for your knitting bag. (For that purpose, I still prefer the smaller, index-card-size [[ASIN:1596683147 Knitter’s Companion by Vicki Square]], which is less complete, but is easier to read.) If you are deciding between this and the larger book, this one has all the technical how-to material that you’re ever likely to need.

These SLICE 10546 POINTED TIP CERAMIC SCISSORS (BPA, 1 Pack) have blades that are finger friendly (because ceramic doesn’t cut skin easily). However, the pointed tips are so sharp, they can inflict scratches and punctures without a whole lot of added force.

The short blades of these scissors limit their usefulness. I use them for cutting off bandages, cutting adhesive tape, etc. They do cut paper easily. The ceramic blades make very precise, clean cuts.

The handles aren’t particularly comfortable in my large hands, but the holes are large enough that they don’t trap my thumb or fingers. In general, I prefer conventional metal scissors to these ceramic ones.

This ELMER’S GLOW-IN-THE-DARK LIQUID GLUE (Washable, Natural, 5 Ounces) works great for drawing glow-in-the-dark pictures or decorations on plain paper. I made line drawings with a raised bead of glue, let them dry, exposed them to sunlight for 2 minutes, and tested them in a dark room. They really do glow! When the glow fades, you can reactivate it by exposing the glue to sunlight again. In ordinary light, the glue is sparkly, so it works as a decorative element on anything (invitations, scrapbooks, etc.).

The label says that the glue is “NOT for photos, bare metals, submerged or heated surfaces”. I understand the warnings about bare metals (glue won't stick), submerged surfaces (glue is washable), and heated surfaces (glue softens or melts). However, I’m puzzled by the warning about photos. I’m guessing that the glue may react with photographic chemicals and damage the images.

You can use this glue to make slime (see the recipe on the product page). The glow-in-the-dark feature makes it fun to play around with, in all kinds of creative crafts projects.

Quilters in particular will love this FISKARS 3 PIECE RULER/CONNECTOR SET (132060-1001), but it’s a wonderful tool for any kind of precision cutting. The wide rulers are made of 1/8” thick acrylic, so they’re heavy enough to help hold fabric or paper in place during cutting.

The two rulers can be combined for extra-long cuts or for right-angled corner cuts. The connector goes on top of the large and small rulers. When the rulers are properly aligned, you press down on the connector to engage the suction cups and secure the rulers.

The ruler is marked with 30-, 45-, and 60-degree angles, in addition to a grid. Because the ruler is transparent, it’s easy to see pattern placements underneath.

This SAUNDERS UHU TAC PROPOWER REUSABLE PUTTY (2.1 oz (50g), 21 pads (48680)) is useful to have on hand for attaching lightweight items to all sorts of surfaces. It’s definitely reusable, and it comes off smooth surfaces without leaving marks. (I’d recommend testing it in an unobtrusive area before using it on a painted wall, but would expect it to come off without pulling off a layer of paint.)

My favorite use is for re-attaching [[ASIN:B00AN5L8FS Air Wick Stick-Ups]] to the inside of my garbage can. I have to remove the Stick-Ups when I roll the can to the curb for pickup, or they’ll fall out with the trash when the can is emptied. This reusable putty makes it easy to pull them off and stick them back on.

The main key to success is to use enough putty pads to handle the weight of your object. This UHU TAC product is intended to hold items up to 6.6 lbs. “under optimal conditions”. For slightly lighter objects, try [[ASIN:B0085T0G16 UHU TAC HOMEDECO]].

This ARTEZA 12" x 18" ROTARY CUTTING MAT (Self-Healing, Double-Sided,3mm Thick) is an excellent size. For most hobby cutting jobs, you don’t need a mat that covers your entire worktable—the mat only needs to cover your immediate project working area.

The printed linear grid (1” squares) and angle markings are extremely helpful for cutting specific shapes (e.g., quilt pieces). The grid and markings are black on white on one side of the mat, and white on black on the other side. The mat is self-healing, so that shallow cuts will close up instead of leaving distracting permanent slice marks.

WARNINGS (to avoid damage to the mat): Avoid extreme heat, cold, or direct sunlight. (Don’t use an iron on the mat.) Avoid repeatedly cutting in the same spot, deep cuts, or sharp angles. Only use sharp rotary blades.

The best toy trains are the electric ones—like this one, the LIONEL 6-82427 “THE PATRIOT” U36B FREIGHT LIONCHIEF SET—that run on electrified track. The track pieces snap together, and are powered by a transformer plugged into an AC outlet. The train’s operation (start, stop, reverse, throttle, whistle, etc.) is controlled by a battery-powered remote (three AAA batteries are NOT included). Multiple remote frequencies are available, to accommodate multiple trains on the same track.

The LionChief locomotive can run on any O-gauge legacy, TMCC, or conventional transformer layout, as long as 18 volts is being supplied to the track. Families can run legacy trains (with Cab-1 or Cab-2 controllers) on the same track, at the same time, as this one (with the supplied remote).

The set can be expanded as desired by purchasing additional track sets, transformers, and freight cars (without the patriotic theme). The red, white, and blue engine, caboose, and freight cars, with their Presidential seals and flags, are unique to this special set. It recalls the patriotic color schemes that were applied to many real trains during the USA’s 1976 bicentennial year.

If you save the box and styrofoam packing, it will be easy to store the train, cars, track, transformer, and remote in the original box. The styrofoam is shaped to hold the engine and cars securely.

Fiskars is a brand you can rely on, when you're buying scissors. These FISKARS CRAFTS TOTAL CONTROL PRECISION SCISSORS (Non-Coated Razoredge) work flawlessly when I’m cutting paper shapes, or trimming custom-printed, adhesive-backed labels.

The blades really do cut all the way to the tips. I like the slider lock that easily locks the blades closed. It protects the blades when they're stored in a drawer, and also makes the scissors compact for storage.

This sleek, dark LIONEL DC COMICS BATMAN PHANTOM TRAIN SET - O-GAUGE, with the Batman symbol on the engine, makes a great starter set for a serious Batman fan, or a fabulous collector's-item addition to an existing O-gauge electric train layout. (The electricity-powered O-gauge track is used by many different kinds of electric train sets.)

Because it's a Lionel, it's a beautifully designed, high-quality model train, with fun features (e.g., headlight and cabin lights). It has an advanced remote control that moves it around the track. You can buy additional straight and curved track pieces to expand the supplied track layout.

I've loved real and model trains all my life--a train trip is my idea of a perfect vacation; a model train, the perfect toy. What I love most about this set is the menacing black finish, and the futuristic profile that screams "speed", and is so appropriate to Batman. This is a very special superhero-themed train set.

This battery-powered PENNSYLVANIA FLYER READY TO PLAY TRAIN SET is an inexpense standalone model train that's perfect for young children's play, or for holiday decorating (e.g., under the tree, or on a tabletop). Because it's a Lionel, it's a high-quality train with realistic detail. The track is NOT standard 0-gauge, so this is not a starter set suitable for expansion with other cars, or for adding to a larger train layout.

I've loved real and model trains all my life--a train trip is my idea of a perfect vacation; a model train, the perfect toy. What I love most about this set is that it features the ultimate classic steam engine, the Pennsylvania Flyer.

This REVELL FIRST ORDER SPECIAL FORCES TIE FIGHTER is a sturdy model that's terrific for zooming play. It's nicely weighted and feels good in the hand. When you press a button, the cockpit lights up in red, and it makes a shooting and swooping sound. (To my ear, the sound has a snarling, animal-like quality, but I haven't seen the movie, so it could be completely accurate.) The sound is moderately loud--loud enough for play, but not loud enough to annoy parents.

As a display model, it's very good, with lots of interesting molded-in detail. However, the flip-open cockpit cover doesn't close completely flush with the body, which detracts a little from the display quality. (Pressing down on the cover while pressing forward on the hinge helps to close the cockpit cover as much as possible.)

It took me 15 minutes to assemble. Most of that time was spent identifying the top and bottom body parts, which need to be compared closely to the drawings. A 6-year-old child would probably need help with the assembly. The parts snap together in just 8 steps, but some need to be positioned exactly right for them to snap together snugly. (The instructions warn: "Caution: Position all parts in this kit carefully. Parts will only snap together once." However, the parts can be pried apart with a spudger tool if you start them a little crooked.)

Overall, I think this is a toy that gives extremely good value. It has a lot of play potential--it really calls out to you to pick it up and "fly" it. It comes with two round 1.5v LR44 non-rechargeable batteries already installed, and the batteries are replaceable.

The introduction to KNITTING WIZARDRY informs us that these "magical patterns were first published by Interweave in a publication that must not be named." That publication is, of course, the special 2013 Interweave knitting magazine that was titled "The Unofficial Harry Potter Knits". If you own the magazine, you don't need this book--all 27 patterns are in the magazine, and the magazine has at least three additional patterns.

In the book, all of the original pattern titles and descriptions have been changed to delete the Harry Potter references (e.g., "Order of the Phoenix Winged Vest" is now the "Aviary Vest"; "Hagrid's Sweater" is now the "Giant's Sweater"). Without a doubt, the pattern collection isn't nearly as much fun without the original Harry Potter theme, even though the photos with models made up to resemble the series' characters are still there.

Most of the 27 garments in the collection are knit from laceweight, sock weight, sportweight, or DK yarn, on U.S. sizes 3-6 needles. My absolute favorite is the "Tracery Vest", a charted Fair Isle V-neck pullover that resembles Gothic stained glass windows--it uses multicolored yarn to represent the window glass panels, and this makes the design somewhat easier to knit. I bought the yarn to be able to make this one for myself!

Other patterns that I like a lot are the "Owl Cardigan" (a complex lace cable resembling an owl's head runs down the middle of the cardigan back); the "Sorcery Sweater" (a long-sleeved, masculine unisex pullover with an elaborately interlocking Celtic cable design in front); the "Mermaid's Song" shawl (a lace shawlette with a wave pattern and bead-tipped fringe); the "Inky Sweater" (a long-sleeved, masculine unisex pullover with an elaborately cabled body); the "Lady of the House Wrapper" (a pretty shrug with an allover diamond lace pattern and an unusual front closing treatment); the "Petal Socks" (traditional top-down socks with twisted-stitch patterning); and the "Lacy Cloak" (a sleeved and collared lace cloak, with body knit in one piece to the underarms, then divided into two fronts and a back--definitely a project for advanced knitters).

All in all, this is an excellent pattern collection, worthy of a 5-star rating even though the patterns have already been published in magazine format. I only wish that Interweave could have found a way to warn prospective purchasers (like myself), that the patterns aren't new ones.

KNIT YOUR OWN ZOO is another fine collection of patterns for small knitted display animals (not really suitable for knitted toys) from the authors of [[ASIN:1579128742 Knit Your Own Dog]] and [[ASIN:1579128939 Knit Your Own Cat]]. These patterns are well-written, with clear instructions, but probably require advanced intermediate knitting skills (or beyond), because each animal is constructed from many small, shaped parts.

For example, the wonderful giraffe is assembled from (1) a right back leg, (2) a left back leg, (3) a right front leg, (4) a left front leg, (5) a right side of body, (6) a left side of body, (7) a head, (8) a tummy, (9) ears, (10) tail, and (11) mane. The parts for the legs are held on stitch holders until it's time to add the body; some body stitches are also held on stitch holders until it's time to add the head; and so on. The giraffe's "spots" are knitted in using Fair Isle and Intarsia methods. The mane is crocheted; the eyes are embroidered; the tail uses buttonhole stitch; the "horns" are pipe cleaners. To finish, pipe cleaners are added for support when the animal is stuffed. The patterns are written out with row-by-row instructions. The animals are knit with fine yarn on US 2 and US 3 needles.

It amazes me, how well the authors replicate the animals' essential characteristics, so that each finished animal is immediately recognizable as the zoo animal it is intended to be. I really love the giraffe, the mandrill, the bat, the armadillo, the koala, the leopard, and the crocodile!

Patterns are included for the following animals: giant anteater, armadillo, fruit bat, ring-tailed lemur, brown bear, wolf, gray seal and pup, polar bear, penguin, crocodile, koala, chimpanzee, lion, leopard, tiger, zebra, mandrill, camel, meerkat, elephant, giraffe, panda, orangutan, and kangaroo and joey.

I like to read British knitting magazines for the excellent articles and patterns using advanced knitting techniques, because American knitting magazines seem to focus primarily on relatively simple patterns that use only basic techniques. This book, KNITTING MASTERCLASS, is a collection of wonderful articles, all previously published in THE KNITTER (launched in January 2009), that teach the finer points of knitting.

There are many articles that justify the price of this book, but my favorites are (1) Judy Furlong's "Correcting Mistakes in Lace" (unpicking by stitches, rows, repeats; fixing missed or dropped yarnovers; joining yarns with the Russian join); (2) Jane Crowfoot's "Flawless Fair Isle" (one-handed and two-handed methods of holding yarns; importance of tension; concept of yarn dominance; eliminating ends to weave in); (3) Belinda Boaden's "Confident Cables" (reading cable charts; fixing mistakes in cables; avoiding baggy stitches); (4) Emma King's "Re-Colouring Fair Isle Designs" (using a color wheel; how value works; warm and cool relationships; balanced neutrals); (5) Jane Crowfoot's "Creating Seamless Colour Changes" (jogless stripes; spiral stripes; avoiding loose stitches at color changes); and (6) Belinda Boaden's "Altering Patterns to Work With Alternative Yarns" (pattern mathematics and schematics explained).

In addition, this book has 15 gorgeous patterns for intermediate and advanced knitters that incorporate the techniques. The projects are (1) Sedgemoor Cardigan; (2) Elwood Hat; (3) Nexus Socks; (4) Baird Collar; (5) Cable Cushion Covers; (6) Golden Wheat Lace Shawl (shown on the book's cover); (7) Chic Spring Beret; (8) Carmine V-Neck Jumper; (9) Milne Tank Top; (10) Abella Cushion; (11 & 12) Cuthbert Scarf & Hat; (13) Scooter Socks; (14) Climbing Mistletoe Socks; and (15) Charleston Cushions. Every single one of these classic designs produces an article that I would wear or use, and would enjoy knitting.

The article contributors and designers are Belinda Boaden, Judy Furlong, Jane Crowfoot, Emma King, Annie Modesitt, Woolly Wormhead, Judy Becker, Kirstie McLeod, Todd Gocken, Jon Dunn-Ballam, Amanda Crawford, Jeanette Sloan, and Jen Arnall-Culliford.

Magazine articles rarely say everything that there is to say about a given knitting technique, so this collection is not a comprehensive reference book. However, because I don't have enough storage space available to keep my subscription copies of THE KNITTER, I'm really pleased that the editors have decided to republish the magazine's technical articles in this single hardcover volume. As knitting magazine articles go, these are well above average in quality, because they consistently talk about the most important (and sometimes surprisingly obscure) things that serious knitters ought to know about each technique.

This card of SINGER MERCERIZED COTTON HAND THREAD is only 7" tall. You get 12 spools of cotton thread, but each spool is only 1" high, and contains only 25 yards of thread. This means that each spool has only enough thread for doing a small amount of minor mending or for sewing on a few buttons. You'll quickly run out of white and black, and you may never use the other colors at all. The colors are: black, white, charcoal, pale yellow, brown, beige, dark green, light green, red, pink, navy, pale blue.

In my opinion, the product page photo is a little misleading. I'll concede that the price (a little under $5) should have alerted me that the spools were not standard-sized spools, but I was definitely disappointed to receive these small, sewing-kit-sized spools. The cotton thread is nice enough. I rate this product at 3 stars ("It's okay" on the official Amazon scale).

The SINGER 5532 HEAVY DUTY SEWING MACHINE is a high-speed machine that sews 1,100 stitches per minute. It's a lot faster than my ancient Singer "Touch & Sew" machine (a Christmas gift from the 60s, still working fine), but it's not as fast as the Pendleton factory machine that I operated one summer as a minimum-wage temporary worker. (Those factory machines really fly!) Nevertheless, this machine is plenty fast for home sewing, and it makes short work of both straight-stitch seaming and decorative stitching.

The Singer 5532 has an all-metal frame, but it is NOT an all-metal sewing machine. Much of the outer housing is made of some sort of high-impact plastic. Even so, it IS a "heavy duty" machine, because the weight of the frame holds the machine solidly on your sewing table (so that the machine won't shift around and cause the needle to skip as you sew).

Because I've done a lot of machine sewing, I found the SINGER 5532 easy to set up and use--even though I've never worked with a zigzag machine before. The manual has line drawings and instructions (written in "near English" that includes some odd phrases) that tell you how to wind a bobbin, thread the machine, make buttonholes, etc. If you've never used a sewing machine before, you may have some trouble threading the Singer 5532, but there are additional drawings on the Singer web site that clear up any difficulties you might have in working only with the manual. I needed to consult the Singer site to learn why my bobbins weren't winding evenly. (The fix is to wind the thread twice (not once) around the bobbin tension post.)

The features that I love the most about this machine are probably available on other Singer machines and competing-brand machines, but they are new to me.

***First, the "automatic needle threader", which isn't really "automatic" but does make it easy to thread the sewing machine needle. (You pull down a swing-arm device, lay your thread over a couple of hooks, and the device pulls the thread through the needle eye--yes!)

***Second, the automatic bobbin-winder atop the machine that you can use to wind additional bobbins even when the machine is fully threaded. (This machine comes with five Class 15 metal bobbins.)

***Third, the included buttonhole presser foot that holds a button so that the buttonhole will automatically be sized correctly. This little foot does the same work as a huge attachment (about 6" long and 3" high, and expensive) for my old "Touch & Sew" machine that must be manually adjusted for the buttonhole size. I'm a little concerned about the durability of the Singer 5532's nylon (or hard plastic) buttonhole foot, because ratchets made from nylon can wear out quickly. However, the buttonhole foot probably can be replaced as needed at relatively low cost.

***Fourth, the drawings on the machine itself that tell how to thread the machine. Drawings on the machine also tell how to thread the bobbin winder.

***And finally, the zigzag stitch dials that let you sew with 32 different stitch patterns. The dials take some getting used to, because you must adjust the stitch width (and sometimes the stitch length as well) to get the patterns shown on the main dial. But it's fun to play with scrap fabric until you get the stitch settings right.

The SINGER 5532 is an extremely portable machine. It's not as heavy as my old "Touch & Sew", and it has a flip-up carrying handle that makes it easy to pick up and set onto a sewing table. Also, the machine fits comfortably into the inexpensive but sturdy [[ASIN:B000WOM50W SINGER 611.BR Universal Hard Carrying Case for Most Free-Arm Sewing Machines]].

Since I've only had this machine for a month, I can't say how durable it is over the long term, or how well it holds up under heavy use. But what I can say, is that from what I've already seen, I like this sewing machine very much.

Caitlin Sainio's book, 100 LACE FLOWERS TO CROCHET, presents a collection of very pretty crocheted flower and leaf motifs. A few of the motifs have double layers, but most are flat, single-layer motifs. You must look elsewhere, if you want patterns for three-dimensional flowers and leaves. However, the "spring tea cozy" and "colorful corsage" projects (photos and captions only, no explicit instructions for actually making the projects) illustrate how three-dimensional effects can be achieved by piling up these motifs (next to and one on top of another).

The flower motifs are beautifully photographed. They are displayed in a gallery with captions that identify the particular flowers represented, such as hibiscus, plumeria, alyssum, petunia, sunflower. In most instances, I cannot identify the flowers simply by looking at the photos--but when prompted by the captions, I can see that the motifs do resemble the named flowers. It's okay with me, that the motifs are somewhat "generic floral", because they are colorful, attractive, and decorative. My favorites are the Pansy, the Sunflower, the Tulip (almost three-dimensional), and the Poinsettia.

The book includes a "Crochet Refresher Course" section that explains basic crochet techniques. Each motif is presented with both written-out instructions and a chart. In addition to the flower motifs, there are generic leaf and stem motifs, as well as some butterflies, a dragonfly, and a caterpillar. Most of the 100 motifs represent different flowers, but the insects and some special leaves (e.g., Coleus) are included in the total count. The flower motifs shown on the book's cover are good examples of what you'll find in the rest of this pretty collection.

METROPOLITAN KNITS is a collection of patterns for elegant, classic cardigans and tops--with simple but creative variations that make them interesting to knit, to look at, and to wear. A good example is the Meier Cardigan (shown on the book cover), a classic cardigan with a gently shaped stockinette body that is embellished with chevron eyelet side panels and sleeves.

As Heidi Klum of Project Runway would say, "I'd wear that!" In fact, I'd wear every single one of the designs in this collection! Although the lines are classic, the patterns are adjusted to give the currently fashionable snug fit. There are also a few patterns for scarves, hats, cowls, and shawls. My personal favorites are the Meier Cardigan; the Open Air Pullover (a drapey faux-wrap top with gathers at the shoulders); the Cobblestone Hoodie (a long-sleeved, striped V-neck pullover with generous attached hood); the Atrium Cardigan (a minimalist V-neck cardigan knit in open-stitch lace); the Brooklyn Bridge Cardigan (a no-buttonband cardigan with lace panels along the fronts and around the edge of the neckline and generous attached hood); and the Grand Army Plaza Shawl (a pretty little half-circle shawl knit in open-stich lace).

I can't make up my mind about the Carriage House Cardigan--usually I don't like tops with long triangles of knitting hanging at the sides, but this one drapes nicely and has an interesting drawstring detail at the back which also helps the drape. A design that has a fun surprise is the Secret Garden Tank, which looks like a plain Henley-neckline tank, but has a fancy lace insert in back (sort of like a peplum), knit in a contrasting color.

None of the designs appear difficult to knit--intermediate (or adventurous intermediate) knitting skills are probably all that's needed. Most of the cardigans and tops are knit from sportweight yarn, although a few call for fingering or worsted weight yarn. The needle sizes vary from pattern to pattern (US 3 to US 7), but there is a fair amount of knitting required for all of them--these are not big-needle, make-in-a-day designs.

The book's patterns are presented in three sections. FIRST, Heart of the City--Opera House Mitts, Uptown Scarf, Museum Sweater, Le Cirque Cowlneck Sweater, Meier Cardigan, Open Air Pullover, Skyline Tunic. SECOND, Urban Bohemia--Bleecker Street Cardigan, Magnolia Cafe Cardigan, Washington Square Cardigan, Washington Square Hat, Union Square Cowl. And THIRD, City Gardens--Cobblestone Hoodie, Atrium Cardigan, Brooklyn Bridge Cardigan, Sunday Henley, Carriage House Cardigan, Secret Garden Tank, Grand Army Plaza Shawl, Courtyard Pullover.

SWEATER GIRLS is a lovely collection of classic designs from the 1930s, 1940s, and 1950s--all updated by two well-known designers, Weston and Taylor--that use currently available yarns and are sized for today's women. The patterns are divided into four types: pretty tops (7 designs), cute cardigans (4), starlet sweaters (6), twinsets & two-pieces (3), and finishing touches (4). The four "finishing touches" are a thick lace stole, a Fair Isle beret, lace stockings, and lace wrist warmers. The modern yarns used include merino, angora, cotton, bamboo, cashmere, alpaca, and silk--some of them suitably glamorous for the movie-star-inspired designs.

My favorite design is the "Wallis pleated cardigan", with wide ribbed waist (to the bust line), ribbed shoulders, gathers at the bust, narrow roll collar, and button-front. This style is flattering to most figures, and so versatile it can be worn with almost anything. I also love the "Ava angora bolero", a fuzzy angora shrug with curved fronts and a beaded band of ribbing.

Many of the patterns will probably look best on full-figured women (like the "sweater girls" who inspired the designs). However, in addition to "Wallis", the demure and alluring "Kate lace-panel blouse", with its short sleeves and scoop neckline and openwork fabric, should look great on anyone.

If you're looking for elegance, you will probably love the "Natalie feather-stitch sweater", a long-sleeve pullover with a round scoop neck and allover vertical rows of feather stitch. A tailored look can be had with the "Celia Lacy Fine-Knit Twinset", comprised of a short-sleeved pullover with a stylized panel of chevrons, and a v-neck cardigan that uses the same chevron pattern in a band along the fronts and around the neckline.

There isn't a single pattern in this book that I wouldn't want to knit! (Of course, I really love the "vintage" look in knitting patterns.) Most of the designs call for fine yarn, knitted on US 2 or US 3 needles, and for shaping. These designs require patience--they'll take a while to knit because of the fine yarns used--but I think they can be knit by adventurous intermediate as well as advanced knitters. There are many full-color photos of the sweaters, usually shown on models in luxurious surroundings but sometimes shown on hangers as well.

If you love vintage knitting patterns, and have some old patterns that you'd like to adapt for yourself, then you might also be interested in [[ASIN:1596688394 Vintage Design Workshop: Knitting Techniques for Modern Style]]. I like SWEATER GIRLS better, because the authors have already done all the work of updating these fabulous classic patterns for us knitters.

What I like most about KNITTED WIRE JEWELRY is that the finished pieces look like commercial jewelry, because they're made entirely of metal wire, sheet metal, and semiprecious stones. I purchased the book because I liked the necklace on the book's cover: that design is fairly representative of the pieces in the book.

This book is an excellent addition to a knitter's technical bookshelf. About one-third of the book is devoted to tools, choosing materials, wire knitting techniques, and knitted jewelry techniques (e.g., wrapping wire, adding beads, forming sheet metal, applying a patina). The techniques are explained step by step, and the steps are clearly illustrated with large full-color photos.

There are patterns for rings (5), earrings (6), bracelets (7), and necklaces (7). My favorite patterns are the Lily Necklace (the cover design), the Viking necklace, bracelet, and hoops (simple knitted metal cords), the Mixed-Link Bracelet (three sheet metal rectangles with knitted interiors, all linked together with knitted cords), the Leaf Earrings (a single leaf with wrapped-wire veins), and the Opal Rectangle Earrings (long, dangly strips with single pear-cut Peruvian Opals attached as pendants).

Probably I'll never actually make any of these pieces, but the book gives me all the information I need to create my own professional-looking wire jewelry based on knitting techniques. Two other good books on knitted wire jewelry are Nancie Wiseman's [[ASIN:1931499314 Knitting with Wire]], and Annie Modesitt's [[ASIN:0307340198 Twist and Loop: Dozens of Jewelry Designs to Knit and Crochet with Wire]]. Of the three, I like KNITTED WIRE JEWELRY the best, because of the excellent photographs illustrating the techniques, and the elegant simplicity of the designs.

In compiling this collection of traditional but thoroughly modern afghans, crochet designer Robin Chachula asked other well-known designers "to use the afghan as a vehicle to showcase their favorite aspect of crochet." The designers responded with afghans using crochet cables, colorwork, lace, granny motifs, and Tunisian crochet.

In purchasing this book, I was hoping for more of the "unexpected". Thus I was somewhat disappointed by the "traditional" look of most of the afghan designs. Two of the most intriguing designs are Vika (Celtic cables and knots) and Bethlehem Star (inspired by the Bethlehem Star quilt pattern). However, Vika's central Celtic knot is accomplished with applied I-cord, and Bethlehem Star's motifs are made from small, individually-crocheted pieces (the rays of the stars) that must be sewn together. Thus I can't see myself making either afghan, although both are quite pretty.

However, you can't go wrong with a pattern collection published by Interweave Press! The crochet instructions are given in both written-out and charted form, and the full-color photographs of the afghans (including closeups showing important details) are beautiful. The individual patterns include quotations from the designers, plus tips for making the particular afghan look its best. For example, the Vika tip explains how to avoid catching your stitches on other stitches when you're working post stitches behind stitches already worked; the Cabine de Dentelle tip explains the importance of blocking the squares by carefully ironing them.

Many of the designers have contributed short motivational "Beginner Tips" in the book's introduction. The cable designs are by Robyn Chachula, Marty Miller, Drew Emborsky, Annie Modesitt, Jill Wright, and Simona Merchant-Dest. The granny motif designs are by Robyn Chachula, Linda Permann, Ellen Gormley, Edie Eckman, Annette Petavy, and Tammy Hildebrand. The colorwork designs are by Robyn Chachula, Kathryn Merrick, Leigh Radford, Carol Ventura, Kimberly McAlindin, and Mary Beth Temple. The lace designs are by Robyn Chachula, Tracie Barrett, Kristin Ohmdahl, Marly Bird, and Doris Chan. The Tunisian crochet designs are by Robyn Chachula, Darla Fanton, Diane Halpern, Dora Ohrenstein, Kim Guzman, and Megan Granholm.

I love dogs and cats (especially cats), and wanted to love this book as much as [[ASIN:1579128742 Knit Your Own Dog: Easy-to-Follow Patterns for 25 Pedigree Pooches]] by the same talented authors. But I can't, because most of the cats don't look like cats. Some have pointy snouts (more like rats); some have overlarge ears; many have heads too small for their bodies.

Like the small figures in [[ASIN:1579128742 Dog]], the cat figures represent many different breeds: Persian, Ragdoll, Maine Coon, Turkish Van, British Shorthair, Devon Rex, Abyssinian, Burmese, Siamese, and Housecat (orange, black and white, tabby, black, and tortoiseshell). There are also some kittens, curled up cats, and a Bengal tiger. Some of the cats are sitting, some standing.

But unlike the figures in [[ASIN:1579128742 Dog]], I cannot identify the cats' individual breeds by simply looking at the figures. The Persian's loopy mohair coat doesn't resemble fur (and the figure reminds me of a Pekingese). The Turkish Van's loopy mohair tail doesn't look like a cat's big, fluffy tail. The short-haired Siamese is white with brown face, ears, paws, and tail, but somehow the figure doesn't evoke the exotic appearance of a Siamese.

For me, the most successful figure is the stripey Orange Cat (a seated cat). However, if I were to make it, I would change the colors and try to blend the stripes to make the coat look more realistic. Perhaps the small scale of the figures makes it impossible to represent the individual characteristics of the various cat breeds in fabric knitted from fine yarn on US 2 needles.

Like the dog figures, the cat figures have many parts and are somewhat complicated to knit and assemble. I rate this book at 3 stars ("it's okay" on the Amazon scale), because none of the cats appeal to me enough to make me want to knit them, especially given the complicated construction.

This slender little book (6" x 9"; 46 pp) offers 14 different patterns for tube socks knit in brioche stitch and half-brioche stitch. The advantage of tube socks is that there's no heel to turn--the only shaping required is for the toe. However, tube socks themselves have a disadvantage: they don't wear as well as socks with standard heels.

The advantage of brioche stitch is that it creates a stretchy fabric that shapes itself to the leg, heel, and foot, making it perfect for tube socks. Half-brioche stitch is easy to knit, once you get into the swing of it. In the first row you make pairs of yarnovers and stitches slipped purlwise; in the second row, you purl the yarnovers together with slipped stitches. Brioche stitch is almost as easy, but it has an extra repeating step on the second row.

Tube socks are knitted in the round. This book gives complete instructions (written-out and illustrated by full-color photos of stitches on the needles) for knitting both brioche and half-brioche stitch in the round on double-pointed needles. It includes useful sock sizing/sock knitting charts (based on Regia 4-ply, 6-ply, and Kids yarns) for brioche-stitch tube socks in multiple sizes (Child's S,M,L,XL; Women's XS,S,M,L,XL; Men's S,M,L,XL,2X). For each sock size and yarn weight, the charts give (1) the number of stitches to cast on; (2) the number of stitches to place on each of your four stitch-carrying needles; (3) the leg length in inches; (4) the number of sole and instep rounds; and (5) the number of rounds for the toe.

The patterns in this book did not bowl me over. Because brioche stitch produces a ribbed-look fabric, most of the 14 patterns produce socks that resemble the ones shown in the cover photo. The patterns are varied (1) by using more than one color of yarn, in horizontal or vertical stripes; (2) by adding texture with garter stitch bands or garter stitch on the sock "tops"; or (3) by using netted (honeycomb) brioche stitch. The skill level for the patterns is easy (6), intermediate (5), and experienced (3). Overall, this is an attractive, well-done book with full-color photos printed on glossy paper. However, I rate it at 3.5 stars, rounded down to 3 stars because the content is so limited.

Dog lovers may want to knit these 25 charming "pedigree pooches" as a display set, although it will be a lot of work! Each little dog--they range from 6.75" high x 8" long (Afghan) to 3.5" x 6.25" (Corgi)--has a lot of parts, which need to be joined or sewn together, stuffed, and embroidered with noses and eyes. For example, the cover dog (Wire-haired Fox Terrier) requires knitting two back legs, two front legs, a right side and tail, a left side and tail, a body, a neck and head, a tummy, two ears, and a collar.

The designers have done a fine job in capturing the essentials of each breed. My favorites are the Poodle (knit in boucle yarn) and the wrinkly English Bulldog; my least favorites are the Labrador and Dalmatian, which are still quite identifiable by their breeds. Breeds included are: Afghan Hound, Whippet, Dachshund, Basset Hound, Wire-haired Fox Terrier, Jack Russell, Scottish Terrier, West Highland Terrier, English Bull Terrier, Cocker Spaniel, Red Setter, Labrador, Portuguese Water Dog, Dalmatian, Poodle, Miniature Schnauzer, French Bulldog, English Bulldog, Pug, Rough Collie, Border Collie, German Shepherd, Old English Sheepdog, Corgi, and Siberian Husky. Experienced, creative knitters can no doubt adapt the patterns for other breeds.

The patterns call for small amounts of various Rowan yarns (Pure Wool, Kidsilk Haze, Cashsoft, Felted Tweed, etc.), but the authors include a brief note on substituting yarns. If I were knitting these, I would use something other than Kidsilk Haze for the Schnauzer's long muzzle hair and the Rough Collie's thick "mane", as the thin mohair isn't quite convincing as a dog's silky coat.

I haven't knit-tested the patterns, but the instructions seem very clear. Stitch counts are included at the end of increase/decrease rows, and close-up photos illustrate head details and leg angles (for sewing to bodies). I was briefly confused by the instructions "k3wa" and "k2cr", but these merely refer to yarn colors ("wa" for "walnut" and "cr" for "cream"). Special stitches, such as loopy stitches, are explained at the end of the book, and there are hints on stuffing, and on adding pipecleaners as needed to strengthen legs.

This is a book for experienced knitters, as the patterns call for a lot of shaping, saving stitches on holders, and some special techniques. However, any moderately experienced but adventurous knitter should be able to knit the dogs from the pattern instructions. If you're a real dog lover, this is a 5-star book, because it's fun to look through even if you don't want to knit the dogs. If you're mainly interested in patterns for knitted toys, then better choices might be Debbie Bliss' [[ASIN:0312119011 Toy Knits: More Than 30 Irresistible and Easy-to-Knit Patterns]], Kath Dalmeny's [[ASIN:0715312243 World of Knitted Toys]], Zoe Mellor's [[ASIN:158180900X Knitted Toys: 25 Fresh and Fabulous Designs]], or Sarah Keen's [[ASIN:082303318X Knitted Wild Animals: 15 Adorable, Easy-to-Knit Toys]].

KNIT KIMONO TOO, like the author's previous book, [[ASIN:1931499896 Knit Kimono: 18 Designs with Simple Shapes]], presents a varied collection of pretty patterns for knitted kimonos. The patterns are carefully thought out: asymmetric neckline closings lie flat (re-su yugata), traditional front-openings hang straight (tsui-do hanten), and fashionable uneven hems form controlled vees (nishiki) or gently irregular curves (keshi murasaki).

There is a brief, interesting historical discussion of the Japanese tradition of layering five (or as many as twenty) different-colored robes according to "approved" color sets. In keeping with this layering tradition, the book includes patterns for several simple tank tops (with minimalist border or panel decorations) that can be coordinated with and worn beneath the kimonos.

This book also has clear instructions for several sophisticated couture knitting techniques: a placketed v-neck, a decorative knotted i-cord button band, a shaped multi-color-paneled front band, a traditional Japanese rectangular sleeve, an asymmetric wrapped neckline, a short-row-shaped border band, and applied embellishments that use embroidery or crochet chains. In the included DVD, author Vicki Square demonstrates exactly how to accomplish any unusual techniques called for in the patterns, and also explains how to carry out other techniques such as multi-color patterning with slip stitches (mosaic knitting). The DVD is produced with the high professional quality that one has come to expect from Interweave Press.

To see some of the designs, take a minute to view the "Look Inside This Book" feature at the top of the page. The photos clearly show how well-designed these kimonos are, because none of the models are posed in unnatural or contorted positions to hide flaws. If you have the time to visit the publisher's web site, you can also check out a You Tube video of Vicki Square demonstrating her techniques to Shay Pendray, which can be found on the book description page. KNIT KIMONO TOO is a welcome and valued addition to my knitting book collection!

Far and away the best book yet on entrelac knitting! This is the book that I wish I'd had, when I first attempted entrelac. It has big, beautiful photos that illustrate each step in the entrelac process, and it includes detailed information on things like how to avoid holes where the triangles and rectangles are joined. It also explains how to knit backward (for increased speed), how to pick up stitches (from garter and stockinette edges), and how to knit entrelac in the round.

But it gets better. It also includes written-out pattern instructions (no charts, sad to say) for knitting fancy, non-stockinette rectangles that can be combined with plain stockinette rectangles to add interest to entrelac fabric. The "designer" rectangles incorporate lace patterns, cables, relief designs, geometric designs, colorwork, beads, felting, embroidery, and "melange" techniques such as two-color garter stripes. Each "designer" rectangle pattern is illustrated by a large full-color swatch photo. By my count, there are more than 50 patterns for different fancy rectangles.

And it gets even better. There are attractive patterns for garments that use entrelac accent panels (highly-textured entrelac fabric can be overpowering if a garment is knit entirely in entrelac). There are also patterns for pillows, socks, purses, blankets, cowls, scarves, shawls, and long mitts. The garment patterns include: (1) uptown poncho, (2) rose garden baby cardi, (3) Edwardian cardigan, (4) shades of gray vest, and (5) garden path jacket. I particularly like the baby cardigan, which has an entrelac border with embroidered roses, and the garment patterns that use stripes in combination with entrelac sections. All of the patterns are basic enough to have general appeal, without being boring. Moderately experienced knitters should have no trouble knitting the patterns, because the entrelac technique is easy to master, even though it appears complicated.

Finally, there is a brief chapter on entrelac designing that includes a discussion about figuring out gauge. I really love this book, which belongs on any serious knitter's reference shelf. Another good new book on entrelac knitting is [[ASIN:1933064196 Entree to Entrelac: The Definitive Guide from a Biased Knitter]], which has quite a bit of technical how-to information, but is primarily a collection of patterns which mostly use plain stockinette rectangles.

I love the cushioned grip on these - in fact, the handles (and the bit of weight they add) might be my favorite part of these scissors. The package says these are for office or home use, and I would agree. Although I've used them for some light crafting, these are definitely not the most appropriate scissors for cutting fabric - there are much nicer shears for that function, at roughly the same price (on sale).

The larger, thicker blades, plus the ergonomic grip and higher quality construction mean I'll probably be hiding these scissors from the kids (who would just gunk them up cutting off the tops of their Gogurts and such). Glad to have a nice pair of scissors when I need them!

LOVES

DISLIKES:

The current price of $9.40 ($9.89 minus 5%) is reasonably priced. If one does not mind dumping the pill cases to get to them, then I recommend this for that price. However, I personally want to be able to remove a day's worth of pills without dumping the case.

This is my first foray into drawing (see hideous photo of my sketching attempt) meaning this set is overkill for me but provides room for growth as I enhance my skills. The set comes with a case with placeholders for 39 items inside. The drawing pad is the only item that cannot go in the case. Below are the 40 items included, excluding the case.

- 1 100 sheet 6"x9" sketch pad
- 3 drawing pens (.035mm, 1.0mm, 2.5mm)
- 3 charcoal sticks
- 3 graphite sticks
- 4 blending tools (1 rubberized double side & 3 paper double sided)
- 1 dual pencil extender
- 2 metal sharpeners (1 single, 1 double)
- 2 erasers (1 vinyl, 1 kneaded)
- 16 graphite pencils (6H, 5H, 4H, 3H, 2H, HB, B, 2B, 3B, 4B, 5B, 6B, 7B, 8B, 9B)
- 1 woodless graphite pencil
- 1 white charcoal pencil
- 3 black charcoal pencils

Based on my YouTube research, the B on the pencils stands for black. The H on the pencils stands for hardness. The higher the number on the pencil, the softer and darker it is when it is followed by a B and the lower the number the harder and lighter it is when followed by a B. The higher the number on the pencils with the H, the harder they get. HB stands for hard in blackness and would be considered a normal pencil. All this is a bit confusing. I attached an image that I grabbed off of pencils dot com (not I cannot include links in my reviews) which I find easier to understand. Besides these pencils, included are blending tools, pens, etc. that are all used in the drawing process.

The case is nice but some items do fall out of the elastics (like the two pencil sharpeners and the graphite sticks). To get the case to display the pencils in an upright position, pull the organizer sleeve on each side away from the Velcro holding it flush with the case, and then fold down the top half of the back. Once the top half of the back is folded on each side, the Velcro on the rim will stick to the Velcro on the case "floor" and prop up the implements as shown in my photo.

In summary, this kit has everything needed for an aspiring sketcher or one who has been around sketching for a while and likes to add more implements to their arsenal. However, most experienced sketchers have probably graduated to buying their items individually and not in a set as they would know the type of drawing implements they like and would want to save money by purchasing them separately.  I ordered this set at the $24.99 price but now it is being offered for $22.99 ($24.99 minus $2) which I consider a steal.

When I first saw this, I figured it would be great, especially since it matches my purple bathroom. The current price of $15.90 is a bit price for this, in my opinion.

UPDATE: I ordered these in the [[ASIN:B079KH9Q52 blue color]] as well and that pair was missing the safety sheath. I lowered this to 3 stars as a result because this means poor quality control. Note that the blades are black (graphite colored) on the blue scissor.

I love this pen set. There are 48 dual tip brush pens included (see first picture). The second and third photos list the colors (same pic different angle).

The fourth photo shows my blank drawing. Photo five shows it filled in with these pens. I was amazed at how easily the color flowed out of the brush end. The fine tip end is a bit more harsh and better for outlining or detail work. Since the fine tip is so fine, it does sometimes "fuzz" the paper, so be careful. I mostly used the brush end. Only time will tell how long the colors will last before they run out of ink.

Overall, this gets 4.5 stars from me and at the current price of $29.62, this is very reasonable.

This pouch is really a ingeniously sewn zipper (2nd picture) that turns into a pouch (1st picture). I ordered this to store loose items like a spare car key, band aids, etc. in my purse. It has a lot of uses and it is colorful which is what sold me on this pouch.

[[VIDEOID:e940558b3f26177bff3f692b129e789f]] This train set gets 4.5 stars from me and is truly plug and play. All I had to do was:
- Put in 4-6 drops of liquid smoke into the locomotive’s smokestack.
- Set up the oval track. The first time I set up the track the connectors were a bit stiff and I had to use a little bit of “elbow grease.” However, after that, the setup and breakdowns were easy.
- Insert the 3AAA batteries (NOT INCLUDED) in the remote.
- Connect the locomotive drawbar connection to the tender (coal car) and then couple the passenger car at the end.
- Set the train on the tracks (a bit tricky but still pretty easy).
- Plug in the power.
- Turn on the remote and go.

INCLUDED in the SET
- The oval track contains eight curved sections, two 10” straight sections, one wall pack terminal section, and one 10” plug-expand-play-power-lock-on track section. Basically, besides the eight curved sections there are four straight sections, two of which either powers the track or provides for some other type of expansion connection.
- There are three tiny headlight replacement bulbs that are easily missed in the box. I did not even see them until I was repacking the train back into the box.
- A small bottle of smoke fluid.
- Three train cars –> the engine (locomotive), the tender (coal-car), and a passenger car.
- A manual and various other sales pamphlets.
- A remote control.
- A power cord.

BEWARE
- This set is only recommended for children 14 years or older.
- The smoke generator on the engine car SHOULD NOT be operated without smoke fluid unless the smoke switch in the back is turned off. If it is operated without SMOKE fluid, the set could be damaged.
- Three AAA batteries (NOT INCLUDED) are needed for the remote and a Phillips screwdriver is needed to open the battery compartment.

FEATURES
- The remote has an on/off switch, as well as bell, whistle, and announcement buttons. In addition, the train stops or moves forward and backward on the tracks depending on the turn of the lever.
- The train set is Bluetooth enabled for use via their Lionel app. One does not need to use the remote if using the Bluetooth app.
- The stopped train announcements differ from the moving train announcements.
- The chuffing sound and smoke are coordinated together as the train rounds the track.
- There’s a drawbar connection that connects the locomotive and tender (coal car) electronically. As a result, the cars are organized in the order of engine, tender, and passenger car.
- The locomotive listens to commands from the LionChief remote so both the locomotive and remote must be a matched pair.

NEGATIVES
- I could never figure out what the announcements were saying wen the train is going because of all the noise.
- The instructions are pretty good but lack more info about how to find the Bluetooth app, do not explain what a tender is (it is a coal-car), etc. I could have used additional information overall as this is my first train set.
- Neither my boyfriend or I could find the LionApp specified in the manual. It was not in the Apple or Play stores. We actually had to Google for the train app to find it.
- Bluetooth will not pair if MAC filtering is enabled on the router. That has to be turned off and then the MAC address of the train set found before MAC filtering can be re-enabled.

Overall this is a well-made easy-to-set-up train set that should last for many years. I attached two pictures and one video which became grainy after the Amazon upload. The first picture shows what is included in the box minus the three replacement light bulbs I found later and the second picture is after is was all set up but not yet powered up. The video shows it all in action.

These are really nice strong scissors. I dread ironing so much that I've rarely used my ironing board and have either chosen to wear my clothes wrinkled or avoided wearing those clothes altogether. I wanted take the drudgery out ironing so I took a risk (after months of consideration) and decided to spend a lot of money on this unit. I was going to purchase the [[ASIN:B00ACDXJSU Steamfast SF-623BK Mid-Size Fabric Steam Press]] but its small size is too limiting and would cause me to take a long time to iron one article of clothing. This Elna (labeled on Amazon as Janome) heat press has an ironing surface that is about 3/4 the size of a full-size ironing board and it works perfectly. There's space in the back of the unit so that the clothing can be pulled forward as it's pressed.

I absolutely love this. The unit takes about 10 minutes to heat up when on the highest (cotton) setting. Once heated, it is easy to use and my garments come out as if they were pressed by a cleaner. The DVD supplied with this provides a silent presentation (with the same music playing in the background) that demonstrates how to iron various articles of clothing, patches or crystals, and how to operate the unit. I would have preferred a talking demonstration without the music but it is sufficient as is. There's also an instruction manual, a padded square that helps with ironing tight spots like cuffs or the shoulder area, and a spray bottle. The spray bottle is used to spray down the clothes with water (especially cotton) before pressing to create the appropriate amount of steam. The unit locks in place for carrying but is a bit heavy to carry around.

Overall I am thrilled with my purchase even though I consider this very expensive. I'm looking forward to getting years of use out of it and might even use it to embellish some of my clothes with patches or crystals.

These titanium blended 6" spring assist scissors are very sharp. I was able to cut paper, cloth, felt, cardboard, and even packaging plastic easily. Since the blades are very slender, this can be used for precision work.

The provided lanyard is a nice touch so that it can be hung around the neck for easy access during use. The lanyard tip has to be looped through the hole in the handle. I feel it is imperative that the safety cap be secured on the blade before "wearing" these scissors.

Although I did not need to do this, the blade tension can be adjusted by removing the round plastic cover in the center and using a small Phillips screwdriver to make the adjustment; turn clockwise to loosen and counter-clockwise to tighten. I did find it a bit tricky to remove the cap, however, when investigating this option.

The only other disappointment I found with these clippers is that the blade locking button sticks and can only be engaged by using two hands and my nail to pull up and push forward. The button is easy to slide back with the thumb to open the scissor. However, after closing the handles and pushing the locking button forward it only partially engaged and then had to be manually lifted with my nail to lock fully so that I could affix the safety cover. This is the biggest disappointment on an otherwise handy pair of scissors. I feel Westcott should fix this feature before selling this product. I certainly wouldn't want an open pair of sharp scissors hanging around my neck.

I purchased this table because I just bought a sewing machine and needed a work area. I also do not have a lot of space in my place and needed to be able to break this down when not in use. The frame on this thing is incredible, I was able to open this up and put on the Formica-like top in less than 5 minutes and break it down in less than 3 minutes.

The feet on the table ends unscrew slightly to adjust height in case the table does not sit level to the ground. The top, although not the best top around, just hooks and locks into place underneath. The yardstick on the table top is just a piece of plastic tape but it is good enough for those who need it. My top did have a couple of "matte finished" spots that didn't match the other areas of the Formica-like gloss on the rest of the table; but it was barely noticeable so I did not take off points for this.

Once this sewing/craft (I can even see this as a computer table) is assembled, it's extremely sturdy and just big enough. I love, love, love this table. It's definitely the collapsible and sturdy frame and the simplicity the entire package that makes this a winner. I also feel the under $170 price makes this a great deal.

Nothing off putting about the scent at all.. it’s pleasant and would truly be enjoyed if only it had a strong enough throw to be noticeable when used.. Unfortunately, as this wax melts from a cube so does the aroma! This is strongest when in block form which just defeats the purpose..

From photos included you can see my other issue with these melts.. Being made of a brighter green color- really makes a mess of the actual wax dish!! It will of course clean up.. But, if used in a visible area- this mess is less than desirable to look at! I use wax melt on corner table in living room.. so, it looks yuck! Just something to keep in mind- green can look icky-messy!!

For the price point of $7.99- receiving 2 clam shells really is a solid deal. If wanting a stronger throw.. this may not be the best fit. Hoping this info can help your decision- stay safe.

What can I say, they did the trick and did it well. Over the holidays, we had paint night at our house and it was a hit!! There were eight of us total it was nice to have a paint palette for each individual to use. These are a must when painting with acrylics… They help limit the amount of paint used which as we all know, we always take too much! It’s easy to get seconds if needed- it’s just a waste to get too much.. which is what happens if you don’t use a pre-measured palette like this.
These clean out so nice with just a little soapy warm water- Boom! The acrylic comes out quite easy. After hand washing the excess paint off the plastic, I ran this through the dishwasher and they came out brand new and ready for next paint night.
They do have a little bit of bend but, that’s expected bc, these are made of plastic- easy to remedy, just use 2 hands which is a good rule of thumb anyway! And- this is just a titch of bend.. so, I be careful but, not deterred from using in any way whatsoever!!
I would definitely recommend it for the price.. at the time, it was by far best $ I found for the # offered in the package!!
Hope this helped.. feel free to let me know!

What I need and expected... these arrived on time and in helpful storage box- they were used in an “art/wine” gift basket.. so, perfect amount of paint for the project x4 people!
We included paints/brushes/canvas board/glasses/ wine and finishing spray.. these gave plenty of color choices and enough to make paint night a hit for at least x1-2 for the family that received!! I’d purchase again... they were priced decent and they have easy to use/recap bottle making them be paint ready in the future!!

UPDATE: It erases any foil design over time, buyer beware...BUT it does great on plain clay and bezels.
Original review:
One huge difference between this one and the larger size is the bottle top, I do NOT like this top at all, the cap on the 6 ounce gets in the way, the ONE ounce works great
My original review for the 1 ounce:

I have used this on polymer clay about 15 times now to test it out, with mixed results, however that was MY fault, not the products.

What works and works wonderfully: Plain polymer clay, baked. Add it to a cool piece of baked clay in direct sunlight and you have a wonderful piece of art! A few words though, premo clay will make it bead up more then say regular sculptey so you'll have to move it to the edge of the piece with a toothpick and then add a second coat or two until it is thicker and pushes itself toward the edges.

Filling a bezel: It works wonderfully. So far all I have done is polymer clay pendants and filling 2 inch pendant bezels. I place a handmade polymer clay tile, thinner in depth then the bezel, and then fill the rest with resin. You have to apply it in at least two layers though, yes it will pull away from the edges unless you apply just enough of it on the second application to "Dome" the top of the piece.

What does not work at all so far: Applying it to varnished or otherwise treated pieces. The resin turns to goo and STAYS as goo. It reacts badly to most finishes, so use this mainly on unfinished materials. Also I have killed several pieces by being distracted and not checking on them for air bubbles. Raw clays and anything with a plasticized or coated surface probably wouldn't cure either.

The price is absolutely prohibative for many, I couldn't afford to buy this every week, so I recommend you buy the small bottle and play around with it for awhile before investing in more.

I have found that if you accidentally spill it, a baby wipe is your friend! I accidentally added too much and it overflowed on my piece and work surface. I just put a baby wipe over my finger and gently wiped it away ( getting this stuff OFF once it's cured usually requires a blade).

So it's great, and easy, if you use the right materials, apply it away from UV light and then move it into the light when finished, and then watch it with a toothpick in hand for the first 2-3 minutes to guard against air bubbles. It just takes a little practice!

I love the price for the amount of mats you get, it's perfect! The mats have the perfect amount of adhesion, too. It's nice to have 4 green ones, since that's the one I use the most. I've had these for a while now, and they are still as sticky as when I bought them.

After doing a lot of looking and comparison shopping I decided on this basic but comprehensive kit. I read all the reviews, a few mentioned not receiving all the pieces so first thing I did was check. Everything was included as in the description and there were no duplicates in the stamps. I can't speak to the quality because I haven't used them but I can say everything looks well made. I haven't used anything yet but I'm excited to start. If you're looking for a beginners leather making kit I'm confident in saying that you won't be disappointed. Shipping was great, I received it the next day and packaging was great. All the sharp items were protected well. Overall I'm very happy with my purchase. I will update after I use the tools.

Bought this for a costume party. they are cute and comfortable. They are thin and narrow and have an adjustable chain to fit any neck size. These are made of a thin vinyl material. The packaging was a cardboard piece with the three choker necklaces attached. The items arrived without any damage.

I was looking for a compact and flexible tape measure [easy to wipe down] for my baking and pastry.  This is ample in length and easy to wipe clean.  Very easy to detect with a click of a button -which could be the weak point of construction - time will tell.  For the price point - this is the best one I have seen.  Takes a very small real estate space in my kitchen.

Great for making small candles with max 4inch diameter; otherwise, add 2 wicks.  These are fast burning wicks so trim them down to near surface before use.  Otherwise, once in wax, these are thin that you get a nice long burn.  Pretty tall to accommodate longer candles.

we have a little motor for the window blinds and the area is not huge.  we were seeking some STRONG tape to hold down the little motor device and even the 3M tapes were not strong enough.  hence this must be the 5th type off tapes - nope - will not hold it in place.  if you not seeking for the item to move - then this would be adequate - but just touching the sticky tape - it does not feel that strong at all.  false advertising here.

These are beautiful tapes and I am using them to decorate my files, some pencils.  Very easy to use, and fun.  I have used something similar, the pattern could fade if used on items that are handled a lot.
the calligraphy nib is closer to Chinese calligraphy brushes.  hence, not easy to wrote with and definitely more for art than office use.  that said, did love the flow of the ink.

This paper is as good as other brands that I have sued: and better value as I am a beginner and hence use a lot of the paper to practice on.  Love the quality for this price point.  Very thick and the colors do not leak through to the other side.  Holds well many of the techniques for color blending.

Perfect for many little projects that need a little adhesion: open and shut functions.  I am using it for bread recipes that I can switch up.  I have a zen area that I post different mantras; so many little organization uses.  Endless.  Very easy to remove without stickiness left on object.

A trusted brand to get a quality product, and we were not disappointed.  It has three layers, but did not feel or look bulky at all. I only wish it was an inch larger as we don’t have large faces - snug, but comfortable.  It is hand wash only, no wringing, and air dry.  Quality material and design and looking forward to wearing them as an alternative to the medical face masks.

This is a great brand that hub likes, but ordinarily for his underwear!  The quality of these face masks are as good as the boxer shorts, which is why we waited to find a trusted brand.  This has 3 layers, very thin, not bulky.  I wish it was an inch or so larger, and we don’t have large faces.  More for small heads, women faces and older kids.  You could get these extensions.  Whilst it stayed on the face, we will add one of those flexible nose wires to keep the mask in place.

One of the good paint brushes brands- the brittles keep their shape even after spraying them out when cleaning.  The bristles do not fall out.  Lovely tool for acrylic and water color painting. Love this brush.

These are not the jumbo sized sticks. More the standard stick you find in popsicles. I bought these for using in my piano practice - write the scales and different piano exercises...pick a stick a day to practice. I also use these sticks to stir my sourdough starters..quick wash and you can use them a few more times. I have a bunch of them near my stove as it acts alike a little spoon. I have more unconventional uses for them- but I love that they are so versatile.
Very well made- sturdy.

Using this for sewing and quick opening light tape/packing.  Light and easy to use.  This instrument isn’t shape so don’t over think it.  I do a lot of light sewing and mending, so good to have around as suppose to pair of scissors.

These beads I like for both the size and the pattern.  There were plenty of natural variation that I am not getting a uniformed single look.  Without breaking one of the beads, it is hard to tell if these are glass o real stones!  I will update if I end up with a spare bead to see the quality.  the feels is polished 'stone/glass'.

It is hard to assess the quality off these beads after extensive and daily wear. for the price the beads look decent and as advertised. the beads are largely glass looking and some beads have some nice pattern on it, but fewer than I like.
I plan to use them to make a bracelet for self.

LOVE. I have the larger sewing scissors .. and again, I am not disappointed with the quality of this small scissors, which fits my smaller hand a lot better. a great general use scissors for my smaller sewing projects and very light to hold as well [great for those with arthritic hands].
super sharp and hence little force require to get the job done. Could be used for general household use, but these scissors feel a little too good for that!

Great value kit to kick start candle making.  It has everything you need, andthe instructions were easy to follow.  I could beleive how easy it was to use.  One Pound of waX fills three of the 8 oz cans.  I wanted to melt some of the other candles I got as gifts that never burned down completely, so this whole kit was a handy spartan for me to recycle old candles!  You can use other oils, but make sure you stir it well before pouring into the cans, as it leaves a little streak.  Love it.  These cans look like they are totally reusuable, just need to get new wicks.  And I maDe some for gifts once I started the process.  Very very easy to use.

I purchased this because some candles' wick do not last that long and I still have a bunch of wax to burn off,  it took a whole to make this wick work with the wax as it burns way to easily, a good thing.  Great price for the amount of wick.

The Singer brand speaks for itself.  With this 'basic' model, I would rathe rig was called the CLASSIC model as it should be one model that covers most sewers needs.  I had a Brothers brand (basic) and the quality of this singer's model far excels the basic brothers model, hands down.  The solidness of the machine (reduced plastic, which is a common thing these days) meant that it did not wobble much when I am sewing at a fast pace, and the pedal is sensitive, but not overtly so that you get annoyed! It has all the basic needs from button hole, multiple range of stitch types.  I sew a lot of clothings, and to tell you the truth, if you have the technique, this machine is ample even for the professional.  I recommend buying this is this is the one and only machine you are ever going to get ..and great for beginners too.  I love it.

For a fucntional palette, this serves it purpose. Nothing more. It is not finsihed off and I caught a few splinters, so be careful with it and polish down the splintered ends before use.

The free brushes are a plus, in my books as they are a decent quality; but the knives were okay, but even as a novice, I would spend a little bit of money to buy a quality knife. It will be worth it for practicing.

price point: you get what you pay for. Nothing more, which is why I socred it high at the price point.

I am new to arcrylic painting and hence there are so many concepts to try, and it is not worth buying larger canvases as it would a waste at my level of competence! I like the Strathmore brand and it works well with most of the paint (not the expensive brands for me right now).

at ~90 cents per sheet, I am deriving a lot of joy from practicing with this product.

Wow, great price for three very functional needles, and comes with a screw top case to hold them all in.  Perfect sizes for the knitter and crochet crowd.  So happy with the quality and will last me for ever as long as I don't loose them!

For trimming fabrics this is the perfect pair of scissors: light and sharp. Definitely, for one layer of fabric only, but I was looking for thing that is not too heavy, had enough teeth to trim fabric without 'snipping'.  I have the larger gingher, but it was too big for trimming!  It comes with a leather pouch to cover the blades.

For the price this is a great pair of pinking shears.  It has good teethes that really grip into the material, even with silk chameuse. Although with light and slippery materrial it is not easy to navigate the cut and will require some patience!  Otherwise, super happy working with this pair of scissors.  In hind sight I am glad I bought this lighter pair as a heavier pair would really make it hard to use on slippery and light fabrics.

I had to turn most of the dials to maximum and I have to say it was pretty easy to get it to my size and measurements!  The range of measurements provided with the product description is accurate.  It was really easy to set up.  The body is pretty good quality where I had no problems pinning material on it.  Although the base is made of plastic, I really didnt find it unstable - quite on the contrary. It is so refreshing to hang my projects on this as I can now caste a more informed eye.  It also makes working with sleeves so much more easier too.  Whether you are a seasoned sewer or a newbie, to get good results, you really need to work with the dress form.  For this price, it is well worth it.

Wow, these new machines are super light and that was one great thing I noticed about this sewing machine. My old one was so heavy that it doubled up as a weight machine!
What I love about this machine:
1) fantastic price for all the functionalities and great tools that came with it.
2) the tools include 3 bobbins, various foot peddles, tools to clean the sewing area, thread shredder, and many many more! Believe me, I was shocked how many extra tools that came with the machine.
3) a hard cover is included and an extended arm table for many projects that uses a lot of material.
4) three speed control .. Great if you are a beginner and especially for when you need to go slow
5) a clear panel where you can see your bobbin, believe me, it is an enormous help to see when the bobbin needs refilling!
6) Great light to work with.

There is a little to fault this machine right now,it is very easy to use, great instructions. My only slight concern is the lightness of the whole machine, which could mean the components may not last as long...and I hate to create another land fill item too soon.

I am a believer that the tools are an integral part of getting the best finished product. So just like cooking, good sissors are just as important as good pans! The scissors just glide across the taffeta that I am working on: it sets my mind in a positive frame and that is all good for the sewing part!
Up until I got these scissors I was using those new fangle steel scissors, which are great to work with too; but for fabric, you cant beat the Gingher: a marriage made in 'sewing heaven'. Just so glad I bought this now. You definately get what you pay for in this product.

The online site has a lot of tips on how to take care of your scissors as well as offering a sharpening and repair service! I have not tried the latter yet, but it is good to know that there is a 'green' policy in place.

Also, the scissors came in a beautiul matted silver case - great for storing. Love it love it and the price is very competitive with other fabric stores.
The quality is great and the best price.  With prime members, it arrives so quickly that it really does save you time.  I love the fact that this is now available online.
