The pins are nice, and come packaged with little moisture absorbers that help make sure the pins stay in
a lovely condition - no rusty spots. And the company did a stellar job of packaging and mailing.
Zero problems with the brand - I just needed a shorter pin!

These came as advertised - flower head long quilting pins.

They are extremely thin pins, and the flower heads are incredibly thin plastic.
I've loaded my pincushion with them, but have not had the chance to do a project
using these pins. I can see them being helpful in going through several layers,
as long as they don't bend.

I probably would not reorder these,but they are just what you see.

Add me to the long list of customers who LOVE this pincushion!

It is indeed large, but it was just right to hold long quilting pins!
There is nothing that I do not like about this pincushion.
The material feels great, similar to a fleece. It feels good in your hand,
and sits nicely on whatever surface you put it on. The beaded decoration is cute as well.
The bright pink is a cheerful addition to my work area.
The filling appears to be tightly packed polyfil - not sand or pellets.
I would not have thought that would work, but I have no problems with pins not
going in easily.

If I could ask the manufacturer for anything, it would be for MORE COLORS!
I'd love to have this exact same cushion in other colors to keep types of pins separate.

If you are a crafter and use a lot of pins, this is the way to buy them!

The only difference I saw between these pins and the ones you could pick up at any discount store,
was the heads of the pins. These are slightly rounded/peaked compared to basic straight pins.
There were a few bad pins in the box, but that is to be expected at this quantity!

I had no idea how many pins to expect, but most sources say about 5000 #17 pins per pound,
which would make these about 2500.
I personally had close to 3,000 pins in the box (I do craft projects that take 300 pins each, and got ten projects out of the box).

Overall, very pleased with the pins and have already reordered them!

Save yourself some money. I was so disappointed in this purchase!
I thought I was buying a cone of thread. Not from the seller I had...
I got a tiny spool smaller than my pinky finger.

Fine if I was still doing counted cross stitch, but useless for quilting.
And yes, I feel a little ripped off, even though no one twisted my arm to buy it.

I don't think you can go wrong with this pack.
I am a recent convert to Jacquard paints, and will be a life-long fan.

When I read about the Halo line, I wanted to try them all! However, my personal
economic situation just won't allow that - I can't work outside the home anymore, so
I don't have the "mad money" I used to. This is a great way to try out some colors.
Now I know a little more about exactly what they look like, and how they will work for
my projects. I'll be able to pick up a better variety of their paints now! One bottle
at a time, I plan on owning every single color they make!

I have not tried these paints on other surfaces yet - I bought them to use on fabric,
and they do a spectacular job on them. Note that you might need two coats, depending on
what sort of end result you want to have. However, I have had ZERO problems with them.

Jacquard - you have a life-long fan here. I've used just about every brand, but
you have won me over completely! Wish I had known about you sooner!
Thank you for making a really great product.

I have used fabric paint of one kind or another for most of my life.
I've done painted t-shirts for many years, but usually used the squeeze bottle paints
for outlines and use acrylic paints with fabric medium for the rest of the design.

I recently started pursuing my hidden love of art quilting, and needed a way to be able to
paint on the quilt without having to heat set it, and not have it harden on the fabric.
This paint is PERFECT for this purpose!
(I had used another brand of paint pen on part of my quilt, then when I ironed on the
appliqué, the color completely changed, becoming extremely dark from the iron heat.
I was NOT a happy camper, and could not remove the appliqué because of the fused web on the back.)

The paint is so easy to use! It blends well, covers well, can be thinned with water for
a light touch, and basically is everything I need it to be.

I have begun getting a couple colors a month, to increase my color collection.
I can definitely say that I want this paint in every color that they make! I'll never be without it again.
It will do nothing but expand my creativity on my quilts, allowing me to be more expressive and add
detail I could never get from the fabric alone.

I have small cutting mats, and they have worked great for my smaller projects.
As I have decided to jump into the world of art quilting, I needed a bigger mat.

There are no places where I live (The Boonies) which sell sewing supplies beyond a basic pack of
needle and cheap 50-cent threads, so I went to my favorite place to shop (Amazon) to look for a decent, yet
affordable mat.

There were several mats that had the dimensions I needed (one direction had to be at least 24"), but my
budget was very limited this time. So, I was looking for decent without breaking the bank.

I have to say that I was very disappointed indeed.
I have cut three binding strips that were two feet long each, so six feet of fabric,
and trimmed the edges of my art quilt. That's all. I have four or five black lines
(my fabric was black cotton) on the mat permanently now!

I cannot brush them off, and they did not wash off. I had a new blade, so it wasn't an old
blade digging a rut. And a self-healing mat should be self-healing anyway!!
Not that it should matter, but my cutter is also a Fiskars: [[ASIN:B000B7M8WU Fiskars 9521 45mm Contour Rotary Cutter]].

I am really disappointed by this purchase. I needed one desperately, and it kept me from cutting
the table, so I won't be sending it back. But good grief - permanently "scarred" after ONE project that
wasn't even a lot of cutting?? I'll post a few photos. I see other reviews don't mention this. I wonder
if I got a bad mat?
Also, mine came shipped relatively flat in a large box, so I guess Amazon has changed the shipping methods
and no longer rolls the mats. For storage, I just put up a small nail at an angle against the wall, both
to save space and to keep the mat flat.

For those who care about such things, this board also contains the state of California warning about being
made with stuff known to cause cancer. Thankfully, I don't plan to eat meals off this mat, so hopefully
just touching it will not transmit anything horrible. Just to be safe, I washed my hands after using it.

Meanwhile, with a name like Fiskars, I thought this would lead the pack, not trail sadly behind.
For shame, Fiskars. For shame!

Well fine!
Now I will have to get more of this brand of paint!!

I have used fabric paint for decades, but most of them have a very stiff finish.
I was looking for something to embellish a quilt, so wanted something that would not be
crunchy hard or extra shiny. This is it!

I absolutely love the way it looks on the quilt. For example, I have branches that are
made to look like birch trees. I wanted them lighter, but could only find gray and black fabric that
had the branches replicated. I used this paint, slightly watered down, and it did a fabulous job!
It made the lighter branches I was looking for, without covering up the trunk markings.

I also used it full strength for various highlights. I tried applying it with a paint brush - great;
a q-tip - great, but watch for fiber drag and finally with fingertip - great!

Note that this is NOT a completely opaque paint. You WILL be able to see the surface you started with
underneath it. If you want to do several paint/dry, paint/dry layers, you might get a more solid appearance, but
this paint is made to be semi-translucent (see-through).

I also love that this is made to mix with other colors and lighten them.

Thank you, Jacquard, for making a fabulous product!

My daughter loves to sketch with pencil, and loves using this model sketch book.
We've been buying the 54404 for years, usually from our local WalMart store.
We've had a hard time finding them there any more, so turned to online to get what we needed.

The only real complaint that she or I had was that #54404 USED to come with MEDIUM weight paper.
It was great for what she used it for. (We have several full sketch books with the same cover, same
number and MEDIUM paper weight.

When we got this one (and it arrived quickly and packed well), it was HEAVY paper.
I don't think that is a real crisis, but it isn't exactly what she wanted. Not sure when Academie
made the paper weight changeover, since we have so many of the medium-weight 54404's.

Note that this paper WILL eventually rub off on the back of the previous page unless you use
spray fixative on your sketches. However, my daughter loves the features in this sketch pad - she likes
the stiff back that makes it easy to support while sketching, loves the spiral binding on the left
(she is right-handed), and also likes having the extra pocket for notes or sheets of other papers.

All in all, a good bargain, but be aware that the paper weight in the book has changed over time.
Great for keeping kids and adults busy
The drying time is great and it's very bright
Great product. Took a minute to get it but was awesome
My daughter absolutely loves these. She's working from water colors to something a bit more vibrant and this was a great beginner set.
You get what you pay for, I did not want to spend much and therefore the first time I used it it broke, I say spend a bit more and get a quality one that will last for years to come.
Great heavy body acrylic to mix to lay down a base coat you can’t go wrong.
These panels really need some review by their company, they rolled up so badly all of the paint ran to the center.
Very pleased with this fabric, heavy enough not to see through but not to heavy to look cheap. Lovely. Seller is amazing to work with also. Will buy here again!

Arteza is a brand I've come to trust over the past few years after purchasing a number of their art supplies, so when I was looking for a case to hold my 3 sets of Sharpie Ultra-Fine pens (24 in each set, 72 total), I didn't hesitate to order this case.

It's the perfect fit -- all pens fit snugly into the elastic loops and the entire case is sturdy and well-made. No complaints at all -- very happy with this. Solid value, well-made -- perfect!

I'm on a "washi eggs" kick -- I enjoy making them, and I recently moved into a new apartment where my color palette is navy blue and shades of gray. So I searched Amazon's listings to see if I could find washi papers in a variety of patterns that would work with my decor.

This is one of the sets I purchased -- this one from Amazon's Japan location. I was amazed that it arrived faster than many of my Amazon US orders (even with Prime!). The papers arrived in perfect shape, and I can get at least 2 eggs from each sheet.

This is high-quality washi, too -- beautifully made and printed. Would happily purchase it again if I need it. Highly recommended.

I'm using these to make washi eggs: eggs covered with Yuzen Chiyogami paper and then covered with a transparent sealer, similar to decoupage but the paper's pattern is retained by careful cutting. Sure, I could use "real" eggs (blowing out the innards), but these are much easier definitely more durable.

The flat bottom helps in the sealing process, making it much easier to do the multiple coats necessary to get the desired effect. Will purchase more when needed.

Love the design and color of this yarn bowl.

As with most yarn bowls, it's not huge. If you usually work from center-pull skeins or larger balls, you'll want to rewind your yarn into smaller balls in order to take advantage of the smaller size of this bowl. (I use a swift and ball winder -- just takes a few more minutes to prepare the yarn balls).

The workmanship is fine -- no rough edges to snag. I've used it so far with some bulkier (size 5) scarf yarn and also for some finer (size 2) sock yarn with no problems at all. It has a nice weight, too, so it doesn't slide around. I just set it down next to my chair and pull the yarn from there.

Would make a nice gift for any knitter or crocheter.

I purchased this washi tape set to use on my hand-crafted greeting cards.

This is a particularly nice set -- beautiful patterns, great colors, and you get a variety of sizes. I use these with solid metallic tape and occasionally some glitter tape. Results are always lovely.

Will purchase again when needed.

I do a lot of diamond painting of different sorts: full drill, partial drill, and special shaped. The special shaped ones have been mostly bookmarks, notebook covers, and "keychains" (which I use as Christmas tree ornaments). This was my first experience with a "picture" special shapes project.

Easy to do -- it comes with everything you need to complete the picture, although I did use a different "jewel picker" for the larger shaped stones. No issues, really. The colors are perfect for my decor: blue (mostly navy), with hints of yellow and orange. Very happy with the result.

I purchased this to use with some nice patterned washi that I used for trimming hand-crafted greeting cards. It's not like most washi -- I think the metallic component is mylar or something similar (it's not paper, like most washi), so you'll have to use scissors to cut it. But that's a minor issue for me

The assortment of colors is nice, too. I can use the copper with my fall-themed cards, and the fold and silver for my holiday cards.

Would purchase again.

I've used this for placing small gems on my hand-crafted greeting cards, but I'm now finding that it works great for picking up the "special shape" sparklers in partiall-drill diamond painting projects like bookmarks, notebooks, and special holiday designs.

While a regular diamond painting pen will work just fine for the smaller drills, you need something bigger to handle the larger pear, marquis, and "pearl" gems. I've used both ends of this depending on the size of the gem to be placed. Works great -- picks them up securely, and I can place them exactly where I want them.

Can't argue with the quality, either, nor the value or money Highly recommended!

Love these! I'm using them mostly for making handcrafted greeting cards, using washi tapes of various widths and colors. These fit right in -- the narrow width is perfect.

You get about 6.5 feet of tape per roll -- so not a huge amount, but enough for my use. I got the "A" version of this-- it has some neutral colors (white, gray, etc.) that work great for some of my designs.

May end up purchasing the other version ("B") as it has more of the brighter colors, too.

Overall, quite happy with these.

Some of these are more "pearl" than "metallic," but that's OK - they're all nice quality and the assortment of colors is super.

I'm using these mostly to put together some handmade greeting cards, using various widths of washi tape to create designs and patterns. So I needed some solid colors to offset the patterned washi I love so much.

These are good quality, adhesion is OK, and there are enough different colors to match with my entire washi collection.

Will purchase again when needed. Good value, too!

I've used other Ohuhu art products in the past (colored pencils, gel pens, etc.) and always found them to be good quality (and their customer service is great, too).

I'm happy to report that these pens continue that tradition. Good colors, nice presentation packaging, seem to last a relatively long time, too. I've used them for fine detail coloring (think Johanna Basford) as well as Querkles. The dual tips are perfect for both fine details as well as filling in larger sections.

No complaints at all -- very satisfied with these.

This seems to work reasonably well to hold my "custom" (larger) diamond painting drill pens.  It would work a little better if it were maybe 1/2" taller, as some of the heavier ones lean.  But it does hold them somewhat securely and keeps them handy.  I use it primarily for my multiplacers -- I keep a single placer tip on one end, then a multiplayer on the other with the single placer down.  I usually have a 3-, 4-, and 6-placer on hand for various color blocking needs.  Works nicely for that.

To be honest, I didn't expect this kit to be as nice as it is. I've purchased "budget" diamond painting kits before with very mixed results. This one is much nicer, more comparable to some of the higher-priced "designer" kits.

I'm still somewhat of a beginner with this hobby, but I've been learning as I go. So let's look at the basics of this kit:

1) Canvas -- a better quality of canvas material than I've seen in other comparably-priced kits. It was easy to "flatten" and it's reasonably flexible. It arrived with no serious creasing.
2) Glue -- this kit uses poured glue rather than double-sided tape, so there are no bubbles, rivers, puckers, etc. It's sufficiently sticky with no "weak" spots so far (I'm about 2/3 finished -- see photo).
3) Drills -- round drills, very nice quality. Very little "trash" -- a few random bits, almost no tags or bubbles. I think I have maybe a dozen or so trash drills out of the hundreds I've placed so far, and most of those were from one specific color. I did find one "hitchhiker": a random square tomato red drill was in with one of the light-taupe color packages.
4) Tool kit -- the standard green tray, pink pen, red wax.
5) Printing/design -- very clear printing, no "blurry" sections at all. Care was taken to arrange color/letter assignments so that there's little chance for confusion. For example, C and G are completely different colors and aren't close to each other in the design field; the same with E and B. It makes placement easy and clear.

I'm very happy with how this is going -- I was going to wait until I was finished to post a review, but it's such nice quality that I wanted to get the review up while it is still a work-in-progress.

Definitely recommend -- worth every penny.

Who doesn't need a supply of safety pins? Different sizes come in handy for different needs, and this assortment includes all the best sizes. They're organized in a compartmentalized case although the lid doesn't fit as securely as it should so if you drop or shake the box, some of the safety pins will shift from compartment to compartment. A minor inconvenience, sure, but it needs mentioning.

Very happy with these -- I just keep them in the bathroom drawer.

I'm a "poly-crafter" -- I enjoy a number of crafty pastimes including thread crochet, paper crafts (especially card making), zentangle, paint-by-number, counted cross stitch, coloring, and now diamond painting. As I've gotten older, I've developed fairly severe osteoarthritis in my hands which makes holding a pencil/pen difficult. Having a cushion makes it possible for me to continue the pastimes I so enjoy.

These foam grips do the job nicely. I use them both on my colored pencils as well as my drill pens. The cost is quite reasonable considering you get 100 of them.

Very happy with these -- would recommend them highly.

I purchased this set to use on some zentangle tiles that I intend to shade/color and frame for my bathroom. There's a great range of tip sizes from super fine to more broad, which works well for my style of doodling.

It's a large set and well worth the price. Will happily repurchase if needed. No problems or issues at all.

I've been crocheting for over 60 years and have several sets of hooks in my collection, but I was away from home for what ended up being an unexpected extended period and hadn't brought any hooks with me. Amazon to the rescue! Looked at a lot of sets, and decided on this one.

I'm glad I did.

The set is good quality, and the sizing appears to be spot on. It's complete (B-J), and includes a lot of useful extras (yarn needles, tape measure, stitch markers, etc.). The handles are similar to another set I own and work well for me (I have osteoarthritis in my hands). They have some "give" but are still sturdy enough to control.

The case is also nicely made -- sturdy with a quality smooth zipper. This is the set I'll take with me on future trips as it will pack easily. Nice investment -- highly recommended.

A relatively easy crafty activity. Not much in the way of instructions as to which tool to use for what purpose, but trial and error works to find what works for you. The results are nice looking and frame-worthy.

I’m a senior and found it necessary to use my “high powered” reading glasses for this so keep that in mind. Overall, a good purchase and I would recommend it for crafty friends and older kids (10+).

I was very pleasantly surprised at how nice these are. I purchased them to us as an embellishment on some handcrafted greeting cards I make for a women's organization I belong to that has as one of its symbols the daisy (marguerite). The petals are slightly sheer -- very delicate looking -- and the two sizes are perfect for my use. I use "glue dots" to affix them to my cards -- no issues there at all.

Will absolutely purchase again when I need more. These are that nice. Solid 5 stars!

I purchased this specifically to use with handcrafted greeting cards. Some of my designs need a bit of ribbon to add just the right touch, and since I tend to use black and white as a "foundation" color scheme (accented with jewel tones), finding a decent black and white ribbon was key.

The width is perfect (3/8") for making small bows or single knots to add a bit of embellishment. The quality is fine -- just right for my purpose. Not too thin, not too thick. And you get a lot for the money.

I also purchased the navy-and-white version of this, too, as i also use a lot of navy in my card designs. Very happy with this.

I like to use vellum to create focal embellishments -- things like layered butterflies, flowers, etc. I usually use some printed card stock and then add a couple of layers of this sheer vellum on top, folding/curving the wings or petals to create a 3D image. This product is excellent for my use. I use punches to cut out the flowers or butterflies and there are no issues at all -- it cuts cleanly without the light/white "outline" I've gotten with some vellums. And you get a great assortment of colors in this set.

Very happy with this -- will order again when I need to replenish my stash.

I got this set to take on a World Cruise. There was an art instructor on board who offered to teach watercolor classes during the 131-day trip, so I purchased this set and some watercolor paper pads to bring along. Unfortunately, the trip was cut short in Australia after only 53 days due to the pandemic, but we managed to have about 10 lessons during that time.

The art instructor was impressed with this set -- the paints are good quality and provide enough variety for a beginner who hasn't learned to mix colors just yet. The water brushes were nice, too -- although I also used some "regular" brushes as well.

Very happy with this set, and the fact that the art teacher "approved" was a bonus.

I purchased this to use in some hand-crafted greeting cards.  The size (6" x 8") works so that I can get at least two card fronts from each page, plus there are some nice coordinating patterns.  I paired it with some black heavy cardstock, black crystals, and a bit of vellum.  Very happy with it -- it's a tad pricey, but the designs are very nice.

I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards and I'm always looking for good quality cardstock to use for matting focals and layers. I hadn't used this brand before but decided to give it a shot.

Very happy I did. The color is perfect -- I use a lot of navy (I just seem to gravitate toward that color), and this particular shade seems to work well with almost every designer paper I have in my stash (and I have a lot of different brands ... ). It's fairly heavyweight -- 85lb/215 gsm cover -- so it's substantial and easy to use for mattes or card bases. It scores nicely (I use a bone folder to score my card bases) and I can get a good sharp fold without cracking.

Very highly recommended. Great color and economically smart, too. Highly recommended.

I learned early on in my card making hobby that if you want to get a sharp crease on a folded card, you need to use a bone folder/paper creaser.  It seems like such a little thing, but it really does make a big difference.  A proper bone folder will not leave any marks on the paper/cardstock but with a bit of pressure will provide a sharp crease.  I usually score my card blanks and store them flat; then use the folder to set the crease as I'm making the card.  Works great!  This is a good one and a great size for card makers.

I'm on my second package of these papers -- the first I purchased several years ago, so I was interested to see if they had changed the quality or assortment.

I'm very happy to say that they're just as beautiful as my first package. I use these when I make hand-crafted greeting cards as accents to coordinate with other papers. The assortment of styles and colors is wonderful. There are lots of pages with metallic accents, embossed textures, and a full range of colors. Some of the papers are almost translucent while others are heavier -- more like a light cardstock.

They are pricey -- that hasn't changed, either -- but you get 100 six-by-six papers that are really unusual in terms of colors and designs. I'll get dozens of cards from this pack, so it was worth the investment for me.

For my purposes, they warrant five stars.

I make handcrafted greeting cards and like to use flat-back adhesive pearls or rhinestones to add a bit of "bling" or sparkle to my cards. This set is amazing -- so many different colors, each sheet containing 3 different sizes (tiny, medium, and "large"). I use reverse-action tweezers to remove them from the sheet and place on my card. Occasionally the adhesive doesn't stick as strongly as I would like, so I use a tiny drop of clear adhesive. No problem!

Quite happy with these. Great colors, decent quality, very useful.

I make handcrafted greeting cards and use small bows like this to provide a colorful accent on some of my cards.  I've been tying my own for years but I thought I'd try purchasing some premade ones.  I ordered these in red and another package in navy.  Very happy with the quality and size.  I haven't found any yet with construction issues (I've used about 15 or so between the two packages), and I really like the way they look on my cards.  I attach them with a micro glue dot and it works perfectly.  No problems at all -- will purchase again when needed.  Saves me a LOT of time!

I purchased these to use on handcrafted greeting cards, and I'm quite pleased with the quality and color assortment.  I haven't seen any issues at all -- they're nicely made from decent quality ribbon and the perfect size (small!) for use on 4.25" x 5.5" cards.  I've been using them on a set of cards with printed designer paper balloons of different sizes with hand-drawn "strings" gathered together with one of these bows in a color to coordinate with the papers, attached at the base of the gathered strings.  I would consider ordering more of these when needed.

I make mostly 4.25" x 5.5" cards and these smaller sentiment stickers work great for placement on a small banner for my cards.  The sentiment lends itself to a variety of situations:  friendship, condolences, get-well, etc.  The company also makes this same sticker set in gold and silver, and I those along with these black ones for most of my "general purpose" cards.  They're not too tricky to manipulate -- some stickers tend to fold back on themselves making placement difficult.  I'll continue to purchase and use these as long as I can find them online.  Very useful.

I travel quite a lot -- mostly cruise travel -- and I got this to use on a 131-day world cruise. I love the compact size, but it has so much useful stuff! I've used it already to reattach an errant button, and I've used the safety pins to attach a hat to my cross-body bag to make it easier to get on/off the tenders (you really need to have both hands free). You get thread in so many colors that there shouldn't be any problems finding one that works with whatever needs mending.

This would be a perfect gift for someone who travels a lot. It doesn't take up much packing space, and provides so much stuff! Definitely a 5-star item.

Wasn't sure what to expect, but these are definitely comparable to pricier brand-name hooks. The design will work for a lot of us with arthritis -- some hooks are just impossible for me to use, but these seem to be a lot more comfortable.

Quality is fine, no obvious design or manufacturing flaws. Good quality for a reasonable cost.

I'm actually quite happy with these. No, they're not "artist quality," nor do they claim to be. They're perfectly fine general use pens. The ink is water-based (says so right in the description), so they'll bleed/run when used with watercolors or other water-based pens. But water-based also means minimal bleed through in general use, much less than the waterproof alcohol-based markers/pens I sometimes use for coloring.

My only "quibble" is that they're rather small in diameter. If I have a lot to write, I have to take a break once in awhile or my hand cramps up. Otherwise, they're perfectly suited to journaling or general use, and you get a lot of them for a reasonable price (currently less than ten dollars).

So bottom line: I like them. Four stars.

As I've gotten older, I've developed a need to label things as I (ahem!) tend to lose track of things all to easily these days. I'm also a paper crafter, so I've been labeling my supply storage "cubbies" for years. All this adds up to a need to produce labels that are striking, easy to read and not too pricey.

I've owned a couple of similar label makers over the years -- they all work pretty much the same way: each uses a proprietary "cartridge" that contains the background plus ink strip. Load the cartridge, design your label using the onboard keyboard and small screen, then "print" and "cut." Voilà -- an adhesive label ready to apply where needed.

This particular model offers a range of options that definitely enhance usability and possibilities. You can print on the usual label material using the TZe cartridges. But you can also use special TZe cartridges containing actual fabric ribbon -- this is new. So, creating custom ribbon ties for party favors embellished in gold with the date and honoree is a piece of cake.

The P-Touch Embellish comes with the basic unit and one cartridge of white ribbon. You'll need to supply 6 AAA batteries (or the external power cord, available at an additional cost) and any other type of label material you need for your project. I purchased a "black ink on white background" cartridge.

Really, this is so simple to use -- essentially the same as previous makes/models: pop in the cartridge, turn it on, design your label using the fonts, frames, and graphics included (the instruction manual will give you the details on how to summon these up and apply them to your design), and then print and cut the label.

My next project for this gizmo: creating labels for an over-the-door multi-pocket shoe storage unit that I'll be using to store toiletries, OTC meds, and other necessaries on an upcoming world cruise. I'll be on board a ship for 4.5 months, and I do much better if I have a specific spot for each "thing," so labeling is a good way to encourage that.

Overall, I like this a lot and intend to use it to help keep things organized. I'd like it better if it came with an additional cartridge for labels (in addition to the ribbon cartridge) and I'd LOVE it if they could include the power cord. Solid 4 stars.

I purchased these to take on a cruise for use by a group of us from an online forum to be able to recognize each other in the early hours of the cruise. I got precisely what was advertised, none arrived broken, and they went into the suitcase for the trip. Handed them out at an early "meet & greet" at sail away and they were well received and worn throughout the first week or so of the 28-day cruise.

No quality issues at all -- I don't think they'd withstand a lot of tugging/pulling, but for our use, they were just fine. 4 stars -- I like them!

I do a lot of paper crafts, some of which require fine detail cutting. These work nicely for most of my projects -- "fussy cutting" layered images ("3-D decoupage") for use on cards and in scrapbooks. The points are sharp and the blades themselves are thin enough not to interfere with the acute angles and curves I sometimes encounter.

I received the light blue version - the handle is comfortable and fits my fairly-large hands. They're well-balanced, too, so I don't get tired after a couple of hours of use. They come with a protective cover, too. Nice touch, that.

Definitely higher quality than I expected. Size is good for portability. Four stars, for sure.

Nice set -- you get 36 crayons and 36 pencils, all "twistable," all with scents. The back of the box shows you what scents are included. In the set I got, the scents are subtle -- certainly not overwhelming, stronger in the crayons than the pencils (naturally). These are the same Crayola crayons we all know and love, but in the twistable format, so no "points" or sharpening. Colors are vibrant in both crayons and pencils.

Overall, I liked these, and they would make a great gift for a budding artist.

Excellent quality printing on heavy card stock weight paper, double-sided.  This particular set includes designs that feature "panels" and strips that can be cut apart and used individually as borders, focals, etc., perfect for card makers but also useful for scrapbookers.  The colors are very nice with a lovely vintage feel.  I originally purchased three different sets of this brand -- I'm getting ready to order more!

I'm finally at a stage in my life where I have both the time and the means to travel. Due to some health issues, cruise travel seems to work best for me to visit the most places on my bucket list with the least amount of day-to-day fuss. You get on the ship, unpack, and for the next however many days, you sail around going from port to port, getting off the ship to see the sights, and then back onboard for meals and to sleep. And there are "sea days" where there are no stops, so you can relax, take a class, read, nap, whatever. So relaxing!

And of course, I want to document my travels. I've never done scrapbooking before, so I'm a complete novice. I have an idea of what I want to commemorate, and it's just a matter of figuring out how to arrange it all. I've purchased several books on travel scrapbooking, and this is by far the best of the bunch.

You get 30 two-page layouts ranging from 3-11 photos per spread. All sizes are give based on a 12" x 12" scrapbook. Mine is smaller -- 8" x 8" -- but reducing the size is easy.

I now have what I need to document my first ever cruise. I took a 14-day Alaska cruise, round trip out of Seattle, and took nearly 200 photos. I also recorded daily impressions and thoughts, so I think I'm ready to get everything printed and assembled. And now I know what to do!

When they say "mirror," they mean it! This is gorgeous cardstock with a super reflective smooth surface and a good copper color -- not too "orange," not too "dull" -- more like a brand new copper penny.

I used this for some fall and Thanksgiving cards this year along with my fall-themed designer paper, and it was perfect. It works nicely for die cutting (I use a Sizzix Big Shot), and you get quite a lot for the money. Will absolutely purchase again for future projects! Good quality, easy to work with -- love it!

I used a LOT of this for my handcrafted holiday greeting cards this year.  When they say "mirror," they MEAN mirror!  It's super shiny and the cardstock is heavy but still easy to cut in my Sizzix Big Shot.  It added just the perfect amount of bling/sparkle to my cards.  Would purchase again when needed.  No issues or complaints with the quality at all.  Very nice!

I make handcrafted greeting cards so I'm always looking for new embellishments that will add a bit of sparkle. I also just started cruising - my first ever cruise to Alaska was magical, and I'm hooked. I like to give a cash bonus to stewards and other staff who have performed above and beyond to make my experience even better. So I make "thank you" cards to use for this. And that's where these nautical charms come into play.

They're perfect for my purpose. They're relatively flat, too, so they don't add too much to the thickness of the card in the envelope. You get both silver-tone and gold-tone, so I can use them with a variety of papers and cardstock. High quality, too -- I haven't found any flaws in the 8-10 I've used so far.

I would absolutely purchase these ageain. Really nice quality.

What a fun case! Wasn't sure what to expect, but I'm pleasantly surprised by the quality and "look" of this. It's very similar to a couple of cases I purchased recently designed to hold electronics, but without the extra interior mesh pockets. Although intended as a pencil case, this would work for anything, really. I'm using it for my crochet hooks, accessories, and the reading glasses I use for my fine thread crochet. It's the perfect size hold lots of "stuff" and it fits in my needlework bag very nicely.

I see no quality issues at all. Everything is securely sewn, the zipper is good quality, and the exterior design is sharply printed. The manufacturer says this can be washed in the washing machine on a gentle cycle and low temp -- I haven't tried this, but it's nice to know in case I ever need to wash it. The interior is lined in bright red soft fabric, so the case would work nicely for electronics, too. And it carries a lifetime warranty.

Given the size and quality, I give this one a solid 5 stars: I love it!

I actually got this to use in making hand-crafted greeting cards. The designs are so unique and colorful -- and the scale is perfect for use on cards. The pattern appears on one side; the opposite side is a coordinating solid color (not white), so that makes folded embellishments much more interesting (I use tea-bag folding techniques sometimes -- this paper is PERFECT for that!).

The quality is good -- it's a thinner paper than many card-makers might be used to since it has to be "foldable" for origami -- but it holds up nicely for use as a background pattern for a sentiment banner or a matted focal. I really like that so many of the designs are coordinated (there's a series of designs that feature a sort of nautical/navy blue that coordinate together nicely).

I'll be getting a lot of good use from this pack -- solid 5 stars from me. I love it!

UPDATE: June 12, 2020 -- I just purchased my second pack of this, and I'm as thrilled with it now as I was three years ago. Very nice quality, a lot of "coordinating" patterns/colors, great for card makers who want something a little different. Still warrants 5 stars.

I got this primarily for the range of colors and the interesting patterns. I wanted something that was more interesting than solid-color card stock, but not so specific as most printed designs. I'm quite happy with this.

The colors of the pages in the pad I received are very close to the colors shown in the product photo -- maybe just slightly less contrast (the brightest yellow color is slightly less bright -- not so "neon"). I gather from some of the other reviews that this was not the case -- so that could indicate a printing issue at some point in the production of these pages. All I know is that mine are very close to the photo, and will work nicely for my use.

I make hand-crafted greeting cards, although I might be using some of this for a scrapbook to commemorate an Alaska cruise later this year (especially the blue/green pages). The mottled pattern will work well as both background and mat for sentiment banners as well as focal images. And the quality is good -- it's a nice sturdy card stock (not paper).

I would purchase this again in a heartbeat -- I really do like it!

Ultra fine point, nice quality, no skipping, good colors. These are great for coloring highly detailed coloring pages. They're water-based, so not as likely to bleed through. So far, I've had NO bleed through at all on higher-quality coloring pages (Basford, etc.).

I've used these so far on 3 average-sized coloring pages and they're still going strong, so it looks like they hold a good amount of ink. No complaints at all. This is an economical option for fine-point water-based coloring pens. I love them!

Love this stamp and die set. It works for a number of "gratitude" situations (thanks for kindness, thanks for a gift, general appreciation, etc.).

I usually use the die to cut the "thanks" out of foil or glitter card stock, mount it "raised" with foam tape, and then stamp the rest of the sentiment. I also use the "thanks" stamp alone, stamped in Versamark, and then heat embossed using various colors of embossing powder to go with my card's color palette. The stamps are excellent quality (what I've come to expect from Hero Arts), and the die cuts beautifully in my Sizzix Big Shot die cutting machine.

I've already used this set to make several "thank you" cards for giving to staff on an upcoming cruise, and will be using it a lot in the future, too, as I make cards for the "courtesy committee" of a women's organization I belong to. I love being able to tailor my cards with various papers and card stock and this set will make coming up with the "thank you" cards a piece of cake. Solid 5 stars -- I love it!

I will be taking my first ever cruise vacation in a couple of months to Alaska, and to say I'm excited would be an understatement. ** TWO ** bucket list items in one: 1) take a cruise and 2) visit Alaska. I can't wait!

One thing I've learned is that while gratuities are paid in advance and included in most fare quotes, it's customary to offer a little extra at the end of a cruise to the steward and other ship staff members if they've done a particularly good job. I'm an avid card maker, so I'm making some "thank you" cards ahead of time using nautical-/cruise-themed papers.

And that's where this stamp and die set come into play.

So far, I've used the anchor, ship's wheel, and compass rose with quality card stock to accent a few cards. I stamp the image in Versamark and heat emboss (silver for the anchor, gold for the compass rose, and brown textured for the ship's wheel); then use the dies to cut the image with my Sizzix Big Shot. The dies work beautifully -- it's a clean cut, no gaps. The stamps produce a clear image with no fuzziness. All good quality.

I'll also be using them when I put together my scrapbook after the trip to accent and illustrate my journal pages. And since I'm planning on taking more cruises in the future, these will get a lot of use. Solid 5 stars from me.

I will be taking my first ever cruise vacation in a couple of months to Alaska, and to say I'm excited would be an understatement. ** TWO ** bucket list items in one: 1) take a cruise and 2) visit Alaska. I can't wait!

Which is why I've started putting together a small scrapbook to commemorate the occasion. I'm using a 7" x 9" wire-bound journal that already has lined pages, and I'm adding scrapbook papers of various designs fitting with my "cruise" theme along with embellishments, twine/ribbon, space for matted photos, envelopes to hold souvenirs like menus and daily activity sheets, and spaces to journal while on the trip.

I'm using this stamp set to caption some of the pages using black or dark brown ink as well as heat embossing in copper and gold. These are really nice stamps -- nice and crisp, typical clear stamps so they're easy to clean. They cover a variety of situations and occasions -- the cruise line I'm taking is famous for its excellent cuisine, so I know I'll be taking photos of some of the creations, especially on Gala Nights. My plan is to "pre-stamp" some pages, but wait until I get home and print off my photos to arrange and add the text to highlight the moment.

I realize that these are intended to be used for a planner (pre-trip) -- but they absolutely work for a post-trip scrapbook. Really happy with these!

Surprisingly good quality! I've gone through a couple of other sets of gel pens and was just starting to look around for yet another new set and saw these on a gold box deal -- jumped on it.

I'm NOT disappointed! Au contraire, these are really decent gel pens and the number of colors/finishes (metallic, glitter, etc.) is impressive. I use these mostly for either detailed coloring books OR for coloring stamped images on hand-crafted greeting cards. The tips are fine enough that I can manage fairly tiny detailed sections in the images without going outside the lines. Gel pens have a tendency to use a lot of ink fairly quickly, so being able to get a set that doesn't break the bank is a real plus -- I won't feel so annoyed if I have to replace them in less than a year.

Really don't have any complaints with these -- I would absolutely purchase these again and can recommend them highly.

I've been making handcrafted greeting cards for about 10 years, and I've used a lot of different stamp blocks with my cling rubber and clear stamps over the years. If you're like me, you want something that allows you to have a secure grip or hold so that you can position the stamp carefully, but also allows for an easy clean "lift" so that you don't get smudges. Some of the thick acrylic blocks are just too big and hard to handle for the way I use stamps (I use lots of layered stamp sets). As a result, I tend to go for "thinner" blocks but they also have problems sometimes being harder to "pick up" cleanly.

Enter these new Fiskars blocks. These are of the "thin" variety, with a couple of nice features

You get four clear blocks: 1" x 1", 2" x 2", 3" x 3" and about 3.5" x 6". All feature grid lines etched into the plastic and side "handles" that curve upward. The three smaller blocks also have "softgrip" silicone-like inserts in the side handles that provide a non-slip grip. VERY nice touch, that. This makes it much easier to maintain a secure grip on the block and makes removing it cleanly much easier. The thinner profile also helps with precise placement for layered stamps. The three smaller sizes are great, too, for the types of stamps I use.

The larger block has the curved "handles" but no softgrip insert; however, the sides are textured so it's not really a problem. My only quibble with the larger block is the size and "smashability" of the non-stick grip feet. These are about a little longer than a typical rubber cling stamp, and allow you to carefully position the block w/ the stamp over your card or paper before you have to commit to actually stamping. That part is fine. However, the feet don't "collapse" very easily. For the thicker cling rubber stamps, this isn't as much of an issue since it takes a lot less pressure to "collapse" the feet enough for the stamp to hit the card. But it takes quite a lot of pressure to press a thinner clear stamp onto the card. Fiskars has another similar version of this style of block with sponge feet that accomplishes the same thing: position the stamp, then press to stamp -- that is much easier to use. Perhaps the feet will "soften" with use -- right now, I worry about cracking the block due to the amount of pressure needed to stamp with clear stamps, so I'll probably use it only for my cling rubber stamps for now.

Overall, I really do like this set. The sizes are useful for the types of stamps I use, and the thinness plus the softgrip handles make it easier to position and stamp. The larger block is the only one I'm less enthusiastic about, simply due to the design of the grip feet. So -- solid 4 stars.

I've done "traditional" calligraphy using a metal nib and bottled ink, but this is my first experience with brush lettering. And I've been using Tombow dual-end markers for coloring for a couple of years, so I'm familiar with the quality of their products.

Overall, this is a nice set that includes just about everything you need to get started: 4 dual brush pens, a colorless blender, 2 finer-point calligraphy pens, a black permanent marker, a drawing pencil, and an eraser.

Although the instructions included in the kit are minimal, there's a link on the package to the Tombow website where you can download a 17-page lettering workbook that includes the entire alphabet -- upper and lower case -- numbers, and punctuation/special characters. The instructions are clear and easy to follow, and with practice, I achieved satisfactory results. I'm using this set to add greetings and sentiments to the hand-crafted greeting cards that I make for friends and family. I've been using rubber and acrylic stamps for this in the past, so it's nice to be able to add an artistic touch myself.

I'm using this to corral my balls of crochet thread. I make doilies and lace trims using size 10 or 20 crochet thread. These usually are NOT center-pull; rather they unwind from the outside in, which means that unless the ball is "controlled" somehow, it will likely wind up across the room somewhere or under the sofa.

Enter the Yarn Tainer. This small size is perfect for all of my crochet thread ball sizes, but it's large enough that I can store the unfinished project + hook + instruction sheet inside the jar which helps keep me more organized.

No complaints at all -- this is a good quality item, all trims/latches fit perfectly, no rough edges, etc. Solid 5 stars -- I love it!

These are VERY fine point water-based markers. Because of the super fine point, you'll probably want to use these mostly on highly detailed coloring projects or writing -- NOT for general coloring where there are larger spaces to fill in.

PROS: They're decent quality -- not as good as Tombow Dual Brush but a little better than Crayola Supertips neither of which has the super fine point you find with these. I've been using them for a couple of weeks, and the quality is consistent from color to color. They write smoothly, and because they're water-based, they don't bleed through as badly as alcohol-based markers (Copic, Spectrum Noir, Sharpies, etc.).

CONS: There are NO color numbers or names. I added my own numbers just so I could keep track of which colors I used where. The case is difficult to open at first -- I almost had to resort to brute force with either a screwdriver or box cutter but finally got the "catches" to release. This has improved with use, but just be aware that you may have to fight with the case the first time out.

Overall, I like these for certain types of projects -- I'm making a set of holiday greeting cards using a set of very detailed rubber stamps of holiday images (trees, poinsettias, etc.), and they're perfect for this. I've also used them with the more detailed Valerie Harper coloring designs (sample in photo below). So 4 stars -- I like them.

I do a lot of thread crochet -- doilies, lace trims, etc. -- and Aunt Lydia's is one of my favorites. It's one of the more economical choices (compared to the "luxury" brands), and it's a consistently good performer for the kinds of things I like to make.

The colors are good, too -- I chose this "Cream" to use with some small crochet mandalas. The yellowish tone worked perfectly with other Aunt Lydia colors (Maize, etc.) to get the effect I wanted of shading from light (center) to dark.

Definitely can recommend this -- good quality at a good price. I like it.

I knit and crochet, but I've never tried weaving -- just never got around to it. This little device is an interesting reasonably economical way to try it out without investing too much time and resources into it.

Setting it up isn't difficult at all -- just pay attention to the videos and be careful about making sure the pins are pointed in the right direction. Instructions are reasonably clear, too, about how to cut the yarn for the warp: double the length of whatever you want to make plus 12". That extra 12" is critical -- I found increasing it to 14"-16" worked better that the recommended 12" while I was still experimenting.

The actual weaving isn't difficult, either, but bear in mind that this is made of PLASTIC, and those pins are tall. You really need to be careful not to "pull" the yarn at the end of each row; otherwise, you bend the pins which can lead to breakage. Just be careful with it, work gently, and you should be fine.

The yarn that comes with this is a medium weight (about worsted weight, maybe a tad smaller), so your finished item with this yarn is fairly thick. I just ordered some lighter-weight (DK weight) yarn to use for the weft, and some coordinating fingering-weight yarn for the warp that I think will work better for the scarf I want to make. That's one of the positives about this: you can use almost any yarn on the market and still get a decent result.

As far as age group for this, I wouldn't recommend it for anyone younger than 8 or 9, and even then under close adult supervision due to the fragility of the plastic pins. I do think it would be perfect for anyone over 11 or 12 yrs old who is interested in creative projects like this. There are lots of Youtube videos showing how to make various projects, and since you can choose what type of yarn to use, the possibilities are endless.

As for me, I'll make a scarf using the single loom -- so 7" wide by around 60" long. After that, I'll decide if I like it enough to invest in an extender loom set to make placemats, etc. The jury's still out but so far, I do like it. It's an easy way to experiment with weaving -- solid 4 stars.

I got these to use to make small crocheted "mandalas" using size 10 crochet thread. These are exactly what I had in mind.

The quality is excellent (Darice is a quality brand in the crafts world) -- very smooth, nice finish, no scratches/chips, not too thick and not too thin.

I've also purchased these in the 4" size for holiday ornaments (crocheted, natch).

No complaints at all -- these are precisely what I need for my project. Highly recommended.

Charles Wysocki is well known for his paintings of nostalgic Americana -- almost primitive in style, always colorful and comforting in their subjects and detail. His images have been used for calendars, jigsaw puzzles, note cards, and now ... a coloring book!

Those familiar with Wysocki's work will instantly recognize the angular buildings and 1890's feel (horses and buggies abound). The print quality is good -- sharp and clear. The paper is what I'd call "average" quality -- not too heavy, but not too light, either.

Even better: The images are printed on one side only -- the reverse side contains only the name of the drawing. And the pages are perforated, making it easy to remove a page for coloring and/or framing. This means you can use any coloring medium you like -- no worries about bleed through ruining the backside image.

The level of detail is exactly as one would expect given the artist: some "wide open spaces" as well as some tiny details -- providing good variety and different levels of challenge. This would be a nice book to bring along on a cross-country road trip, or a relaxing vacation by the beach.

Solid 4 stars -- I like it.

I'm using this in several colors to make scarves for a crafts fair later this year. This Bonfire color is lovely!

The photo shows mostly dark brown/red/burgundy. It's actually a combination of YELLOW, orange, red, reddish brown, and a dark chocolate brown. I really like that the you get quite a lot of yardage in each color, and the transitions are gradual. I'm including photos of the two balls I received, both from the same dye lot, and totally different in appearance until you peak into the ball a bit.

It's a soft yarn, partially mohair, but not the "scratchy" mohair I recall from my school days. The texture is perfect for the scarves I'm planning to make.

I've started one with this color (I also have this in Woodrose and Porcelain), and I'm really liking how it's crocheting up (using a size 4.0mm hook). I'll post another photo once I finish the scarf.

I have fairly severe osteoarthritis in my hands and wrists -- especially in my right hand, my "hook" hand. I've tried almost every hook available, and this is the one I use most often.

It's not perfect, but it IS the most comfortable for me. It fits my hand nicely, isn't too long or too short, and the shape is easy to hold and maneuver. I should add that I hold my hook "knife style," rather than "pencil style."

I'm using this 3.75mm (size F) hook to make some daisy-shaped coasters with cotton yarn. No issues at all with quality; the metal part is securely embedded in the plastic handle, and it's totally smooth (no snags). I have these Soft Touch hooks in several sizes and use them for both yarn and thread (I have the 1.75mm, 1.50mm, and 1.25mm sizes for thread work).

No more searching for the "best" hook -- for me, this one works the best.

Aunt Lydia's Classic 10 is my "go to" crochet thread, and this "Peacock" color is gorgeous, even more intense than the photo.  I've already done one doily with it and have plans for several more.  The quality of this thread is consistent with other Aunt Lydia's Classic 10 colors (sometimes there can be differences ... ).  No complaints at all.  Will continue to purchase and use Aunt Lydia's as long as I can still crochet.  Love it!

I'm using this to make coasters in the shape of a daisy, with yellow centers and white petals. It's perfect for my purpose -- easy to work with, and with this size, economical, too. I need to make a LOT of these coasters, so this will be more than enough to finish the project.

I've used Sugar'n Cream for years to make pot holders and washcloths, so I know it holds up to a lot of use. I have pot holders that I crocheted in the 1990s that are still going strong after literally hundreds of washings. Nice colors, good quality.

I purchased these to use in blocking my thread crochet projects (mostly doilies, table runners, snowflakes, etc.). I needed something that could stand up to wet cotton without rusting or other nastiness. I've used these on two pristine white doilies (and a number of doilies of varying colors from light to dark), and not even a hint of a problem.

The size is perfect for my use -- not too long, not too short. I use them with [[ASIN:B0050P218E TG 8 Piece Multi-Color Eva Foam Exercise Mat (Medium)]] -- 8 interlocking foam squares that I can configure to the size I need for larger items. The combo is perfect for my needs.

You get 100 pins per package. OK, I didn't do a definitive count, but I managed to block 3 doilies today, one of which needed 48 pins, another needed 24, and the third needed 18, and I had pins left over in the package. Still, if you're blocking larger items, you might want to do what I did and spring for 2 packs (200 pins) just in case.

No complaints here -- they work and don't rust. That's 5 stars any day for me.

I've used the Clover Soft Touch aluminum hooks (the ones made for yarn) and had a good experience with them, so when I decided to take up thread crochet again after a 10+-year hiatus, I didn't hesitate to order the Soft Touch steel hooks (I purchased both a 1.5mm and 1.75mm).

I have fairly severe osteoarthritis in my hands and wrists, so finding a comfortable crochet hook is crucial. I've tried a number of other brands/styles (including the Clover Amour), and this one just seems to work best for my particular issues. I'd like it to be maybe another 1/2"-3/4" longer, but it's OK, and I can usually crochet for 3-4 hrs at a stretch with minimal pain/discomfort. Other hook styles/makes resulted in fairly severe pain after only 15-30 minutes or so -- even much pricier ones specifically designed to be "ergonomic."

I'm not saying this one is perfect -- but the size and shape seem to work best for my particular situation so far. For now, it's my hook of choice.

This was my first experience with Iris nylon crochet thread. It's ... interesting. The size is comparable to size 10 crochet cotton, maybe just a smidge smaller. The colors are vibrant, and the quality is quite nice.

Since it's nylon, ironing is probably not a good idea. I wanted to use it for a doily and ended up combining it with some size 10 crochet cotton so that I could block it. It worked nicely -- the cotton holds its shape after blocking, so the nylon stays blocked as well. The doily at the top in the photo uses this nylon thread plus yellow crochet cotton.

I like this enough to want to try other colors and see how it behaves on its own.

I've had my eye on this stamp set since they first came out. Such a fun, cute, and quirky flock! Easy to stamp and then color with markers, pencils, or inks. I also invested in the matching die set. Variety is the spice of life, and I really do enjoy making cards of various types: formal, "frilly" as well as more casual and funny.

These are good quality cling stamps, too -- good size as well (2" or so). I'm making a set of "themed" cards for a fund-raiser using these: get well, "thinking of you," birthday, etc.

I'm much more of a "doodler" than a "real artist," so the idea of having something to "kickstart" the creative juices is definitely appealing to someone like me.

And for the most part, this is a good sketchbook. It's hardcover, nice binding, pages are perforated, a nice mix of pages printed with dots, triangles, hexagons and grid squares plus enough plain paper to allow for total freedom from preconceived design elements.

The problem, however, is that both brands of the alcohol markers I use most often -- BIC Mark-its and Sharpie, both ultra-fine point -- bleed through. Not a lot, and it's only the darker more saturated colors (black, dark green, purple). It's definitely less bleed through than one would normally get with "standard" sketch paper, but if they claim is "The Derwent Inspire Me Sketchbooks are completely bleedproof, meaning you can create pieces of work without the worry of it running through." -- that just isn't the case for these popular alcohol markers.

For water-based markers, this is ideal, and that's probably what I'll use most often. My Staedtler Triplus Fineliners worked great -- absolutely NO bleedthrough at all. Recommended only if you're planning to use water-based markers or pencils.

This review is for the Silver version of this thread.

I've used this for years to make crocheted snowflakes, along with the white/pearl version. I just replenished my "stash" of this so I'm ready for next Christmas projects.

Normally, for size 10 thread, I use a size 8 (1.50mm) hook. But for this, I find I need to use a slightly larger hook -- 1.65mm or 1.75mm -- as the additional metallic filament increases the diameter of the thread just enough that it makes using the smaller hook difficult -- it's too easy to snag the filament without the thread when I'm going fast.

I've been using DMC's Cebelia Crochet Cotton for a LOT of years (at least 25), and it's always high quality.

This review is for the Beige Rose color -- really a light dusty apricot -- but I've used almost every color they make at one time or another, and they're always excellent quality. The manufacturing process results in a slight silky "sheen" and tight twist so it flows beautifully from the ball. This size 10 thread is just about right for my uses (mostly doilies, lace trims, and holiday decorations). I've used the smaller size 20 and 30, but my eyes just aren't up to it anymore. No problem -- my lacy crocheted items always turn out looking good with this thread.

This will continue to be my "go to" thread for lacy crocheted things.

I actually purchased this at full price and I'm very happy with it.

I recently purchased a new set of 72 colored pencils and needed something to store them in instead of the box they came in. Saw this, thought it looked OK and decided to take a chance.

It's really very nicely made and accommodates the 72 pencils perfectly. I got the "Bohemian" design (loved the colors and pattern), and it's even nicer in person than in the photo. The interior includes a flap that can protect the tips and the inclusion of the extra beads for the ties is a nice touch. I'm including a few photos below.

I'm planning to order another one to store my 36 Staedtler Triplus Fineliners -- I know that some markers won't fit in this (no way that BIC Mark-its or Sharpies will fit ... ), but I think the Fineliners are "slim" enough to work. Will update this as soon as I try it.

Overall, though -- very happy with this. Solid 5 stars from me -- yes, even at full price. :-)

I use a lot of this in my card making. It's true 110lb paper (says so right there on the label), and rated 94 brightness which is a nice clear white.

Most of my cards are 4.25" x 5.5" - so this 8.5" x 11" works perfectly -- I get 2 cards from each sheet. It's acid and lignin free, too -- so no yellowing or discoloring over time. Really good quality, and so much cheaper than purchasing paper crafter card stock. It also works with my HP printer, so I can computer journal to add sentiments or digital images to my cards. I also get great results when using it with embossing folders, with nice crisp impressions without any cracking (see photo below). It has a nice smooth finish, too, which means that alcohol markers (e.g., Copic, Spectrum Noir, etc.) don't bleed quite so badly as with other card stock.

Overall, very happy with this.

Really beautiful sparkly glitter tape, in great colors for scrapbooking, cardmaking, or other crafts.

I'm a card maker -- and these tapes work great to add just a hint of "bling" to my cards. You get twelve in all -- and they're over 10 yards each, so you get a lot in each roll. I used the two blue shades, the purpose, and the "white" (silver) for a few sets of holiday cards. The green and pink tapes will find homes on some spring card sets, and I'll definitely be using the yellow/orange/brown tapes for my fall cards this year.

Very nice quality and a good size, too. Very happy with these, and can recommend highly!

This is a really nice collection of patterned papers -- actually more like light cardstock -- with whimsical colorful designs.

I needed something to make some birthday cards, and this really is perfect. It has patterns that will work great for both adults and children. You get 12 12"x12" sheets of double-sided paper and 2 12"x12" sheets of stickers. One sticker sheet is "element" stickers (figures/shapes/sentiments/borders/etc. derived from the designs and patterns), the other sheet contains "alphabet" stickers and includes a few shapes and sentiments. Everything is coordinated in a navy/soft red/aqua/pink/soft lime color scheme.

I've found that many of these papers will also coordinate nicely with [[ASIN:B00M3401E6 Echo Park Paper Company Creative Agenda Collection Scrapbooking Kit]], which also features navy, aqua, soft red, etc.

Really happy with this.

I make a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards, and use a lot of punches in my work, so I looked forward to trying out this Marth Stewart Pennant Garland punch.

For my use, it's ... OK. Not great, not terrible, just OK.

First, it's HUGE -- the punch itself is large: 6.5 " long x 3.25" wide x 3.25" tall at the highest point; and fairly heavy. Storage could be an issue if you're used to smaller punches. Even my large 3"+ circle or daisy punches aren't this big/heavy.

The punched pennant itself isn't too big, but maybe a tad too big for use on 4.25" x 5.50" greeting cards. The pennant measures 1 7/8" x 1 3/4", plus the "tab" that allows you to link several together into a garland.

I found the punch works best on heavier designer patterned paper or card stock. With thinner designer paper, I can't get a clean cut. There's a section along the top edge of the pennant that is rough/curved -- obviously a manufacturing defect. (see photo -- this is plain white designer paper) It doesn't show up with heavier paper, however.

Overall, I may use this a few times, probably for birthday cards. And I do like the idea of this punch: a way to link multiple punched pennant shapes into a garland. I'm just not sure how/where this would be most useful. Maybe scrapbook pages?

I'm a paper crafter, making mostly handcrafted greeting cards. I've had the Brother ScanNCut on my wish list for a couple of years, adding it when Brother first announced the original ScanNCut machine. I have no experience with other similar machines (Silhouette, Cricut, etc.); I've been using a manual die cutting machine (Sizzix) for several years with metal dies. So I'm coming at this with no basis of comparison to other similar cutting machines.

First things first: there are 2 models of the ScanNCut2: the CM350 (this machine) and the CM650, a pricier model. The difference is what's included. The CM350 is "wifi ready" but does NOT include the activation card you need to be able to use wifi; the CM650 comes with the card included in the price. So understand that you will NOT be able to connect yor ScanNCut2 to the internet until you invest another $50 for the activation card.

That said, you can do just about everything you need to do without the wifi connection as long as you're using a desktop or laptop computer with a USB port available. If you're using a tablet without a USB connection, then you'll need to invest the extra $50 for the wifi activation card in order to move cutting files from your tablet or the web to the ScanNCut2. There's been some confusion about whether or not the ScanNCut2 is usable without wifi -- I've been using it for over 3 weeks WITHOUT activating wifi, and it works just fine. I've been using a USB memory stick to transfer files from my laptop to the ScanNCut2, and have had no problems at all.

So -- set up. Easy, very straightforward. In the box, you get the machine, power cord, standard mat, standard blade + blade holder, two pens (red & black) + pen holder, the rhinestone kit, and access to Brother's "Canvas," a free web-based application that allows you to access additional designs/fonts, convert SVG (Silhouette cutting files) to Brother's FCM file format, and generally augment the built-in patterns/designs with material from the web. It took me less than 10 minutes from opening the box to being ready to play.

From there, how to proceed really depends on what you want to do with the ScanNCut. I work ONLY with paper, not fabric, so my approach may be quite different. For example, although the CM350R here on Amazon comes with a rhinestone kit, I don't do "bedazzling"-type projects (tee shirts, etc.) that use iron-on or heat-set rhinestones. I honestly have no use for this -- other reviewers have written about how that works. So what follows is my experience scanning and cutting paper for my various card projects.

Each activity takes several steps, so learning to do even some fairly simple things takes a bit of reading, digesting, and practice. Eventually, these steps will become second nature, but at first, it can take a certain amount of focus and concentration to get through a task. Just hang in there! I've been at this for over 3 weeks, and I'm just starting to feel pretty comfortable with the basic scan / cut processes.

The mat that comes in the box is VERY sticky, so if you're playing with paper, you'll need to be careful. A trick I learned for dealing with overly sticky things that need to be not so sticky is to pat the surface with a clean tee shirt. Enough of the microfibers will adhere to the sticky and reduce its stickiness a bit. I did that with the standard mat, and it worked. Still, be careful with anything less than 80# paper or card stock.

I used the recommended settings for my first cuts, and it worked just fine. I used the built-in designs for those first trials, resizing them, etc. No issues at all.

Then, I stamped a few images on white card stock, scanned them, and then had the ScanNCut2 cut them with varying "borders" (wide, narrow). This was fun -- and probably the way I'll use the ScanNCut most often. Stamp and image, color with markers, then cut around it to create a focal that I can pop up on a card with foam squares. I used to have to spend hours "fussy cutting" these images. ScanNCut is PERFECT for this! Also creating a mat in a different color card stock -- I tried that with a couple of my stamped designs as well. Yes, THIS is what I was hoping for.

If you have the patience and tenacity to stick with this, you'll find it does pay off in the end. I just may be able to retire my Sizzix in a couple of months and go with the ScanNCut for all my paper-cutting needs.

Overall, I'm very happy with the ScanNCut. It's a nice solid machine that should provide years of productive service.

Even without activating the wifi on your ScanNCut, you can use Canvas to create new designs, but you'll need to use a memory stick (USB) or directly connect the ScanNCut to your computer to transfer the files to the ScanNCut for cutting. This may or may not be a major inconvenience for you. I still don't have the activation card and I've been working with the ScanNCut2 for 3 weeks now, using Canvas periodically to test out its functions. So you really CAN do everything

Cuts beautifully! I use this quite often and get great results every time.

When they say "large," they mean it. The punched area is about 7/8" wide from the edge of the card or paper.

It's easy to line up the punch to create a longer decorative edge by repeating the punches. You can also punch along the edge of some paper or card stock, and the trim the punched edge about 1/8" from the punched area to make a 1"-wide "ribbon."

Easy to use, cuts cleanly, well worth the investment.

This "Deep Edge Punch, Embroidery" can be a little difficult to punch, but it is pretty. I like that it folds up for storage so it takes up less room. Just be aware that if you're punching heavy cardstock, it can take a lot of muscle to get it to punch. And if you're punching relatively light-weight designer paper/etc., it might be a little tricky to remove from the punch without snagging/tearing.

I had the best results using it with medium-weight cardstock (Photo below). It's like most edge punches: relatively easy to line up so that you get a nice continuous border.

So many uses for this!  The size makes it easy to trim and the colors lend themselves to any season.  You get 4 sheets each of six different colors.  I did a coordinated set of handcrafted greeting cards using this, but it would also work wonderfully to highlight photos in a scrapbook layout.

One of the problems with the original Secret Garden (and Enchanted Forest, too) is that the images are printed on both sides of a page, which makes using alcohol markers impossible as they bleed through and ruin the image on the next (or previous) page. The only options were to photocopy the pages you want to color or purchase two copies of the book. Or just use pencils, gel pens, or (some) water-based markers that aren't as prone to bleed through as alcohol markers.

This new "Artist's Edition" solves that dilemma. Not only are the images printed on one side only, the paper is thicker and of much higher quality. The page size is also larger and the pages are removable for framing.

Granted, there are only 20 images, but the ability to use whatever coloring medium one wants makes it totally worth it. Can't wait for a similar edition of Enchanted Forest!

I'm really in love with this stamp! I belong to an organization that has as one of its emblems a daisy (or "marguerite"). I love making hand-crafted greetings for fundraisers for this group, so I was very happy with I found this one!

It's not large -- the image size is maybe 1 7/8" x 2 1/4" -- but it's nicely detailed. I've stamped it with Versamark and used white embossing powder on a navy background, then touched the center with gold liquid pearl or Stickles. The effect is stunning! I've also stamped it in black on white cardstock and colored the center with Spectrum Noir markers in yellows. You could even change the variety by using different colors for the petals (fuschia, orange, etc.). So many ways you can use this!

It's typical Hero Arts quality: solid wood block, perfect impressions every time, easy to clean.

Super variety here -- and the style is extremely versatile, too.

I do a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards for fundraisers​ so I need a good variety of sentiments. This set provides a large/bold primary sentiment, with several "mix and match" smaller "add-ons" to complete a phrase. This provides a lot of possibilities!

The style has a sort of quirky "old fashioned" look that works on both fancy "lace and ribbons" style cards, as well as more contemporary modern style. The quality is typical of Penny Black: excellent. No issues with bubbling or flaws. Just peel the stamp off the acrylic sheet, and place it on your clear stamping block - it's ready to go.

I've used these with inks of various types as well as heat embossing -- never had a problem

Bottom line: this is an excellent set of basic sentiments covering a variety of events/holidays, done in a versatile style. Highly recommended.

UPDATE: May 27, 2015:
I'm happy to report that the seller Ohuhu stands behind their product. The issues I mentioned in my review of May 21 have been resolved to my satisfaction. I believe fair is fair, and their willingness to remedy the situation means a lot. I have modified my star rating to reflect my satisfaction with both the product (they ARE good pencils) and their customer service.

ORIGINAL REVIEW: May 21, 2015
First, the good news: these are nice quality pencils, not up to Prismacolor standards, but certainly better than most of the lower-priced alternatives. They lay down some good color, can be blended with a blending pencil, and shading "works" like it's supposed to. The actual "brand" is Marco Raffiné -- made in China. Ohuhu may be the manufacturer?

However, there are some issues (you be the judge of whether or not they're "big" issues):

1. My box contains 47 pencils, NOT 48. I have no idea which one is missing, but I counted them 3 times, and there are only 47 in the box.
2. Several colors are duplicated, so you're not getting 47 (or 48 if you're lucky) different colors. In my box, specifically, color #s 501 (white), 502 (cream), 506 (orange), and 505 (golden/orange) all had duplicates.
3. Two of the pencils had obviously been used and sharpened. They were noticeably shorter, and the sharpening pattern was different from what I assume is the "factory" sharpening pattern ("wide" point vs "narrow" point). This set may have been a return at some point, and was placed back in the "sellable" inventory without checking to see if it had been used.

So buyer beware -- based on some of the other reviews, you may luck out and get a full set of 48 pencils with no duplicates. If so, great! The pencils are actually quite good quality and you should get a lot of good use from them.

I'll keep my set "as is," since going through the hassle of returning them just isn't worth it. There are enough colors (43 not counting the duplicates) to make them useful for my purposes. I'd award 2.5 stars if I could; I like the pencils; just not happy with the assortment/quantity.

I'm really liking these dies so far.
