You get 3 sizes of rectangles, the largest of which is perfect for covering a 4 1/4" x 5 1/2" card base with a narrow 1/16" border showing (it cuts to 4 1/8" x 5 3/8"). You also get 3 corresponding "inset" dies that make the pierced borders around the edges of the rectangles.

To cut a "plain" rectangle, you use just the rectangle die. To cut a rectangle with a pierced border, you run BOTH the rectangle and the corresponding inset die nested together through your die cutting machine and voilá, you get a pierced rectangle. Easy-peasy!

All three sizes work for me, but I find I use the largest one the most. I use these with my Sizzix Big Shot, and so far, no problems getting clean cuts.

I make a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards, and use die cuts on most of them. Cutting a square without a die isn't that tricky, really -- but cutting one with PIERCING certainly is! That's where these dies come into play.

You get 8 square dies ranging from 3/4" to 3 3/8", all with piercing around the edges. This adds another dimension to a card -- just a little more detail, especially on my less formal cards. I especially like to use them as backgrounds for "floating focals": a stamped/colored image mounted with foam tape or squares so that it appears to "float" above the pierced background. I also use the two largest squares together to produce a pierced "frame," and then cut a square from the 4th largest die as the background for my focal, centered inside the pierced square frame -- I'll try to post a pic a little later.

As usual with MFT, the quality of the dies is superb. I use them with my Sizzix Big Shot with no problems at all. These are already getting a lot of use.

I use a LOT of Spellbinders dies in my card making, primarily because the quality is superb and the designs are lovely.

This is the newest addition to my collection. The sizes are perfect for my card making. I make a lot of cards that are 5" x 6.5" -- so the largest die in this set works perfectly to highlight a special focal element or sentiment. For my smaller cards (4.25" x 5.5"), the middle and smaller dies are great for layering underneath a sentiment banner. Add some small pearls or tiny flat rhinestones for a bit of extra bling at the cross-hatch intersections -- the end result is striking! These also work nicely for weaving ribbons over/under the elements. Just lovely.

I have had no issues using these with my Sizzix Big Shot die cutting machine. For these more intricate dies, I use a metal shim like this [[ASIN:B00DV8XPAU CottageCutz Universal Shim Plate Die Cuts]] -- place it so that the die cuts AWAY from it, so that it pushes the die into the paper/cardstock. Works great every time.

If you want to add a bit of elegance to your cards or scrapbook pages, look no further. This is an excellent set.

This board is perfect for my use. I make hand-crafted greeting cards, and often stamp in ink an outline of an image, and then color it using colored pencils, watercolor pencils, alcohol markers, or gel pens. I needed a sturdy flat surface that I could "prop" in my lap and lean on the desk and this works perfectly for that.

It's a good size, sturdy but light-weight, and the heavy plastic clasp is strong enough to hold my image securely. I have noticed no quality issues at all; it is perfectly finished, smooth rounded corners, smooth "rivets" attaching the clasp to the board, nice smooth white surface. I have to say that it really exceeded my expectations given the price.

Solid 5 stars!

I've used other Glue Dots products for years in papercrafts (specifically making hand-crafted greeting cards). I like them due to the ease of use and choice of sizes. This was my first experience with the Glue Dots Repositionable dots.

They worked well to affix embellishments to my cards. Things like layered paper flowers, 3-D focals, even ribbons and buttons. I was able to reposition them as long as worked carefully; they had a tendency to stick a bit TOO strongly to card stock, but if I worked slowly I could avoid tearing or "peeling" the paper.

Moving beyond crafts, I used these to affix pictures and menus to my wood-front fridge, and they worked GREAT for that. No problems sticking to the wood, and repositioning was a piece of cake.

I actually like the smaller size of these -- 3/8" is just right for crafts, and big enough to handle putting posters on walls, menus on fridges, etc.

I'll likely purchase more of these when this batch runs out.

I purchased these to use on some hand-crafted Christmas cards, and the colors and gems were perfect for my use.

As others have mentioned, the "glue" isn't very strong on these, so I ended up using a small dot of glossy accents [[ASIN:B000S14MNC Ranger Inkssentials Glossy Accents Precision Tip, 2-Ounce]] to secure them to my cards. No problem -- it worked just fine.

Would purchase again.

I don't do much origami, but I DO make a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards, and this worked great to use as background papers for quick and easy holiday cards. My cards are either 4.25" x 5.5" OR 5" x 6.5" -- so the size of this paper works just fine for either one, since I usually leave about 1/4" border showing.

The colors are 2 shades of red (not "tomato" red -- more of a brick red); 3 shades of green (2 are slightly "olive" tone -- not emerald or kelly; the other is more of a "forest" green); 2 shades of brown (darker, and lighter gold/beige); and an almost greek-flag blue. Most of the prints are on a white background. There are a few solids, and a number of geometric prints. There was really only one design out of 32 that I really didn't like -- not bad, really! The prints are small enough to work well on cards, too.

The paper quality is fine -- it doesn't take kindly to any "wet" adhesive (including glue stick), but works OK with double-sided tape.

Overall, this was a good buy for my purposes. I'm well-stocked with papers for dozens of holiday cards!

I've tried a LOT of different hooks over the years, and these are my favorites. I use the "knife hold," and I've developed osteoarthritis in my hands as I've gotten older, and these are the most comfortable for my hands.

Not every hook will work well for everyone, though, so if in doubt, order *1* of these hooks and see if you like it before ordering the entire set. I made several projects with a couple of these, and then decided to get the set - and I'm very happy with it. I like the case -- it's brightly colored so it's easy to spot in my project bag. I've had no problems with any of the hooks falling out of the case (mentioned in a couple of other reviews), so that may just be due to slight variations in case construction.

Bottom line: At the time I purchased this, I paid less than thirty-five dollars for this set, a good price for a complete set of these hooks with case.

I got this to use with some hand-crafted Christmas ornaments that I'm making for family and friends this year. I also purchased the silver version of this, and I'm very happy with both.

This gold cord is a pretty gold: not too dark, not too light -- just the right shade of gold, not too "tinsel"-ly looking, either. It has some"heft' to it -- it's almost exactly 1/16" in diameter, so it makes a statement without being so big that it's difficult to work with. It would also work well for gift wrapping, tying gift bags -- anywhere you need a bit of golden glitz.

Would definitely purchase again!

I know this yarn is intended for socks, but it's useful for so much more! I'm using it in the "Faded Jeans" color to crochet a lacy scarf, and it's beautiful! I love the color transitions, some wide, some narrow, that add interest and "spark" to my work.

Quality is typical Red Heart -- very nice, indeed. Depending on how this scarf goes, I may order more and make another one or two.

I use a LOT of these Prima "Say It In ... " embellishments ("Say It In Studs," "Say It In Pearls," etc.). I make hand-crafted greeting cards, and these make gorgeous centers for die-cut or punched flowers (the larger ones). They come in a variety of colors, but this set is probably my favorite.

It features pink sure -- but look closely: there's a lot of black and gold featured as well. Very classy, they add just the right amount of glitz to my cards.

The quality is there, too -- these are Prima, after all -- so you needn't worry about bits falling off.

Will continue to purchase these, for sure.

I usually make hand-crafted greeting cards, but lately I've been making some travel journals by "re-purposing" composition books - covering the covers with travel-themed papers, including pockets for photos, etc.

I happened upon these stickers while looking for something else on Amazon, and fell in love! Ordered them, and I'm NOT disappointed. The size is perfect for my use. The largest sticker (the "see the world" label) is 2 1/4" long; the smallest ("explore" label) is 1 1/4".

These are the usual high quality you always find with Sticko -- easy to remove from the backing with no tearing; easy to apply. They're just slightly dimensional -- the print on some of them is slightly raised.

Overall, very happy with these.

I find myself using a lot of Sticko stickers lately on my hand-crafted greeting cards, and I'm delighted to have found these black flourish stickers.

They're printed on a thin clear acetate, with a hint of silver foil that adds a bit of sparkle. They work really nicely with a lot of my papers, and add nice accents to my focals. They're excellent quality, too -- easy to remove from the backing and apply with no tearing (a problem I've had with lower-quality stickers).

Will definitely order these again.

These are much brighter than the photo shows. Super bright lemon yellow, pinks, orange, red, aqua -- really nice! They're highlighted with a little glitter -- just enough to add some "bling" without going overboard. The sizes range from about 3/8" for the smallest flowers to 2" for the largest owl.

I use a lot of stickers in my card making and these will fit right in. These are not the usual paper stickers; they're printed on a clear acetate -- so I can mount them on any color without having to deal with a "white" border (as with some other stickers I've used in the past).

These will make really nice cards for my owl-loving friends. Very happy with this purchase.

... much prettier than the photo. Over the years, I've become a great fan of Sticko stickers for my hand-crafted greeting cards. They're always high quality, and the colors are wonderful.

This set is no different. The flowers are vibrant blues, red, pink, yellow, and orange. What doesn't show up at all on the photo is that every sticker is trimmed in beautiful cobalt-blue foil. What looks like navy blue in the photo is actually the blue foil trim. It adds just the right amount of pizazz to the images.

I'll be using these as focals on some of my "everyday" cards (thank you, hello, thinking of you, etc.). I'll likely mat them on either white pearl or black card stock. The sizes range from just over 3" for the largest flowers/stems to about 1 1/4" for the smallest flower and the smaller paisley design. They're not huge, but they're not insignificant, either. I should be able to get at least 5 cards from the set of 9 stickers. They're priced right, too.

These are really nice -- I may order another set of them!

These are a little smaller than I expected, but that's OK -- they're really pretty! The colors are close to what you see in the photo, but what doesn't show up as well is the delicate silver foil border around each one. The largest stickers are 3/4"-1" in diameter; others range from 1/4" for the smallest to 5/8" for the next-to-largest. These will work nicely for my hand-crafted greeting cards, clustered around a featured sentiment or matted on "inchies."

You do get a lot of stickers on the sheet -- 46 in all -- and the colors are yummy: soft pink, deeper coral, a peacock blue, and a nice violet.

I should be able to get at least 8 cards from this pack, so it's a good deal for me.

If you're familiar with Spellbinders dies, you already know the quality and care they put into their designs. If you're not familiar with Spellbinders, then this would be an excellent set to start with if you enjoy making cards.

I've been using this set to make some holiday cards, and it makes it so easy! The largest die fits perfectly on an A2 card front (4 1/4" x 5 1/2"), and perfectly frames a focal (I've been using some filigree die cut Christmas ornaments cut from foiled card stock). Layer on the next size in a contrasting color or texture, and feature a smaller focal. The possibilities are endless! The long/skinny "label" works perfectly for featuring a sentiment.

If you're into scrapbooking, this set can be used to elegantly frame treasured photos. Really, there are so many places this set will come in handy.

It coordinates perfectly, too, with Spellbinders Label 28 ([[ASIN:B008F4T7UW Spellbinders S5-127 Nestabilities Labels Twenty Eight Die Templates]] for additional layering/matting possibilities.

I own several of these A2 card sets from Spellbinders, but this one has been getting a good workout with my Christmas cards this year. The larges one works as a background to center and feature a lacy ornament. Add the next largest piece in a different color, and highlight a smaller ornament or other focal. The long-skinny label die included is wonderful for highlighting a sentiment. So many ways to layer and use these!

As usual, these are very high quality dies. They work nicely with my Sizzix Big Shot (I use a couple of pieces of scrap card stock as shims, just to be sure all the "bits" punch through completely), and they're easy to clean the extra bits out after use. I've used them on fairly heavy foiled card stock, "regular" card stock, and lighter-weight patterned paper. No problems at all.

If you're looking for ONE useful card making die set, this is probably the one I'd recommend. So versatile, and just beautiful!

I make a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards, many with pencil-colored images. I just started using watercolor pencils, so these made perfect sense.

I use these with my Faber Castell Aquarelle Watercolor pencils ([[ASIN:B000EX0HA6 Faber-Castell Art GRIP Aquarelle Watercolor Pencils, tin of 24]]), and they work great! There are three brushes, each a different tip size (small, medium, wide), and each cap is a different color so you can keep them straight. I use the large one to get a "wash" of color across a card front; then stamp a message or image on the blended colors. The two smaller ones I use to blend details in focal images. It takes a little getting used to, but with a bit of practice, it's easy to get the effect I want.

I also use them with my Faber Castell Gelatos ([[ASIN:B00JPNVJAS FABER-CASTELL 39 PIECE GELATOS]]) to blend colors for backgrounds.

Very happy with these!

This is another winner from the team of JustRite, Spellbinders, and Amazing Paper Grace (Becca Feeken). It's not too large, but not too small, either: the actual piece is 3 3/4" x 2" -- a nice size that will work on both cards (A2 or larger) and scrapbook pages.

I use this with a Sizzix Big Shot, and usually use a couple of pieces of card stock as a shim for these more intricate dies. This one is a stunner -- beautiful form, lots of possibilities for layering and intertwining with ribbon.

This is Heirloom Flourish Two -- there's also an Heirloom Flourish One that is a little narrower, but equally lovely.

THese should be called "studs with crystals" -- a perfect combination of metallic studs with sparkly crystals in fall colors of gold, copper, steel, and pale olive green. Just as point of reference, the piece in the middle of the bottom row has gold colored studs surrounded by pale green crystals, with a larger pale green crystal in the middle.

I have plans to use these on a set of fall cards this year. I love that combination of colors, and it fits with my patterned paper and card stock I have picked out for that project. Very nice quality, too.

I use a lot of this type of embellishments, so I'm always looking for new ones to add to my stash. Saw these, and knew I wanted to use them on some cards I have planned.

They're by Prima -- so the quality is there.

The color in the photo is a little off. The two pieces in the top row with the large pearls in the center - those pearls are a light-medium gray. The two pieces in the third row with the large pearls in the center -- those pearls are a medium-dark gray. And the one in the lower right hand corner -- that one is dark gray. Just so you know. They are NOT white. And the dark pearls are a medium-dark brown.

However, they ARE lovely! Very classy looking, and a striking color combination. I will be using them as centers for large-ish flowers on cards. The fleur-de-lys (middle of the first and third rows) will accent sentiment banners. Really -- there are tons of uses for these. Very happy with these.

I'm a big fan of Darice embossing folders. They're usually priced well, and the quality is excellent.

I purchased this one to use on a variety of card designs -- for both male- and female-oriented cards. It just works nicely for both. The design is not frilly, but it can be "frilled up" for a more feminine look. I've stamped it on tan-colored card stock, and then brushed it lightly with warm brown distress ink -- the end result looks like woven reeds. It makes a great background for punched or die-cut flowers OR for golf or fishing themed punch outs.

I make cards in two sizes, mostly: 4.25" x 5.5", and 5" x 6.5" -- and I've used this on both sizes for layers and matting. Very nice, indeed!

I got these to use on journals that I create by making over common composition books with patterned scrapbooking papers, added pockets, tags, ribbon bookmarks, etc. I wanted something to make the cover more "special," saw these, and decided to try them.

They're SO much prettier in person. Really, the photo here does not do them justice. You get six of them -- 3 each of 2 different designs, and Amazon's price is reasonable at the time I purchased them (just over six dollars).

I would definitely purchase these again. High quality, great look -- what's not to love?

I got these to use on some travel journals I'm putting together. I use composition books, cover with some great travel-themed paper, and attach these charms either directly on the cover, or to ribbon bookmarks.

They're the perfect size for cards, too -- small, but beautifully detailed. Very nice quality. And you get a LOT of them in a package.

Very happy with these.

I make hand-crafted greeting cards, and I'm always looking for suitable papers to use. Some 12"x12" papers have designs that can be difficult to use for cards -- the patterns are just too big. This Jubilee paper is PERFECT!

The overall look is sort of distressed "Bohemian chic" -- great color combinations that include peacock blue, deep violet, rose/pink/maroon, oranges ranging from rusty to orange sherbet. The overlaying patterns include florals, brocades, dots, butterflies, lace, and script. I combine this with solid card stock in coordinating colors, and the results are stunning.

Of course, you're getting K&Co quality. The paper is heavy-weight -- not quite as heavy as good card stock, but much heavier than a lot of patterned paper from other companies. You get three sheets of each of the 12 double-sided pattern combos. It works great in a die cutter, and I've also used it in embossing folders. One of my favorite treatments is to stamp with Versamark, and heat emboss with white or metallic embossing powder.

Very pleased with this -- definitely will be using it for lots of cards, and probably a couple of altered composition book journals. (Photo shows one of the orange/yellow pattern sheets used in the "quilt" part of the design)

I purchased this stamp because I fell in love with the sentiment. I'm a singer, and I have a lot of friends who are singers, so this will be perfect for creating cards for them.

Like all my other Inkadinkado stamps, this one is high quality, easy to use and clean. It's a large image: about 3 3/4" x almost 5" (the measurements given in the description are for the PACKAGE, not the stamp itself), and nearly covers the front of an A2 card. I may end up using it more on my larger cards (5" x 6.5"), just to have a little more room for matting, ribbons, trims, etc.

Definitely recommend this, especially if you have musicians in your life ...

What a wonderfully eclectic set of designs! I make a lot of greeting cards, and like to bulk up my stash a couple of times a year. This was a recent purchase, selected for its variety of bright saturated colors.

The designs run the gamut from sedate stripes to mounted heads, from florals to feathers, from woodgrain to geometric designs, and everything in between. The colors include cobalt blue, some lighter shades of cobalt, deep pink and magenta, orange, reddish-purple, greens, even some wood tones. There are lots of possibilities here for lots of different themes: masculine, feminine, juvenile, fancy, plain, modern, etc. I'm not in love with ALL of the designs, but I'd say most of them will be used at some point.

Pages are double sided -- you get 24 sheets, which is a nice distribution. The quality is typical of Carta Bella -- 65# sturdy card stock.

Very happy with this purchase.

I have a LOT of Spellbinders dies in my collection, and they are usually very high quality. These set is no different.

The selection of sizes works well for card makers. The largest die, the 10-point one, is about 4" in diameter, point to point. The smallest, the little 12-point flower, is about 2". I've cut these using everything from heavier foiled card stock to quality Stampin' Up! card stock to 85lb cover stock to nice-quality designer paper, with no problems. As always, you may need to use a shim or two (extra pieces of card stock), as is the case with most wafer-thin dies. I use a steel shim with anything that has a lot of detail (Cheery Lynn, for example, or Tattered Lace brand dies).

I use these quite often to back circle-shaped focals with flowers or sentiments -- love that effect.

Anyway, these are good quality dies, and produce nice dependable results. Highly recommended.

I purchased these to use with the Graphic 45 "Secret Garden" papers for hand-crafted greeting cards, but they'll work with a LOT of different floral and garden patterned papers.

The quality is excellent, and they cling nicely to my acrylic blocks for stamping. I've used them with Stazon ink and Spectrum Noir alcohol markers (to color the designs), and also with Versamark and heat-embossed in metallic embossing powders. The "border" design is a real favorite of mine, and I've used it on a series of cards for my gardening friends.

I'm a huge fan of Graphic 45 papers and embellishments.  This tag mini-album is another in a great line of basics they offer to use with their wonderful designs.  I made up one of these as a "thank you" for a friend -- turned out great!  Price is decent, too.  Will definitely purchase again for similar projects.

What a nice set of brushes! I was offered a sample to test, and I have to say I'm impressed. I work mostly in watercolors, creating small "focals" for hand-crafted greeting cards, and these have been a nice addition to my arsenal of brushes.

You get a good assortment of sizes. I've used the larger ones to create a "wash" of colors blended to form a background, and the smaller ones for my detail work. No problems with cleaning, either. Frankly, these perform nearly as good as some much more expensive natural hair brushes I already owned (I received a small set of Silver Brush Watercolor brushes as a gift a few years ago -- red sable).

The case is a nice touch, too -- well made, sturdy, and with a "stand-up" feature that makes retrieving the right brush easy.

Very happy with these. I may purchase a set for a nephew that is a much more accomplished artist than I am.

I make a lot of hand crafted greeting cards, and use a LOT of ribbon, lace, etc. I've purchased a number of May Arts ribbons, and the quality is always high.

This one is no different. It's a lovely iridescent pink and green soft ribbon that's easy to work with -- it makes into nice bows easily, and the color is subtle enough to go with pinks, mauves, sage, avocado -- generally any soft pink or soft green.

Definitely happy with this purchase!

These coordinate with the French Country papers, stickers, and chipboard pieces.  The designs are perfect, and you actually get two different sheets (only one is shown above), so there are quite a few options.  I've used these for my cards and altered composition books (journals).  Definitely happy with these!

I purchased these to go with the Graphic 45 Secret Garden papers and chipboard pieces. They coordinate perfectly, and add so much to my card and journal projects.

You actually get 2 sheets (only one is shown above), each with different designs. Lots of possibilities for mixing and matching. Very happy with this purchase.

I love the variety of shapes and sizes of tags and pockets. They coordinate perfectly with the Typography papers, and add a little special touch to my hand-crafted greeting cards.

My only quibble is that I'm not sure they're a great value -- you don't get a huge number of them. I'd feel more enthusiastic about them if the price was a little lower.

Still, for my purposes, they worked nicely, and I don't regret my purchase at all.

I'm a HUGE fan of Graphic 45 designs and products. I make a lot of hand crafted greeting cards, and I've used several of their design suites recently (French Country, Secret Garden, and Bohemian Bazaar). The quality is always first rate, and the designs are so detailed and easy to work with.

This new pattern set, Typography, is no exception. The artwork is somewhat reminiscent of Toulouse Latrec -- those old French posters depicting ladies of the evening. The colors are bright blues, reds, muted yellows, greens and black.

Finding solid card stock to match is no problem. The different patterns combine easily. I really like working with the 8" by 8" paper -- it's large enough for my 5" x 6.5" cards, and also works well on my smaller A2 cards.

Very happy with this -- highly recommended!

I purchased this to use for my greeting cards, and for the most part, it works well. It IS tricky, though, especially on the 1/2" side. If the paper or card stock isn't lined up EXACTLY right in the plastic guide, you'll get a " nick" on one side. It took me awhile to figure out how to get it set correctly, and I still get it a bit "off" once in awhile.

But it's heavy duty, and it looks like it could punch through anything. It breezes through heavy card stock like a hot knife through butter. Everything looks sturdy, even the plastic guides that flip out to hold the paper.

Reasonably happy with this despite the challenges.

Don't get me wrong -- this is a quality item, producing beautifully punched borders in paper and light-weight card stock. But it's HUGE -- bigger than my Stampin' Up! paper trimmer -- and the lever stays up, making storage difficult.

I tried it with several weights of paper and card stock, and found it works best on lighter-weight papers. My heavy card stock was extremely difficult to punch and had a tendency to hang up in the punch. Many smaller border punches have similar problems, but I would have thought that one this size with this much "horsepower" could handle heavier card a little better.

Also, the punch cartridges aren't cheap -- $20 or so each -- so bear that in mind. The one that comes with the unit is wide (2.5), as are most of the full-sized cartridges. This set comes with an adapter so you can use narrower Fiskars Interchangeable Border Punches as well, like this one: [[ASIN:B005NAGWM2 Fiskars 101260-1001 Interchangeable Border Punch, Ivy League]].

I really wanted to LOVE this, but it's reminiscent of their die cutting machine: big, clunky, and the proprietary expansion bits are expensive. Still, if you want a flawlessly performing border punch that will likely last a lifetime, this is for you.

I have several different poinsettia dies from different manufacturers, and this Spellbinders set is by far the most realistic looking. I've used this set at least a dozen times this year for my holiday cards, and the results are always wonderful.

I use them with a Sizzix Big Shot cutting machine, and I use the Spellbinders embossing pads [[ASIN:B001G60VDS Spellbinders W-023 Enlarged Embossing Pads for Wizard Cutting and Embossing System]] in a second pass to get the embossed detail you see in the picture above. I cut the petals, then without taking the cut paper out of the die, I run it through the machine a second time with the Spellbinders embossing pads added to the sandwich. Works not only for this die set, but for any of the Spellbinders dies that include embossable detail.

I like that I can layer as many layers as I need to get the size I want. I don't usually use the centers included in this set, however; I prefer to use Stickles (glitter glue) [[ASIN:B0077D1L24 Ranger ISG-33745 Ice Stickles Glitter Glue, 1-Ounce, Gold]].

Very happy with this set -- highly recommended!

I used this stamp this year for several fall/Thanksgiving cards. It worked great with several different techniques: 1) stamping it in espresso/dk. brown ink, and coloring with Prismacolor pencils; 2) stamping with Versamark and using copper metallic heat embossing powder; and 3) stamping with Versamark, using "sticky" heat embossing powder, and then applying copper leaf.

Penny Black stamps are always high quality, very detailed, and they cling nicely to my acrylic blocks. They're easy to clean, too, with a good stamp cleaner. I own quite a few of this brand, and I'm really happy with all of them.

I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards, and use washi tape in a lot of them.

This set of three rolls is nice. The name says "Plaid," but they're actually gingham check. I've been using the red and green recently for some of my holiday cards. It's easy to work with, tears easily, stays put, and there's a lot of it on a roll.

I paid just under six dollars for this, but the price does fluctuate depending on who is the supplier (different suppliers set different prices, even if shipped by Amazon). Just keep an eye on it, and you can get a great deal.

Very happy with this!

I'm quickly becoming a HUGE fan of the Spellbinders D-Lites line of dies. They're smaller, less expensive, perfect for use on greeting cards, and the designs lend themselves to a lot of variation.

This one is no different: Medallion 4 is an 8-pointed "star." You get 2 dies: an outline that by itself cuts a solid "background" piece, and a patterned insert that cuts a lacy design. You can use the lacy die by itself to cut a lacy pattern in a card front, or use it in conjunction with the outline die to cut a lacy medallion.

My favorite way of using this is to first cut a background piece using the outline die in a solid color; then cut a lacy medallion "overlay" in a glitter or foil paper to mount on top of the solid piece. Another favorite use: cut the background in a multi-colored print, then cut the lacy medallion in black to mount over the colored piece, giving the effect of stained glass.

Spellbinders continues to introduce new designs in their D-Lites series, and I'm finding uses for a lot of them. Well done!

I purchased the Vintage Snowflake to use on some holiday cards this year, and I'm quite happy with it so far.

It punches nicely through fairly heavy glitter card stock, foil card stock, lighter pearlescent paper, and heavier designer papers. The punched snowflake is large -- about 2.5" across. Backed with a scalloped circle, it makes a nice focal image for a card.

Quality is at the usual EK Tools standard: very well made, "smart" design (the punch locks closed for ease of storage). I store all my punches in a shoe rack ([[ASIN:B001F51AHQ Honey-Can-Do SFT-01249 Over The Door Clear Shoe Organizer/Storage Rack, Black]], and the design of this one makes it very easy to see the design without removing the punch from the holder.

I have a LOT of EK Tools punches, and I'm really happy to add this one to my collection.

I've had some problems with SOME Martha Stewart punches: some work just fine and are easy to punch even with good-quality card stock; others are VERY difficult to punch, even on lighter designer paper. This one is somewhere in the middle -- it works OK, but it does take some "oomph" to get it to cut through anything heavier than designer paper.

I've used it with lightweight glitter paper, light card stock, and designer paper. I'm happy with the result -- it cuts cleanly, which is important given the design of this snowflake -- just wish it punched a little easier.

Still, a solid 4 stars. I'm using this for a lot of holiday cards as the size is perfect for my use.

I just love these D-Lites dies from Spellbinders! They're great for adding a small decorative touch to my cards. I already own several of the "medallion"-type D-Lites, and apart from a couple of them being a bit "fussy" to work with, they're really a nice product.

This one works great in my Sizzix. I usually add a piece of card stock under my bottom plate as a shim, and this die works well with that little extra. The larger corner is about 2.5" x 2.5" (the longest edge is about 3.5" or so). The smaller corner is 1" x 1" (longest edge is about 1.5"). The sizes work well with most card sizes (I usually do 4.25" x 5.25" or 5" x 6.5").

So far, I've used these with heavy card stock, glitter paper, designer paper, and adhesive sheets, and they've worked just fine with all.

Very happy with these! Highly recommended -- but just be aware that you MAY need to "shim" your sandwich to get a clean cut.

I use a LOT of Spellbinder dies in my card making, and usually don't have much trouble with them. I have a Sizzix Big Shot machine, and have found that using a piece or two of card stock as a shim usually works. I also have a metal shim designed specifically for use with intricate dies.

That said, I had more trouble with this die than with any of my other Spellbinders. Even with the metal shim, I still couldn't get it to cut cleanly through medium-weight card stock. I ended up having to use my X-acto knife to remove a few of the "bits" that just didn't cut through. It was almost as if one specific section of the die cutting edge was a slight bit "shorter" than the rest. I'm keeping it, and I'll still use it, and continue to fuss with it to see if I can figure out some combination of metal shim + additional card stock shims that will help it to cut completely.

All that aside, however, it is a VERY nice design, and one that I'm using for several holiday cards this year. I cut the "solid" background piece out of colored card stock, and then use the two dies together to create a lacy overlay in silver or gold foil card stock. The effect is very nice, and it makes a great "focal" centered above a sentiment banner or label.

I'm still sold on Spellbinders, and this is the first time I've had this type of problem, so I don't know if it's the particular design of this one, or if I just got one with a manufacturing problem. Just be aware that you may need to fiddle a bit with shims/etc.

... why would anyone want the disposable version of this when the refillable version costs only a couple of dollars more? OK, the refillable version is 2.5" as opposed to 3", but I don't think .5" makes a whole lot of difference in the grand scheme of things.

I was able to make adhesive stickers for my cards with little trouble with this unit. The serrated cutter is plastic and makes cutting a tad difficult -- it's nearly impossible to get a "straight" cut with it -- but if you're careful, you can reduce waste. So it does what it says it will do, and is comparable to other Xyron sticker makers.

I own the refillable Xyron 2.5" [[ASIN:B000E147WW Xyron XRN250-CFT Create-a-Sticker 2.5-inch Sticker Maker with Permanent-Adhesive Cartridge]], and find it works for almost all the kinds of stickers I need to make.

If that extra .5" is critical, you might find this item works perfectly, especially if you don't need to make too many stickers. But if you need to make a lot of stickers, one of the refillable Xyron machines might be a better fit.

Punches can be so tricky sometimes! I make a LOT of handcrafted greeting cards, and use a LOT of punches of various types. I tend to reuse the ones that punch the easiest -- and this one is getting a lot of use.

This one works well, and is fairly easy to punch. I've used it with heavy specialty paper (glitter card stock, for example), and much lighter patterned paper, and it works equally well for both -- the heavier paper takes a bit more pressure, of course, but it's not impossible.

THe square it punches is almost exactly 2" x 2" measuring the widest part of the flourish.

The bottom of this punch is open, so you can clearly see what you're punching, especially if you're trying to center a stamped sentiment or image.

Bottom line: good quality, cleanly cuts a variety of papers with reasonable effort.

I've tried to use this punch with everything from light card stock (maybe 80#) all the way to light designer paper (about the same weight as typewriter/copy paper -- around 24#), and it's nearly impossible to get a clean cut. It usually takes 2-3 tries before I can get a usable medallion.

I rarely have this much trouble with a punch -- and perhaps I got a "dud" -- but I'm not fond of wasting so much good paper trying to get a single usable piece for a card. What usually happens is the interior "bits" get punched, but not the outline unless I almost stand on the punch. Then getting the medallion out of the punch is tricky, since it's not designed to "fall through" the bottom. The problem isn't the "sharpness" -- it's plenty sharp; it's the force needed to punch all the way through.

I'm considering returning this one.

ETA: I did get one card made with it (see photo), but for every "successful" scalloped circle you see, there are probably 3-4 unsuccessful tries. I decided to keep the punch, but I'm still not happy with how it performs. It takes a lot of strength to get it to punch the outer circle correctly, and it still catches and tears quite often when I try to remove the paper from the punch.

Got this to go with my [[ASIN:B00AZGJ4SS Graphic 45 French Country Paper Pad, 12 by 12-Inch]] papers. I make handcrafted greeting cards, and LOVE this paper, so I wanted to find a ribbon that would go well with the "French Country" theme.

This works beautifully! The width is perfect for the bows I like to make to trim my cards, and it's supple enough to tie easily. You get quite a lot for the price, too, so it's a good value.

Very happy with this purchase.

I use these for trimming multiple images needed for 3-D decoupage (layering pieces of images with foam tape to indicate depth and dimension).

They're very good for this use -- nice sharp points, dark color is easy to see, too. My only "quibble" is the size of the finger holes -- it would help if they were a bit larger.

Apart from that, though, these are very good scissors, worth the extra cost over cheaper models.

I've ended up using this set of stamps and dies more than I thought I would when I first purchased it.

The chandelier stamps are lovely -- I've been stamping them on dark card stock and then heat embossing with metallics. Cut out with the included dies, and mount on white or light colored card. Gorgeous! I've used the chairs stamps with various colors of ink for regular stamping on card stock, but also with heat-embossed neon colors. The possibilities are endless!

I love it that there's a die for every stamp, too. These work for so many occasions: birthday, "hello," "thinking of you," etc., and they can be combined with other stamps and dies to create "scenes" (I used the smaller chandelier for a Thanksgiving "dinner" scene on one of my cards). The dies cut cleanly (I have a Sizzix Big Shot) with no problem

Very nice, indeed!

I've had the larger version of this color wheel for several years, but wanted one that would be easier to tuck into my crafts bag.

This one is perfect.

I use it not so much for color mixing, but rather for color coordination. I make handcrafted greeting cards, and use the wheel to get ideas for combining colors based on the "harmonious color schemes" outlined on the wheel (split complementary, triad, tetrad) as well as monochromatic schemes.

The size works great, too. I can take it along to classes, crafts days with friends, etc.

I'll definitely get a lot of use from this.

I seem to be making a lot of cards these days from papers that feature brown, either as the predominant color or as an accent. (Is brown the new black as a neutral?) Anyway, I had ivory ribbon, cream ribbon, but no brown.

I fixed that! Ordered the 50-yr spool of this ribbon. It's a nice warm brown -- not "espresso," but not "latte" either. It works with most of my papers, and because it's double face, making bows is no problem.

I'm always happy with Offray quality, and this is no exception. Very happy with this!

This paper is gorgeous! Obviously inspired by the children's book, "The Secret Garden," it's full of lovely English garden flowers: soft peachy-pink roses, yellow and white daisies, pink and yellow tulips, violets, blue clematis, and, of course, the key to the garden gate. The colors are soft pastels, but with the vibrancy of springtime flowers.

I make handcrafted greeting cards, and these papers are perfect for that. The prints are small enough that they don't get "lost" in the smaller format. I had no trouble at all finding coordinating solid color card stock in my stash (various manufacturers), as well as ribbons and buttons that also work.

Graphic 45 papers are high quality, heavyweight, and easy to cut and manipulate. They also have a series of coordinating stickers, chipboard elements, alphabet stickers and clear stamps to go with this set.

Very happy with this! I purchased the entire set of additional elements, and intend to make several more sets of coordinated cards as gifts for friends.

I absolutely LOVE this paper. I make handcrafted greeting cards, and this paper is my new favorite. The colors are deep and rich: cadet blue, brick red, sunflower gold, creams/tans, black, and soft green. The patterns exemplify the whole "French country" vibe -- harlequin, sunny stripes, blue toile, roosters, bright sunflowers, many on black backgrounds that allow the coors to pop.

Graphic 45 is high quality paper, with nice hefty weight. I'm seriously considering purchasing several pads of this, just to have it on hand. It makes beautiful cards and is so easy to work with. You can also get coordinating stickers, chipboard elements, alphabets, and 3-D decoupage (layered flowers). They've done a great job with this set -- really first rate.

I had no trouble finding solid card stock in my stash to match/coordinate. I also have a lot of cream ribbon, hemp cord, and buttons that work well. Very happy with this!

I'm new to heat embossing with embossing powder on cards, and this is my first heat gun, so I can't really compare it to any other brand. So far, I'm very happy with this!

It doesn't take it any time, really, to heat up to "full strength" -- just a couple of seconds. This sucker gets HOT, so if you're new to this, it might take a bit of practice to figure out how to hold the paper up while attacking it with the heat gun. You absolutely do NOT want to leave the paper on the table while running the heat gun over it -- no, I didn't make that mistake, but I have a friend who did, and she ended up having to get her table refinished.

It really heats evenly, and does a great job of melting the embossing powder quickly. It has a little wire "rest" so that you can put it down on the table without the hot bits touching the surface.

One little warning: when you use this the first few times, it WILL produce some smoke, and it may look like a lot. But just give it time, and that will dissipate. It took mine 3 "sessions" to get rid of all the smoke, and it's fine now. I've made 6 cards of different designs with it, and by card #4, the smoke was gone. No further problems with it.

Mine is still relatively new, so I can't really speak to its durability. If I have a problem with it, I'll edit this review to reflect that.

I'm new to heat embossing, so I wanted to try out some different colors and types of powders without investing too much in "full size" jars of the stuff. I ended up purchasing a couple of samplers containing small amounts of a bunch of different colors, and this was one of the samplers I purchased.

I'm VERY happy with it. It's pretty clear from the color of each small jar what the color is - green usually means green, blue means blue, purple purple, etc. To help manage the powder as I worked with it, I took a tip from a friend who has been doing this a long time, and emptied the jar into a small disposable plastic container. I then used small plastic spoons, one for each color, to get the powder onto the stamped image. When I was done, I just used a small piece of scrap card stock rolled and taped into a "funnel" to get the powder back into the jar. No mess, no fuss.

My first project was a stamped peacock feather, and I used 4 different colors from this powder set. It turned out BEAUTIFUL! It doesn't take much to cover the image, and this powder is good quality, so it adheres evenly to the Versamark clear ink. It melts nicely into really gorgeous metallic and pearl raised colors.

Very happy with this.

I make a LOT of handcrafted greeting cards, and love trying out different color combinations and designs to make each one unique.

But there are times when I just don't have the inspiration, and that's where a book like this really comes in handy.

You get a huge assortment of card layouts -- sort of like "blueprints" -- showing different shapes and layers you can use to create your own interpretation. For each layout, the book shows you 3-5 examples of different ways to interpret the design.

I love flipping through this book, randomly stopping on a layout, and then using it to create a new card. You get layouts for "shaped" cards (round, star, etc.), for square cards, and for both vertically- and horizontally-oriented rectangular cards. Really, there's something for everyone here.

For each of the examples, you're given the "recipe": which brand of papers, trims, stamps, ink, etc. were used to produce it. Very helpful if you want to try to duplicate one of the cards.

The only thing that would make this book better would be if it were spiral bound. I may yet take it to my local copying store and have that done. I'm using it that much.

This is a really good investment if you're into card making. Highly recommended.

If you're into "shabby chic," these folders are for you.

They both produce worn-looking textured patterns; one, a "dots" design, and the other a "woven wire" pattern.

I've used these to texture printed designs to give them an older "antiqued" look, and also used them as backdrops for a layered card design, lightly sanded to bring out the texture.

My embossing machine is a Sizzix, so these work just fine, but they should work perfectly OK in any die-cutting/embossing machine that can handle the standard plastic embossing folder.

This is a nice set of two embossing folders. One is almost like a chicken wire design, but more "graceful"; the other is a simple basket-weave pattern.

The best thing about these is that they "play well" with both more "formal" as well as more casual layouts and card designs. The "wire" one works nicely as the backdrop for a wedding or anniversary card, but can also work great for a birthday card. I love the basket-weave texture for Thanksgiving or other fall-themed cards, but it would also work for Easter or Mother's Day.

The Lifestyle Crafts embossing folders are consistently high quality, producing nice crisp images. I use these with a Sizzix Big Shot, and have no problems at all (just follow the directions on your die-cutting/embossing machine).

It's so rare to find card-making supplies that work for both masculine and feminine cards. This is one of those items.

This houndstooth folder is perfect. It's typical Cuttlebug quality, and gives a crisp clean embossed pattern. I use it with my Sizzix Big Shot with no problems at all.

Definitely glad I invested in this -- it's getting a lot of use!

I make a LOT of handcrafted greeting cards, and I use a LOT of embossing folders to add texture and depth. Cuttlebug makes some of the best embossing folders in terms of quality. They always work beautifully in my Sizzix Big Shot machine, making nice crisp impressions.

This particular pattern, Herringbone, is fantastic for masculine as well as feminine cards. So many of the embossing folders have more feminine patterns -- lace, damask, flowers, etc. -- so I'm always looking for textures that will work well for my masculine cards. This one's a winner, for sure.

I love the look that embossing folders give to plain cardstock -- the texture, the designs.

This is a set of 4 packages each containing 2 different embossing folders, so 8 in all -- a fantastic deal, really. Most of these are "textures": brick, woodgrain, dots, etc., the rest are "images" -- bingo card, letters/numbers, etc. There are enough different designs here to accommodate almost any style of card: formal, "fancy," masculine, children, or whatever. And the Sizzix quality is first-rate. These emboss cleanly, with nice crisp images.

Very nice set!

I'm a real fan of these stamp and die combo packs from Sizzix. They make personalizing my hand-crafted greeting cards a breeze!

This particular set includes three bicycle stamps, two of which have corresponding dies; a small arrow stamp "on the go" and a "blank" pennant outline stamp, both of which have corresponding dies, and three additional sentiment stamps. There's also one more larger arrow die that could be used with any of the sentiment stamps.

A lot of the "new" paper packs include a few papers with a bicycle theme, so these coordinate nicely with those.

These are easy to store -- I just put the entire set in an empty clear plastic DVD case.

Very happy with this set!

I LOVE this Asiana craft ribbon by Offray. I have this same ribbon in a variety of colors, as it's perfect for my card making. This color is actually a darker wine color, but it's sheer, so it appears more "pink" in the photo here. Just bear in mind that it IS darker on the spool and when tied into a bow.

That said, it is easy to tie into bows, and the width is perfect. Very nice, indeed.

I do a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards for fundraisers for various charities, and I'm always looking for something a little "different."

I love this stamp set! Most of my cards are of the "thinking of you" variety, as it works for so many occasions (sympathy, get well, "hello," etc.). The flowers in this set are nice size, and the two individual flower "heads" layer beautifully with a tab of foam tape in between for a dimensional effect.

I use Gelatos to color the stamps, mist lightly with water, and then stamp, giving a slightly watercolor effect. I line up the die and secure with a bit of washi tape (or painters tape) before running through my Sizzix Big Shot, and get good results every time.

Very happy with these! They're already getting a good workout.

I got this to make file tabs for my 6"x6" paper pads -- I make hand crafted greeting cards, and use a lot of different designer paper, so I have a LOT of paper pads.

I turn the paper pads upside down so that the "binding" is on the bottom, then punch a tab from one of the papers, and attach it to the cover using the enclosed tab stickers. (see photo in customer photos ... )

NOTE: you get only a few of these stickers with the punch; if you have a large labeling project, you'll need to purchase more of the stickers, but they're worth it.

I've already labeled most of my collection of papers, and will be creating index tabs for my 12"x12" papers soon. Very happy with this system!

I wanted a small "eyelet" border punch, and this one looked perfect online. Ordered it -- and I couldn't be happier.

It makes a nice narrow scalloped border with a little hole in each scallop -- much like delicate eyelet lace. It's very easy to use and line up along the edge of a card or focal. It folds flat, too, with a little button that holds the top in the "down" position so it stores more easily.

Very happy with this! I'm already using it more than I thought I would.

I use a LOT of dies in my card making, and a lot of them are Spellbinders. They are high quality, and always produce excellent results.

This die set is no exception. The design is lovely -- the rectangle frame fits exactly on an A2 card (4.25" x 5.5"). You can use it with or without the oval cutting die (used with the oval, it produces a frame with an oval opening; without the oval die, you get a rectangular shaped lacy "doily" with a solid "middle").

The set also includes an oval label, a large-ish lacy corner piece, and an accent piece that could be used by itself or in multiples to make a "chain." So far, I've used the frame (without the oval cutout), the oval in a contrasting color layered on top of the rectangular frame, and the label. They cut nicely -- for these detailed dies, I use a piece of waxed paper between the die and the card stock (I use these: [[ASIN:B005G4L5D6 Norpro 3403 Square Wax Papers, 250-Piece]] only because they're already pre-cut to the right size for my die cutting machine).

I have a project coming up to make some cards for a fund raiser for a local charity, and I'll definitely be using this die set for that. Very happy with this purchase!

Wow! Finally a corner punch that will cut heavy (110#) card stock easily! I'm getting up there in years, and I've developed arthritis in my hands. Some punches require a LOT of horsepower to punch even fairly light card stock (65# or so).

This one punches easily by simply squeezing the levers. There are gears inside that make it move easily without a lot of pressure. It cuts cleanly, and it's designed so that you can see precisely where you're punching.

I love this delicate design, too. It works with so many card styles, from "frilly/feminine" to juvenile birthday to sympathy.

Very happy with this purchase!

I've been trying different solutions for die storage, and thought I'd landed on one that worked, but it started to become unwieldy. Decided to give these a try after reading rave reviews.

I purchased the case [[ASIN:B007Q41MO6 Spellbinder Magnetic Die Case]], and started filling up the sheets. Each one holds a LOT of dies -- four sets of Spellbinders Nestabilities circles ([[ASIN:B001DHCLUG Spellbinders Wizard Nestabilities Dies, Classic and Standard Circles, Set of 4]], for example, fit on two sheets with some room to spare.

I'm organizing mine by category -- shapes (like ovals, circles, pennants, banners, etc.), flowers/plants/leaves, labels, flourishes & borders, and letters/words. I have 3 cases now, and I've labeled each case with the category(s) it contains. I've also attached a small label to top edge of each sheet so I can quickly find specific dies that I use a lot.

I'm really happy with these so far. I have noticed that SOME (not all) of these sheets have a tendency to curl. When that happens, I flatten them out using two heavy cookie sheets and some books like a sort of "press": a couple of books on the bottom, then cookie sheet, then magnetic sheet, then the other cookie sheet, and finally a stack of books on top. Leave it for about 3 days, and it comes out flat. So far, so good!

I love that I now have everything organized, and these fit nicely on my crafts room shelves without taking up a huge amount of space (my old method of organizing took up a LOT of valuable crafts shelving real estate).

Very happy!

I have dies from a variety of companies: Spellbinders, Die-Namics, Memory Box, Cherry Lynn, Sizzix, etc. I had been storing them all in a large "bin," trying to keep them stuck to their original packaging, but that was becoming a problem

I had tried to devise my own "system" -- but couldn't find the right size cards/etc. to use for the backing.

Finally found these, and decided to give them a try.

So far, they're working reasonably well. Almost all my dies are smaller: no larger than what would fit on an A2 (4.25" x 5.5") card, so I haven't run into any issues with larger dies since I don't own any. I can store two sets of Spellbinders Nestabilities on each sheet, but I do have to be careful when "flipping" through the sheets; otherwise, one or more of the larger ones will pop loose.

Also, one brand of dies that I use uses clear plastic on some of their dies (particularly the "banner" dies) so you can see through them to line up your die with a stamped sentiment or whatever, and this plastic interferes with the "stick-ability" from a magent standpoint. I may just remove the plastic, since it really serves no useful purpose.

Some have mentioned the "curling" -- and I've found this in several of the magnetic sheets, but not all of them. Not sure what causes it, but for now, I'm using those for my smaller dies, and using the really flat sheets for my larger dies. Also, I've had some luck putting a "curled" sheet between two heavy-weight cookie sheets, placing a few books under the stack and then piling a bunch of books on the top sheet, and leaving it there for a few days (the "sandwich" from the bottom up would be: couple of books, cookie sheet, magnetic sheet, cookie sheet, bunch of books) -- really seems to flatten it out.

Anyway, this is my "storage choice" for metal dies for now, until/unless something better comes along. I've been able to find the refill pages cheaper elsewhere, but Amazon's price on the case is the best I've found so far.

I got this to replace another corner punch that never did work very well.

So far, this one is working OK. It cuts cleanly -- you do have to be careful to get the cardstock or paper lined up properly. t was VERY "stiff" and hard to punch at first, but after a few uses, it loosened up a bit. It's still a little grumpy when it comes to heavier card stock (100-110# or so), but not nearly as bad as it was at first.

It would be nice if there were a cover over the bottom so the trimmed bits wouldn't end up all over the craft table.

Still, overall it's a nice corner punch, relatively inexpensive, too.

I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards, and I needed something to tie on the buttons I sometimes use for embellishment. White twine is fairly easy to find, but I wanted some other colors.

This set is perfect for my use. The size (1 mm) is perfect for the buttons, and this is easy to tie. The colors are lovely icy pastels and close to what you see in the image above.

Very happy with this.

I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards, and I needed something to tie on the buttons I sometimes use for embellishment. White twine is fairly easy to find, but I wanted some other colors.

This set is perfect for my use. The size (1 mm) is perfect for the buttons, and this is easy to tie. The colors are vibrant and close to what you see in the image above.

Very happy with this.

I make a lot of handcrafted greeting cards, and just got into die cutting last year. I find I use Spellbinders dies more than any others, simply because of their attention to detail and great designs.

This set is no different. It's one of my favorite tag sets. There's a nice variety of sizes and shapes -- useful for just about any "small" sentiment stamp. I usually stamp my sentiment on white or cream cardstock, then cut it with the "center" die and shade the edges with distress ink. Then I cut the "frame" from a contrasting color that coordinates with my focal and/or designer patterned paper. Mount the center on the frame using a foam square to give it a 3-D effect. Perfect!

I use these with my Sizzix Big Shot machine, and they work perfectly. No problems at all.

I purchased this set to use for my Easter cards this year, along with the matching cutting die. JustRite stamps are consistently high quality, and this set is no exception. There's a nice assortment of greetings/sentiments, a nice frame image, plus two floral images. I've used the frame image with other sentiment stamps, and a couple of the sentiments will be useful for other holidays/events.

Price is decent, too. A very nice set for the money.

I have it in a bunch of different colors that I use for making handcrafted greeting cards. It's a lovely sheer ribbon, perfect for making bows.

Be aware, however, that the color of this one is lighter than the photo above. It's more of a light beige, rather than the medium beige shown here. It's fine for my uses -- I used it for my "neutrals"-themed cards (chocolate browns, cream, etc.), so it fits right in.

You get a LOT of this stuff for the price, and it's the typical Offray quality. Very nice ribbon.

I got this to use with my springtime cards this year. I found myself using a lot of patterned papers with pink and orange designs, flowers, etc. -- that combination seems to be really popular this spring -- and this ribbon is working quite well with all of them.

The overall effect is sort of iridescent. The picture is pretty close to reality -- it's pink on one side, orange on the other -- which works nicely for making bows for my cards.

You get a nice quantity for the price, too. I'm very happy with this purchase.

This is OK -- it's exactly the size/shape I needed, but it's VERY difficult to punch if you're using anything heavier than thin paper. The problem is that it's quite small, and the punch mechanism doesn't give you much "leverage." I've used it with 65# cardstock (not very heavy), and it's really hard to punch. It's not a question of sharpness -- mine is plenty sharp -- it's just the size/design that makes it difficult to punch through cardstock.

Still, if you're looking for a small stamen punch to use with your flower/daisy punches, this is probably the one you're looking for. Just know going in that using it will not be comfortable.

I make a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards and use a lot of ribbon and lace trims. I got this to use with some of my "summery" papers. It's just the right size to use as an accent on my cards, and the colors are bright and lively.

Very nice quality, price for the quantity is good, too. Very happy with this.

Nice assortment of good-quality buttons in colors I use a lot in my card making.

I use these mostly as flower centers or just simple accents on my cards. You get quite a few different sizes, but none are too big (I think the largest is maybe 5/8" or so; most are 1/4"-3/8"-1/2").

I use a lot of yellow, brown, and pink, so the colors are perfect for me. Price is good, too. Nice value.

... and you get 3 yards of each! These are not heavy-weight laces -- very delicate. Each one is a different width, which provides a nice selection for cards. These are "flat" laces -- not gathered -- so they lay flat against my cards. A couple of them are what I'd call "edging" laces: they have a solid "border" along one edge; the others have lace borders on both edges. This provides you with some options as to placement, either by themselves or with other ribbons or trims.

I'm very happy with this purchase -- a nice variety for a decent price.

I purchased this to use as trim on my handcrafted greeting cards. I wasn't expecting much due to the price, but figured it would be good enough to layer on my cards with other bits of paper and ribbon.

Boy, was I pleasantly surprised! The quality is really nice, ESPECIALLY for the price. You're getting 15 yds of this lace ribbon. I make a lot of cards with neutral colors -- tans, browns, ivory, cream -- and the color will be *perfect* for those. It will be perfect to layer under a colored ribbon (rust, perhaps, or chocolate -- even cream satin for textural variety). I have so many ideas now for how to use this!

VERY pleased with the quality!

I purchased these to use in my hand-crafted greeting cards, and they are the perfect size! They're only 4" in diameter, so they fit on an A2 card without trimming. I've seen similar paper doilies offered on various craft sites in a package of 12 for more than double the price here! I also purchased these in gold and silver for use on holiday cards, as well as wedding or anniversary cards.

They're nice quality, easy to work with.

This is a really good value! Definitely happy with my purchase.

I love these 2-part labels. I usually stamp a sentiment on white or light-colored cardstock, and cut it with the "plain" label die. I then cut the ornate frame out of contrasting cardstock, and layer the sentiment on the fancy frame with foam tape so it's slightly raised. It always looks wonderful!

These work great with my Sizzix Big Shot, too. Love Spellbinders dies!

Offray makes very nice ribbon, and this one is no exception. I go through a lot of ribbon in my card making, and usually opt for Offray.

This one is a gorgeous soft ivory color that goes with a lot of my favorite designer papers. It's soft, so it's easy to work into small bows (I like to make bows about 2"-3" across)' and with the double sided satin finish, I don't need to worry about matte vs satin issues. It's wide enough to make a "statement," but narrow enough to manipulate easily.

The price for this is excellent. I've paid nearly this much for 5 yds of lesser quality ribbon at crafts stores.

These both work well for masculine cards, especially the houndstooth check one. These are A2 size folders (4.25" x 5.5"). My cards are usually a little bigger (5" x 6.5"), so I usually cover about half the card with an embossed piece of card stock, covering the rest with a piece of patterned paper.

These folders work just fine with my Sizzix Big Shot die cutter / embosser. The impression is crisp and clear. Nice addition to my embossing folder collection.

I used this punch along with its larger cousin to make poinsettias for some of my hand-crafted greeting cards this year. Punch them out of red card stock (or red printed paper), then slightly emboss with a ball-tipped stylus. Layer the smaller on the larger one, add a few yellow seed beads or a dab of yellow or gold sparkle glue, and you have a cute poinsettia.

Use the same concept with other colors, and you can create some nice dimensional flower embellishments for birthdays, anniversaries, thank you'd, etc.

I really like the EK Success punches -- I've never had and problems with any of them (and I think I have at least a dozen in different shapes/sizes).

This one does it all: die cuts, embosses, letterpresses. Easy to use right out of the box, directions are clear and easy to follow.

This is a LARGE machine, very heavy for a machine of this type, very sturdy. It has a large "footprint" when fully open, but folds up to a more manageable size, still heavy, but maneuverable if you need to move it to store.

It comes with only one die with two letterpress plates. This is enough to get you started, but you'll soon want to make more than a scallop circle. That's where the expense comes in. You can purchase additional dies (which come with a couple of letterpress plates), and additional plate sets for those dies, but the dies aren't cheap. Watch for sales, and you can save a bundle.

Also, if you already have a smaller die-cutting machine (like a Sizzix Big Shot [like me], or a Cuttlebug) and already have lots of dies from other manufacturers, you CAN use them with this machine. But you'll need to invest in a set of adapter plates, and those aren't cheap, either. The "sandwich recipes" (how to line up the various plates with the dies, listed by manufacturer) are available on the Fiskars website, and they seem to work pretty well. I had to "fiddle" with them a bit, but then I often have to "fiddle" with non-Sizzix dies on my Sizzix machine. Not a huge deal, really (a piece or two of cardstock usually does the trick ... but I digress ... )

You will NOT be able to use the Fuse system dies with your Sizzix Big Shot. I don't know about the Cuttlebug, but I suspect it would be the same problem: the Fuse dies are too thick for the Big Shot. You can't use the Sizzix platform with these extra-thick dies, and you can't cut with the Sizzix machine without the platform. So there's that.

I'm not sure how much better this would be than a comparable machine. If this is your FIRST experience with a die-cutting machine, it might be a good investment, with the understanding that you'll have to spend more money in order to open up the wonderful explosion of available much-less-expensive dies from other manufacturers. Or you'll need to spend a LOT more money to get extra Fiskars dies and plates. I think it's worth 4 stars just for the quality of the machine.

I make a lot of hand-crafted greeting cards, so I jumped at the chance to try out this set of stamps and "gears" from Inkadinkado.

The concept is simple: a shaped acrylic cling-stamp block that can be linked to the different size/shape "gears" to produce shaped repeated patterns based on the included stamps. You get 4 "gears": two oval, two circular -- that produce different size circles and ovals.

For card making, I found I used the larger circular and oval gears most often, stamping on the INSIDE of them (my cards are usually 5"x6.5"). I also used the smaller ones to create "arcs" across a card (I'll try to upload a photo or two later to show what I'm talking about ... ).

The included stamps are quite nice -- I use a lot of "flourish"-type embellishments, die-cuts, etc., so these were definitely my cup of tea. Inkadinkado offers quite a few more reasonably-priced sets designed to be used with the Stamping Gear, so I'll probably be picking up a few of those. It also works with ANY cling stamp that's small enough to fit on the block. One card I made for Christmas used a small "Noel" stamp from another set I already had on hand -- turned out great!

All in all, a very nice set.

The only negative: the packaging is awful. Others have mentioned this as well. It's impossible to remove the gears and stamps without destroying the package. All the instructions are included ON the package, so this was a really poor design. Fix that, Inkadinkado, and I would give this 5 stars.

You can never go wrong with Offray ribbon, and this gorgeous wine satin ribbon is no exception. It's the perfect size for my card making, and the burgundy-wine color is one I use a lot. I've used it in a lot of my holiday cards, along with a beautiful dark forest green. The double face satin finish makes bow making a snap -- you don't have to worry if the ribbon twists in the process since both sides look identical.

Twenty yards may seem like a lot, but the price is great, and I do use a lot of it, so it's super economical for me. You'd pay a LOT more per yard at a crafts store (I've seen similar ribbon sold on a 3 yd spool for 5 dollars!)

You can never go wrong with Offray ribbon, and this gorgeous soft yellow satin ribbon is no exception. It's the perfect size for my card making, and the pale yellow color is one I use a lot. I make a lot of cards with a "daisy" theme -- lots of yellow and white -- so I go through a LOT of yellow ribbon. The double face satin finish makes bow making a snap -- you don't have to worry if the ribbon twists in the process since both sides look identical.

Fifty yards may seem like a lot, but the price is great, and I do use a lot of it, so it's super economical for me. You'd pay a LOT more per yard at a crafts store (I've seen similar ribbon sold on a 3 yd spool for 5 dollars!)

You can never go wrong with Offray ribbon, and this gorgeous scarlet/red satin ribbon is no exception. It's the perfect size for my card making, and the red color is one I use a lot. The double face satin finish makes bow making a snap -- you don't have to worry if the ribbon twists in the process since both sides look identical.

Fifty yards may seem like a lot, but the price is great, and I do use a lot of it, so it's super economical for me. You'd pay a LOT more per yard at a crafts store (I've seen similar ribbon sold on a 3 yd spool for 5 dollars!). If you don't think you can use 50 yards, see if you can find a few friends to split the cost and ribbon. You won't find better quality anywhere else.

You can never go wrong with Offray ribbon, and this gorgeous white satin ribbon is no exception. It's the perfect size for my card making, and the white color is one I use a lot. The double face satin finish makes bow making a snap -- you don't have to worry if the ribbon twists in the process since both sides look identical.

Twenty yards may seem like a lot, but the price is great, and I do use a lot of it, so it's super economical for me. You'd pay a LOT more per yard at a crafts store (I've seen similar ribbon sold on a 3 yd spool for 5 dollars!)

Very nice collection of 65-lb cardstock. It's a nice weight -- not quite heavy enough to use as a card base (I prefer at least 85-lb, and usually use 110-lb) -- but it holds up well to embossing and die-cutitng.

You get 18 colors, 4 pages of each. The colors are good basic "primary" shades, nice intensity (no "dusty" shades). I use them as mattes for my greeting card focals. Very reasonable price for what you get, too, in terms of quality and variety.

One of my favorite "go to" embossing folders when I just need a little more texture on a card. The pattern works great with both formal designs and more informal cards (child's birthday card, for example).

I sometimes dress it up with a few tiny rhinestones or pearls at the "intersection" points. Adds a bit of bling, ya know? Looks fantastic on a wedding card or fancier adult birthday card.

I keep this one handy, for sure!

I purchased this to use in some of my holiday cards. For one, I paired it with this lighthouse stamp [[ASIN:B001683RPK Inkadinkado Rubber Stamp -Lighthouse]] to create a scene, stamping each separately on white card stock, then coloring with Prismacolor pencils. I fussy-cut this snowman, and mounted him on the lighthouse background using foam tabs so that he appears to be in front. Turned out great!

I love his quizzical expression and the staff with the little bird. A lot of snowman stamps are more juvenile; I like that this one is more ... hmm ... formal and "classy" looking.

And of course, it's the typical Inkadinkado high quality.

I'll be using this guy for years! Very happy with this purchase.

I'm absolutely over the moon with this Offray Asiana ribbon. It's sheer and delicate looking, but it makes the most beautiful bows for my hand-drafted greeting cards.

You get 25 yards of 5/8" ribbon on a spool -- definitely a bargain at this price. I've purchased this in a lot of different colors -- most recently this gorgeous red (plus two shades of green, burgundy, and cream) for my holiday cards. It's quite easy to work with -- supple, forgiving. I use a bow maker (a wooden one, with metal pins that I can adjust to make bows of various sizes), and this ribbon makes up nicely into double bows.

Very happy with this -- will probably purchase more colors!

I love the collection of colors in this assortment. I've been using it in my holiday greeting cards, and it adds a classy touch, with a hint of bling.

Someone mentioned a "cracking" problem. Some embossing folders make a deeper impression than others, and that can sometimes cause cracking. What I've found that works is to put a single layer of facial tissue (not the kind with oil -- just the plain cheap "kleenex" type) in the folder on top of the cardstock. You'll still get a good impression, but without the cracking.

Will definitely keep this around for my spring cards, too!

Got this set for my holiday cards this year -- along with several others.

These are easy to use (I have a Sizzix Big Shot), and deliver consistently good results. I've used glitter cardstock with these, metallic cardstock, pearl cardstock, and patterned cardstock. I especially like using them with striped patterned paper, and then finishing with rhinestones, ribbons, and border stickers to highlight.

Will be using these for years to come. Very nice, indeed.

I make a lot of cards, and have just started using stamps -- both acrylic/rubber, and digital. This card stock works great for both.

It takes the ink well using acrylic or rubber stamps with both water- and alcohol-based inks. And it goes through my HP printer with no problem when I print digital stamps. I then color the outlines using colored pencils, Spectrum Noir markers, or Ranger Ink with an aquapen.

At 110 lb, this is heavy enough to be great as a card base, too. And it performs well in an embossing machine (I have a Sizzix Big Shot).

Price is excellent for this quality. Will definitely purchase again, when I run out.

Obviously, it's great for kids' art projects, but there are so many other possibilities!

I make hand-crafted greeting cards, and just started working with stamps and ink -- clear acrylic stamps, wood-mounted rubber stamps, etc. And I've been trying different types of ink to see how they perform with the different stamps.

This paper is great for my practice attempts -- it's economical, there's LOTS of it, it's heavy enough that the ink doesn't bleed through, and I can see how the different combinations of ink + stamp work. Once I find what I like, I then stamp it on my cardstock, knowing how much ink and pressure is needed to get the image I want. I tear off a 24" section of it and put it on my work table, with the edge just under the edge of my cutting mat. Stamp away, then toss it when it's full of practice stamped images.

This 200' roll will likely last me at least a year. Like I said, economical, and so much cheaper than the "practice pads" sold by some of the stamp manufacturers.

I've been looking for an embossing folder that included diamonds w/ a place to add pearls or rhinestones at the "intersections." THIS folder is absolutely perfect for that.

The folder is sized for an A2 card (4.25" x 5.5") -- I wish it were bigger, as a lot of the cards I do are 5" x 6.5", but this size still allows me to emboss a good sized piece of cardstock that covers over half my card, and I can fill in the remaining space with a nice printed designer paper.

These QuicKutz folders work just fine in my Sizzix Big Shot, too, and make a nice clean impression. Very happy with my purchase.

I purchased this along with the "Medium" flower punch. The flower produced by this punch is about 5/8" in diameter. I've been using it for my cards, embossing the petals with an embossing stylus like this: [[ASIN:B000YZ413I Fiskars 56067097J Small Tipball Burnisher Embossing Stylus]]

I use it layered sometimes, too, with another flower punch: [[ASIN:B0013JNA7C EK Success Paper Shapers Small Punch, Daisy]]

Very nice and versatile size. Also, the style is quite simple, so it's suitable for both formal and more informal card themes. Very happy with this.

I got this specifically for the small snowflake punch. I use it both for my holiday/winter cards, but I also use it for my spring/summer cards.

It works great as the center for a punched flower. I punch a small (1/2"-5/8" diameter) flower in one color, then punch a snowflake in another color.
