Actually, I broke it (my fault) but you don't need it. It's a great little easel and the carry bag is perfect, especially for outdoors. Sling the strap over your shoulder and you're ready to go.

These buttery fat sticks go on smooth and rich. The colors are gorgeous and include everything you might need for portraits or landscapes because they are easy to blend by overlaying. When you apply water, the colors dissolve. You can brush them, blot them or let them run. Great for mixed media! Endless possible effects for experienced artists or adult beginners. Young children would be better off with washable crayons, but tween and teen budding Van Goghs will love them.

This handy palette is small enough to drop into your Plein air bag. It allows you to mix custom colors with acrylic or gouache and prevent them from drying out for a little longer. I wouldn't trust it for more than a week unless you are using open acrylic formulations, but if you work on a painting outdoors, this sealable container will allow you to finish the piece in the studio that night or the next day, when it usually would have dried out.
Great medium for extending acrylic paints, sealing finished paintings, or fastening down items in collage, this easy-to pour fluid works for many art applications, and for crafts too. I like it!

These oil-based pencils lay down rich, deep color. They are easy to sharpen and wonderful to use. I love them! The rainbow of colors is just astonishing. Buy them for yourself, or for an artist friend.

Dress up your bullet journal with this generous set of sturdy stencils. Use them with markers, colored pencils, ink, or paint. They clean up easily and are simple to use. They are also great for creating custom greeting cards and decorating small items.

Would make a great addition to a gift--these stencils, markers, and a blank bullet journal.
This great marker pad is optimized to limit unwanted bleed and allow you to get the most out of your markers. Great buy for the money.

Everything you need to start cutting blocks and printing them. Great for cards or repeated designs on fabric. Great assortment of cutting tools. The blocks cut easily yet hold lines and shapes for multiple prints.

These gorgeous frames are beautifully crafted. Use them for the included canvases or for wood panels. They come complete with professional D hanging rings. They are handsomely finished front back and sides. I love them.

This beautiful case holds two hundred pencils—almost all of my watercolor pencil collection. It keeps them perfectly organized and accessible. Any artist who uses colored pencils would be thrilled to have it. Would make a great gift.

I took this unit out with me Plein air painting, and I liked the slots for holding brushes in and out of the water. I put paints in the sections and they were just about right for my acrylics two bigger wells at either end would be an improvement--one for black and one for white. Not a problem, You can fill two or three wells with those colors. A bigger concern came at the end of my session when I tried to put the palette/lid back on the unit to carry my kit back to the car without getting paint all over the inside of my bag.  The lid snapped tight on one side, but not on the other. It wouldn't close. It could just be a quality control issue and the one you get might be fine. Mine is fine, I just have to allow for the fact that I have to clean out my palette at the end of a session or bring a sealable plastic bag for it.

This is an amazing deal. The watercolor brushes are high quality in a gorgeous case. Lids protect the bristles. Take them off and attach them to the ends of the handles and the brush handles become full-sized. The design is simple, elegant, and practical. The kit fits easily into a pocket or purse. Great accessory for a travel sketchbook or for Plein air watercolors on a hike. Would make a wonderful gift for an artist friend (especially one who does watercolor or ink wash.)

If you want to try watercolor but don't want to spend a whole lot of money at first, these real sable brushes are worth a shot. They have nice points and absorb water well. There is a good selection of small to medium sizes in the set.

A good starter set for children, teens, or hobbiests. They aren't bad for artists either,

This sturdy 140-pound paper has a low cold-press surface. At 11.5 x 16.5 inches, the sheets are large enough for most projects. The glue binding makes finished pieces easy to remove. The pad's cover folds all the way back for working convenience and the chipboard backing is strong without being too heavy.

Designed for mixed media, this is a great pad for experimenting with crayon, oil pastel, watercolor pencil, collage, acrylic ink, tempera, and acrylic paints.

In case you create a masterpiece, these Italian-made warm white papers are acid-free.

These nice-sized pouch-like tubes of paint come in a well-made box for gifting or storage. The thoughtful color assortment includes two tubes of white, Mars black, two pthalos (I'm a fan of both), three different yellows, two reds, ultramarine, burnt sienna, burnt umber, and for the crafters, silver. You should be able to mix almost any color with these basics. Neither heavy body nor fluid, they are comparable in consistency with most standard tube brands. The pouch tubes are easy to open, reclose, and store. The tube size is practical. You will use them up before they go bad, but not so small that you run out of paint in the middle of a painting (unless you are doing a mural or something, but I'm sure you know that. These are like the standard tubes of most familiar brands.)

This would make a great gift set for someone starting to use acrylics, but also for artists looking to restock their paint supply.

This collection of long-handled studio brushes will delight any acrylic painter. We go through brushes like crazy and this assortment suits every need at a very reasonable price. There are flat and round bristle brushes, synthetic nylon brushes in flats, fans, filberts, and a few rounds, and soft watercolor-style brushes for fluid-style detail. I'm not sure the watercolor brushes have good enough points to please the watercolor painters I know, but come on, what do you expect for this kind of deal? Oil painters, mixed media artists, crafters, and especially acrylic painters will love this set.

Easy for little hands to hold and use, they have a triangular shape which keeps them from rolling off the table.

The colors go on easily. They are beeswax crayons and not water soluble. I haven't tried it, but I wouldn't want to try to wash them off the walls (just sayin'.) I bought them to use for an (adult) art project. They make beautiful lines over acrylic on canvas.

The nice plastic case keeps them corraled between art sessions.

I paint in acrylics, so soap is a vital part of my work routine. I clean my brushes thoroughly at the end of each work session. Even then, they do wear out. This nice soap is specially formulated for artist's tools. It comes in a resealable tin so it will last longer than brush bars. I use really cheap brushes for large sections of my paintings, but when it comes to details, my favorites are more expensive and harder to find. I want to make them last as long as possible. I'm grateful for this product.

Sized to carry with you in the car, on a train, or on a plane, this beautifully-crafted book binds just the right amount of quality watercolor paper in an elegant cover. Keep a three or four-day vacation forever, give it as a gift, or just practice keeping a sketchbook journal. It's small enough to tuck into a purse or briefcase.

A great going-away present for someone special.

Here's a compact set of classic colors that will introduce beginners, children, and teens. The colors are rich and the paints are about the same consistency as other student-grade acrylics. They will hold some brush strokes, but are best for smooth applications.

Like most acrylics, they work well on paper, wood, metal, and other surfaces as well as canvas. This set is great for exploring the medium, The storage box has a plastic tray for the tubes and nice reusable slipcover that makes them easy to see while keeping the paints corralled on a shelf or in a drawer between painting sessions. The plastic palette is handy too, but be aware that you can't rewet acrylics the way you do watercolors. Squeeze out only what you need. (The palette wells tempt you to squeeze out more.)

Designed as an introductory set, this collection of acrylics includes a rainbow of hues in .7 oz tubes. The tubes are not really what I would call large, but they aren't as small as the ones you will find in some sets. They are great for those who want to explore acrylics and find out which colors they like best before buying larger tubes.
The paint covers well and the colors are rich. They are especially well-suited to smooth techniques. They are not quite as liquidy as fluid acrylics, but not as heavy-bodied as most tube colors. They are more like watercolors in consistency and do not hold brush strokes. If you want impasto effects, graduate to heavy-bodied acrylics or use texture mediums.
The paints work well on paper, canvas, or other surfaces. Crafters and mixed-media artists will enjoy their intensity for details and accents. Would make a nice gift, with a few brushes and a couple of canvas panels for beginners.
The tubes are a nice size for small studies in the field, especially if you like work with a smooth finish. They are a little bigger than most watercolor tubes and there's a nice selection of landscape-friendly colors.

Here's a nice selection of colors for painting outdoors or for trying out acrylics.

These small tubes are handy for your sketch kit, along with a canvas panel or two. They're also great for multimedia, or for trying out acrylics if you aren't sure you'll like them. Thicker than fluid acrylics, they are not as heavy-bodied as Liquitex in tubes, but they cover well.

They are good for beginners, for tweens, and for crafters as well as for artists and illustrators.

My husband is a model-railroader in his eighties. He does a lot of fine work on HO miniatures. These magnifying glasses with built-in rechargeable led lights are a great addition to his arsenal of magnification aids for his aging eyes.

The lit glasses come with a nice case and charging cord. They plug into the same kind of charging station you use for your phone. The bright led lights charge quickly and last through several work sessions. Also included are a pair of flip-up magnifiers for times when you want to see farther away.

Great for yourself or as a gift for your favorite crafter.

Arriving in a handy box, this inspiring assortment of gessoed canvas panels (over heavy fiber-based board of some kind) will provide hours of fun for acrylic pour fans, tweens, mixed media artists, crafters, and artists wanting to experiment with oils or acrylics without a heavy commitment to expensive, space-consuming supplies. They are also great for Plein air studies if you, like me, are a studio painter, but enjoy doing quick pieces outdoors in nice weather. The panels are nicely finished and backed with plain white paper. They are similar in weight to others I have purchased.

Great for quick grass-like textures and blending, fan brushes do things no other shape can accomplish as well, and here's a unique set in feisty hog bristle. Not as stiff as camel hair, they are flexible and resilient. There is an interesting range of sizes for use on different-sized works and levels of detail. They come in a nice plastic pocket for storage or gifting.

I paint in acrylics, but am not great at signing my name on the front of a work. I usually sign on the back, but if someone insists, I can use either the black or white fine line version of these pens. I've tried the white ones over dark paint on canvas and the black ones work great over light colors. They are acrylic paint markers, so they should last as long as the paints do, which is probably much longer than any of us will live. If white stands out too much, it's easy to add a glaze over the top. They are also good for adding any kind of line work to a piece.

It's fun to work in white on black paper, and these pens are perfect for that. Use them alone or add extra zing to colored pencil drawings on dark papers. They are also great for drawing on black or dark-colored stones. Easy to use, they haven't leaked or pooled on me yet. Great for yourself or for a gift, they come in an easy-to store tube.

I love round brushes for acrylics. They are great for adding detail and intuitive line work. This set includes useful sizes with handles that fit into a sketch box for work outdoors.

The synthetic bristles have enough resilience and bounce to work acrylic paints. They are also inexpensive, which is an important feature, at least for me, because no matter how careful you are, canvas and the chemicals in the paint are tough on bushes, so they have to be replaced fairly often.

This is also a good set for those just starting with acrylics. They work well without a huge investment. Large flat brushes will also be needed, but these are perfect for detail.

If you like to use charcoal for sketching, but need finer marks for details, especially in sketchbooks, these pencils may be what you are looking for. They are a little harder and easier to point than charcoal or Conte sticks.

The included colors make them useful for figure work or landscape value studies. They come un-pointed. You can sharpen them either in a dedicated art pencil sharpener or with a craft knife, depending on your preference. The charcoal "leads" hold a point fairly well.

This set makes a useful addition to any sketch box. The case is lightweight, but sturdy and well-designed to keep the pencils in good condition and accessible.

If you have white and light-colored acrylic markers or top-quality colored pencils (and you have used them on black paper before), this sketchbook is a great place to try out dramatic effects.

I've always loved light colors on black. I guess there's something of a clown-on-velvet girl in me. It sparks my inner child to play with dramatic contrasts. This spiral-bound black sketchbook is a terrific place to play. I've seen others use colored pencils on black paper like this, but that's a bit above my skill level at this point. So far, I've had better luck with acrylic paint markers, especially the ones in pastel tones. So much fun!

This sketchbook has a very heavy chipboard backing, which makes it practical to use on the go. The paper isn't really heavy enough for acrylic paint (from the tube rather than markers), but it would be fine for multi-media applications. It doesn't have any tooth at all, so forget pastels, unless you know some secret I don't.

Add this pad to a set of pastel acrylic pens for a fun gift.

If you know an artist or someone who uses mechanical pencils in their work (engineer? architect? designer?), this set would make a fantastic gift. It's the kind of thing you probably wouldn't buy for yourself, but which you would carry with you and use every day for years.

The quality mechanical pencils with included refill leads are enclosed in a gorgeous, sturdy, fine-quality leather case. I love it! I feel like somebody just sent me an amazing birthday present. Hey, the price is very fair. Why not gift yourself?

If you or someone you know wants to try acrylics but doesn't want to make a huge investment, this set is for you. It contains an inspiring rainbow of paints. They are well-pigmented and medium body. You can use them with a palette knife if you want to for a low impasto effect. (For deep texture, add a texture medium--not included.)

The set includes one large tube each of black (unspecified) and white (unspecified) along with smaller tubes of the other colors. There are also three short-handled brushes (two flats and a round.) They are small, but just the right firmness and resistance for use with the paints.

Intended for use on smaller canvases (11 x 14 and smaller), the kit would give you all the paints you'd need to get you through the spring Plein Aire season.

Arriving in a nice storage box, this would make a nice gift for a teen or adult student.

Arriving in a gorgeous, well-crafted slim wooden case with metal hinges and clasps, this set has everything a student artist needs for an online, or in-person drawing class. There are a dozen drawing pencils graded from 6H to 6B.  There are also a dozen graphite sticks (which I love) A sandpaper stick is included to sharpen them, if you want. There'is a polymer eraser and a kneaded eraser(regular size, not tiny). There's vine charcoal and four charcoal pencils too. If you want color, there are a dozen hard wax color sticks with all the basics. Trays with the color sticks and graphite sticks lift out of the box for easy use, but keep the plastic tops if you want them to stay put when you put them back in the case. The most surprising item in this set is a great little posable manniken. This little guy is amazing. He's fully jointed and has a stand. You can pose and light him to plan characters. Make someone very happy, or change a life! Send this set with a fat sketchbook and a coupon for the online class of your choice.

Easy-flowing Japanese ink fills these pens in unusual earth tones (though six blues and vibrant spring green are also included. They are easy to use and the pigments are opaque. I tried the yellow pen on black paper and it was positively vibrant.

These would be great for rock art, wood, metal, and glass. A beautiful set for artists and crafters.

This assortment of illustration pens meet every illustration need. There are six rigid nibs ranging from .003 to .08 along with two flexible tips (F and M) for thick and thin line. I used watercolor and alcohol markers over lines from all the markers and they didn't smear or bleed.

These are great markers for the price.

These are among my favorite colored pencils now. I may buy a larger set, but the basics are included in this portable tin. They lay down rich color, even on black. The cores are sturdy and don't break. I love them! Great for illustration, sketchbooks, fine art, mixed media, cartooning, and more. Check them out!

What a great little landscape set! It's small enough to fit in a coat pocket and has 24 of the colors you need outdoors. It even has a waterbrush and three mixxing wells, as well as the lid. When you run out of these paints, you can replenish them from tubes.

The case itself is beautiful. It snaps closed tight and there's a metal ring on the back, which you can hook over your finger. What more could you want?

If you've seen beads made of polymer clay, you know how beautiful they are. You can choose your own color combinations and there are so many ways to create variations.

This set provides everything you need (except your oven) to create necklaces, pins, earrings, and other jewelry. The only limits are your imagination and your patience.

I ordered the set to make character models for picture book illustrations. The clay is easy to use, has great consistency, and the beautiful colors are inspiring.

What fun! You don't have to mix white glue and silicone with acrylics to create amazing abstract magical artworks when you have this set.

Spread out some newspaper to protect your chosen flat surface, put on a pair of gloves to save time washing your hands later, and go to town. To maintain unity in your composition, choose not more than three colors and white. Tilt the canvas after pouring to let the paint do its thing. You can also use a craft stick to move it around.

I used a red and blue with white over a failed greenish abstract acrylic on canvas to create the sample in the picture. I have a lot of failed canvases around here. It's a great warm-up or relaxation exercise.

It's just fun. You might not want to wear clothes you care about when you do this. It doesn't really spread around much (all the paint stayed on the canvas when I did the demo picture), but it's better if you don't have to worry about anything.

If you've used gouache, you know it's hard to rewet it on your palette. Unlike watercolor, its cousin, once dry, it pretty much is what it is. You can dissolve it, but it just gets grainy and nasty. For that reason, it's hard to mix more custom colors than you plan to use in one session. That's why it's so great to have this amazing collection of brilliant hues, tints, and tones. You won't have to do much mixing, especially if you're working on illustrations. Just chose the colors you like, and brush them on. Speaking of brushing, these paints are a great consistency. They are opaque, as you would expect, yet they flow on with minimal brush strokes.
In addition, they come in four plastic reusable trays nested one above another in a handsome, sturdy cardboard box. They are very nice paints at a student-grade price. Beginners will enjoy them, but so will watercolor artists wanting to touch up Plein air disasters, students, and, hey, I like them. (Gouache isn't my primary medium because of its quirks, but this set solves some of those problems for me, and I'll be using it more.)

I don't know about you, but I like to slip my facemask over my head instead of latching it behind my ears. It's easier to pull up and down when on walks in the park and it's just more comfortable. With cloth masks, I've actually taken off the ear loops and added twin elastic head straps. That's fine because those masks are reusable many times. With these adjustable buckle straps, though, you can quickly transform any earloop mask into one that slips over your head, and you can create a personalized fit too. I've tried the silicone kind with the little bumps on them, and those do work, but I like these better.

These beautiful short-handled synthetic brushes are great for watercolor, gouache, ink and acrylic. They are packaged in a thoughtful zipper case along with a metal #4 palette knife and two sponges.

Each of the two sections of the case lift up making the brushes even easier to access. The brush assortment should cover almost any need. There's a fan brush to create easy forest and grass effects, numerous flats in different sizes, filberts, and detail rounds.

I like the resilience of the bristles. They have sufficient boy to handle acrylics but are soft and absorbent enough for watercolors.

If you have kids at home, you'll love this creativity-boosting set of paint applicators. (They're designed for tempera, fluid acrylics, and watercolor, but you can also use them for white glue, ink, and other liquid art media.)

I guess I fall into the kid-at-heart category because I think they're great. I tucked one of the sponge daubers into my Plein Aire kit to clean up the box at the end of each session. The ones that look like sea creatures are great for making textures and creating quick landscape elements. Experimentation is irresistible.

These would be great for a primary classroom or for aunties to keep along with art supplies in a visiting child toybox. Just wash them with soap and water at the end of each session for longer life.

I ordered this assortment of "pipe cleaners" to help me craft character models for picture book illustrations. I need something to look at when I draw and models of my characters will help me when I want to use dramatic lighting and portray them from different angles.

I was delighted when I got this set. Any crafter would be! (or any tween) The resealable plastic bag includes 600 long, well-made stems in a generous assortment of brilliant colors, black white, and gorgeous glitter. They are going to turn into a variety of monsters, insects, and critter body parts.

Whether you use pipe cleaners for classroom projects, to make ETSY crafts, or for holiday decor, you will love to keep this set handy in a drawer.

I have some Sakura Pigma pens I bought 20 years ago and they still work. The ink is rich black and permanent. It doesn't bleed under watercolor or alcohol markers. This set includes a range of points from 01 - 12. It's a great set for professional artists and illustrators as well as students, crafters, and hobbyists. The pens come in a blister pack. There's no case, but the sizes are marked on both the side and the top of each pen, so you can store them upright or on their sides.
If you want to give an artist an excellent set of permanent drawing pens with rigid (not brush) tips for even lines, this is a good choice.

This soft paper is very absorbent. If you like watercolor papers with either a crisp hot press surface or a rougher cold press surface, be aware that the paper in these pads is neither.
That said, this bright white, lightly sized paper doesn't warp or buckle much, even though it's only 130 lb weight. I don't see any micro-perforation, but it isn't necessary because the pages are glued on the top edge of each pad and they come out easily when you are ready. The cover folds all the way back out of the way. The chipboard backing of each pad is heavy and rigid so you don't need a drawing board. Masking tape to keep the paper taut is all you need.
Its unique texture and absorbent qualities invite experimentation. If you want to try smaller works, folding it back and forth and then tearing it carefully gives you a pretty deckle-like edge, nice for mounting on greeting cards.
It's suitable for all wet media including watercolors, gouache, acrylic, and ink.
This isn't listed as a use, but this paper is great for colored pencil! The low-relief cold-press has the perfect tooth for building up layers of color and the bright white surface makes them sing.

I use a sketchpad for exploring ideas and recording experiences. When I paint, I use acrylics on canvas, but in my sketchbook, I use watercolor, gouache, graphite, colored pencil, and all sorts of markers.

The mostly smooth, medium weight paper in this spiral-bound book is perfect for all those media and more. Rubber stamping, collage, and oil pastel would also work. The book includes a lightweight, flexible synthetic barrier sheet to put behind each page as you work to prevent bleedthrough. That won't happen with watercolor or acrylic. The paper is too thick for that, but it might happen with some markers and alcohol inks, so this was a very thoughtful inclusion.

The pad is made with artists in mind, but it's also great for students at all levels. It's inexpensive enough for guilt-free noodling, but if you do happen to create a masterpiece, each page has micro-perforation, so you can easily tear it out.

There are 64 pages, so if you do a sketchbook challenge, it will hold two months worth of work. Go for it!

It arrives quickly from Amazon in a sturdy box and includes a catalog of other products from the company.

The synthetic bristles are resilient. There is a nice variety of not only sizes but also shapes. I usually paint in acrylic. These brushes manage the paint very well. They would also be good for gouache and other paints with a certain amount of body.

The reasonable price is important for me because, even though I keep brushes in water while I'm working and wash them carefully with soap after each session, the paint is hard on them, and they don't last forever.

There are small ones in this set for illustrations and smaller works as well as larger ones for my works on canvas.
Students and beginners will find everything they need here for exploration.

The colorful pom poms in this generously-sized bag come in a useful variety of sizes. Use them to create cute, fuzzy critters. Add the included googly eyes for a professional touch.

I'm going to use them to make models of characters for my picture book illustrations, but imagination is the only limit for your own applications.

This set would make a great creative gift for tween, teen, or adult crafters. I wouldn't recommend it for young children because all of the parts are small.

If you need legs or a framework for wings, a package of colorful chenille stems would be a useful addition.

I'm going to use this polymer clay set to make models of original characters for my picture books, but it would also work for people who make miniatures of any kind or jewelry, especially custom beads. The set has a gorgeous array of colors. It would make an excellent gift for a jewelry crafter.

If you are a calligrapher or card designer you will love these watercolor brush pens. The "brush" tips of most watercolor markers are a little too rigid. These are actual brushes that respond to movement and pressure to create beautiful, expressive lines.

There are twenty gorgeous colors and a refillable water brush for blending or using with a supplementary palette. All arrive in a sturdy cylindrical cardboard container that makes them easy to use and store. Each brush has a tight-fitting plastic cap to keep it from drying out. If you turn them upside down in the storage tube, the ends of the brushes display their colors making it easy to find the ones you want.

A great gift for greeting card artists and placard designers as well as hobbyists, crafters, tweens, and teens.

Illustrators and designers have always loved gouache. It combines the matte surface and rich color of pastel with the sturdiness and flexibility of watercolor. Now mixed media fine artists and watercolorists are rediscovering its advantages.

This beautiful set provides a rainbow of colors. The tubes are generously sized and packaged in two plastic trays to keep the hues easy to find. A nice palette and a brush suitable for creating swatches are included in a nice-looking box for easy gift-giving. For storage of the set once you start to use it. I suggest a plastic shoebox with a tight-fitting lid. You will want to use good brushes with gouache because picking hairs out of the dried paint and smoothing out brushstrokes is not fun. (Also, wash your brushes well. Gouache isn't as hard as acrylic on bristles, but when it dries, it's not as forgiving as watercolor, which brings up another point, only squeeze out what you need and tighten the caps after opening, once gouache dries, it doesn't re-wet well.)

The set would make a great gift for artists of any age, especially those who have never used gouache before and want to give it a try, or for watercolorists who want to rescue Plein air disasters in their sketchbooks, in fact, a multimedia art journal or watercolor sketchbook would make a good accompaniment to this set.

I love water brushes! They are a great way to do color sketches from the front seat of the car or in the field. They are also great for planning larger works in the studio. You don't need to carry heavy extra water bottles when out hiking. Just fill the reservoir of the brush with a few drops from your drinking water bottle or even a clean creek and you're set.

You can tell from this that I usually carry a pocket set of watercolor pans and just use clear water in the brushes. This set includes marker-style non-refillable color brushes along with several brushes you can fill with clear water or your own paint. The included colors will mix to create almost any hue you need, though color in each brush lends itself best to pure, fresh colors in the art.

The blurb talks about the storage box. It is very nice looking, and makes a good presentation box if you are giving it as a gift, but it is too flimsy and hard to use, at least for me, to be practical for storage or for use in the field. If you are carrying this set to class or outdoors, you will probably want some kind of plastic pouch (waterbrushes can leak if you don't tighten them enough or something heavy lands on top of them.)

With the included practice pages, this set would make an appreciated gift for artistic tweens, teens, college students, traveling artists, or aspiring illustrators.

This gorgeous rainbow of double-ended watercolor markers will make you think of being a kid and opening a big box of crayons.

The sturdy hard-sided zipper fabric case is beautifully designed to allow you quick access to just the color you need.

One end of each marker is very fine (and a few replacement tips are even provided for the ones you use most.) The other end is a fairly stiff "brush" that allows for thick and thin lines.

This set would make a wonderful gift for an artist of any age.

Whether you like to paint outdoors, in your car, at a class, or on a trip, this is the set for you. Good for experienced artists, this kit is a perfect starter for beginners or students. It provides a rainbow of gorgeous colors in a palette that unfolds. There are wells in the lid for mixing custom colors and a pad for wiping your brush. Just add a couple of water brushes, a pad of watercolor paper, a tote bag, and an online class subscription or a list of favorite instruction channel links to make a gift that will turn long winter days into an adventure.

An ingenious design for lasting creative fun.

For the student who carries supplies to class, the Plein Air artist who does a lot of field work, or anyone with limited studio space, this versatile brush set is a boon.

The brushes' synthetic bristles are springy enough to handle acrylics or oils, yet flexible enough for watercolor or gouache. There is a one-inch flat and a number 16 round for larger works and fast layiins, There are also both round and flat detail brushes, including a rigger. Medium-sized selections include two fan brishes in different sizes, a couple of angles, and two filberts. The brushes have short handles, which I like, especially for location work and demos.

The case is amazing. It's a canvas-like synthetic lined with clear plastic. A flap folds over the top of the brushes, which is nice when you have to transport them while still damp. The whole case folds over and ties. Toss it into a tote or day pack with a travel watercolor set, a rag, and a pad, and you're ready for a session anywhere.

A great set for yourself, or as a gift for someone taking an art class. Also good for high school or college art classes.

This set of acrylic paints would make a great gift for any artist (including yourself.) It includes all the basic colors you need in generous, well-made jars that seal tightly keeping them fresh. They are not heavy-body and will not create impasto effects with every brushstroke unless you add a special medium, which I like because it gives me control of the surface. The paints are richly pigmented and cover beautifully. leaving a nice matte finish.

The whole set fits perfectly in a plastic shoebox making it easy to store and transport.

I love it.

Designed to be used with Pinata Colors, this clear medium extends and dilutes alcohol inks to provide a broader range of tints and more control of color intensity.

With experimentation on less-absorbent surfaces, it can also produce a range of special effects.

The bottle of extender comes with a funnel and applicator bottle. The thoughtfully-designed squeezable applicator has a fine tip with a tiny reusable cover.

This medium is highly flammable and has a strong odor. Use it in a well-ventilated area. It is NOT for use by children.

These beautiful artist-quality come in a tasteful box for easy gifting. Each brush is individually sleeved in plastic.

I love round brushes and this is a wonderful collection of sizes from fine to medium. They hold a lot of water and have beautiful points making them good for both beginners and more seasoned artists.

Team them with a watercolor pad, a travel watercolor kit, and an online class to turn the coming year into an artistic adventure.

The watercolor paper in this handy-sized 20-sheet pad is medium weight with a linen-like textured surface on the front and a smoother surface on the back. The cover of the pad folds all the way back leaving the rest of the glue-bound sheets easy to mask and use. The backing is heavy gray chipboard. When you are out and about, you don't really need a drawing board.

This value-priced, handy-sized pad is a boon for visual notes and experimentation in watercolor, ink, gouache, or collage. It's also good for students and beginners.

Team it with a travel-sized watercolor set for a memorable gift.

The pictures on the box show a father making rubber blocks and toys for a son with this set, and you could do that with the included heavy, colorful rubber sheets, but that's not why I ordered it. I'd like to experiment with rubber stamps, ink, and alcohol markers in my art.

This set includes everything you need to make original rubber stamps, except the designs, of course, that's up to you. It's easy to draw on the blocks with pencil, and a wide variety of carving tools is provided.

I would not recommend this set to children for carving themselves. but it's great for artists and adults making things for children to play with or use.

I do watercolors with a local Plein air group and have been watching tutorials on watercolor postcards. I like working small outdoors and would like to try something different than sketchbooks for a while.

Greeting cards seemed like a possibility. These are great. They are high-quality medium-weight cotton cold-press watercolor paper cut and folded to fit in included greeting card envelopes. I like them better than postcards. You don't get the mailing discount you get with postcards, but your art is protected and you have more room to write if you want to.

The cold-press surface is lightly textured. The cards work for colored pencil, ink, markers, or even printing. They are good for travel, though not many of us are doing that these days.

They are a non-threatening way to try out new media and share work with friends.

If you work small, you know how quickly fine brushes wear out. These are a great buy.

The handles are specially shaped to make them easy to hold and control. There is a wide selection of rounds and flats, long and short bristles. There's even an angled spot liner.

The brushes are well made with finely crafted handles, firm ferrules, and resilient synthetic bristles with nice points. They are suitable for watercolor, gouache, enamel, acrylics, or oil.

Great for gift-giving, each brush is individually wrapped with a plastic sleeve protecting the head. They arrive inside a beautiful reusable velvet-like storage pouch, which is protected by a tastefully-designed box. Just add a tag and a bow to make your favorite artist, illustrator, or hobbyist happy.

These quality canvases are great for both hobbyists and professional artists. They are finely crafted and individually wrapped to keep them clean and safe until use. The canvas covers the sides of the heavy stretcher bars and is neatly stapled in the back. Wedges are included, but usually not necessary.

Arriving quickly, they were in perfect shape when I opened the box. Thank you for this great source of supplies for creators during this challenging time.

This handy kit, in a beautifully-crafted case includes everything you need to sew on buttons, snaps and make quick clothing repairs.

There's a rainbow of sewing thread, a few shirt buttons, some snaps, safety pins, needles, threaders, thimbles, pins, a seam0ripper, measuring tape, white fabric marking pencil, tweezers, nail-clippers (?), and a small but sharp pair of scissors.

This handy refillable kit would make a good gift for a student heading off to college or someone going on a long trip. Also useful for a mountain cabin or beach cottage--anywhere clothing selection is limited and small repairs make a big difference.

This set includes eight fine firm-point permanent pigment markers from very fine (.05) to medium (.8) and one brush point marker that allows you to make beautiful marks of variable width. I'm using them for sketching, with watercolors, but they would also be great for lettering.

So far, in my opinion, they compare well with the most popular professional brand.

With pans of beautiful color that pop out so you can rearrange and refill them easily, this handy plastic kit contains everything you need. There's a round and a flat water brush, a small sponge to lift extra paint or clean your brush, and a nice palette with different-sized mixing wells.

The landscape-friendly paper pad is the same size as the paint kit. 15 sheets of medium-weight paper are 3.8" x 7.2". The paper has a linen-like texture on one side and is smooth on the other. This would be a good gift for yourself if you want to try painting outdoors, or for a friend.

These beautiful canvases arrived individually wrapped and ready for art.

Many galleries and collectors now prefer contemporary acrylic and oil paintings on heavy stretcher bars with canvas that wraps around the sides and requires no framing. These beautifully prepared supports make it easy for artists to meet these expectations. They are sturdy and true. They sit flat against the wall.

The small size can provide and entry price point for collectors. They are also great for acrylic pour enthusiasts. Use them to create stunning groupings or sell them at craft fairs. The bargain price makes them perfect for both experienced artists and beginners.

These beautiful alcohol markers have two ends. One is a slightly flexible round tip, not exactly a brush tip, but not totally rigid either. It's great for lettering and detail. The other is a wide chisel tip, good for thick and thin, with practice, and for filling in broad areas of color.

The 80, yes, 80, different colors include a good range of darks, mid-tones, and lights for shading. Each marker has a triangular shape so it won't roll off your drafting table, but the edges of the triangle are rounded for comfort. They fit well in your hand.

Of course, they are not refillable like the, uh-hem, competition, but they are so inexpensive they are easy to replace. The case is definitely a feature that sets them apart. The official description says it's black fabric. It isn't. It's like the one shown in the photo--clear sturdy plastic with a handle, hinges, and latches. You can see all of the colors and pick out the one you want instantly. Brilliant!

This is a great set for a student artist, a kid interested in trying his hand at manga, or any artist who wants to explore the world of alcohol markers.

This easy-to-use kit includes everything you need to get started with acrylic pouring, even two back-stapled canvases, cups, plastic stirring sticks, gloves, and instructions. There are also videos on the company site.

I've been wanting to try this exciting technique for a long time and even bought some of the stuff, but mixing all the components and the mess was intimidating. In this set, the paints are already mixed with the right mediums. All you have to do is shake up the tubes, Squeeze each color into a provided paper cup. stir gently, and pour onto the canvas. (I left white spaces in my experiments. You'll probably want to cover the whole canvas and the sides too. They don't require frames. Just add wire to the back and hang your masterpiece on the wall--allow a day or two for it to dry. It stays sticky longer than straight acrylic paint.)

Then, watch the magic happen. It's great fun for people of all ages (though kids should be supervised). Watch the videos first. Protect your work area. Enjoy! Great for a gift or for COVID boredom relief.

More and more collectors are picking up small square works on gallery wrap canvases and these little beauties fit the bill. They are well constructed and lie flat. They come individually wrapped and arrived in excellent condition. The stretcher bars are heavy-duty and the canvas is well-primed.

If you are a beginner and want to experiment with oil or acrylic, these are a great place to start. You can try out different styles and each will be ready to hang on the wall without a frame.

If you are a professional, it's fun sometimes to try a different size and shape. It can re-energize your creativity. These canvases are small, easy to ship, and allow you to offer an entry price-point. They are also good for galleries catering to tourists as they can be tucked into a suitcase.

For the price, they are amazing. Good little canvases.

Similar in consistency to gloss acrylic medium, this special decoupage glue from Gorilla brushes on smoothly, holds quickly, and dries fast to make layering easy. Crystal clear when dry, it gives paper a soft sheen.

I applied pieces of color printed magazine pages to light-weight watercolor paper. The ink from the magazines didn't run or smear at all and the paper didn't buckle. I'm going to try it with collage illustrations, but it would be great for any mixed-media or craft application.

It's not as thick as the competition, and a little more transparent. I don't know what they mean by "warmth", but I didn't want to take away any stars. Maybe they meant ease of clean-up. It thins and washes up like acrylic medium,--don't let it dry on your brush, but it's easy to wash out.

In a handy assortment of point sizes, these pens compare to the Pigma versions in my sketch box. They deliver even permanent lines wherever you want them.

I use them to ink the best pencil lines when sketching outdoors before adding hints of watercolor. So far, they've worked fine.

Bigger than most travel sets, but more complete, this artfully-designed plastic-encased kit includes a palette, two water brushes (a round and a square), a tube of titanium white, and a beautiful collection of quarter pan colors. (When you've used them up, refill with your own tube paints. The case is perfect.)

You, or the recipient of your thoughtful gift, will love them for outdoor sketching, travel, art journals, home-made greeting cards, art class, and more. Perfect for beginners to the medium.

Packed in a beautiful cardboard case is everything a beginning or intermediate artist will need to get started.

In addition to the spectacular rainbow of watercolors in a sturdy metal case (with a palette in the lid), there's a small round conventional watercolor brush, two refillable water brushes (one round and one flat), a sharpened HB pencil, and a 20 sheet watercolor pad. There's even a beautiful gift card with pressed flowers.

If you know an experienced artist who's starting to experiment with plein air sketching, this is a great set. Working small helps defeat wind and changing light.

If you are an artist who works in a different medium and wants to play around on a COVID stay-at-home day, hey this pretty set will make you smile. (These are real artists' materials.)

The set includes everything you need to get started with stamping except the stamps themselves.

+The stamp pad is firm, absorbent, and resilient. It delivers ink evenly to the raised surfaces of your commercially created or handmade design. It comes pre-inked and ready to use in a plastic case with a tightly-fitting lid.

+The ink is rich, deep black, and permanent. Lines stay crisp under watercolor, acrylic, and alcohol ink.

+Two bottles of stamp pad ink are included for extended use.

+Clean your stamps with a generous bottle of archival ink cleaner.

Use stamps for creating greeting cards, making place cards, enhancing bullet journals, personalizing office supplies, designing original art, and more. Add color with markers or paint. Make your own stamps or visit a craft store for hundreds of pre-made possibilities.

I painted a dark rock with three light colors. I could have added another layer of each color for more opacity. These fluid colors are delivered through two-ended pens. One end is a bullet point for drawing fine lines and the other a chisel point for filling and calligraphy.

The ink stays wet long enough for some blending, but, like other forms of acrylic, dries fairly quickly. You can use light colors over dark colors once dry.

I almost forgot. They also include four amazing plastic mandala design stencils. You can combine them to create an endless number of designs.

The set includes 24 gorgeous colors in two separate clear plastic sleeves for easy access. Artists and crafters will have a lot of fun experimenting with these pens.

These opaque water-based colors are rich and lovely. They cover without streaking while being fluid enough to flow on without brushstrokes. I used them to rescue the attached failed Plein air watercolor (it got too dark on site).

One problem with gouache is its inability, unlike watercolor, to be re-wet. If you squeeze some out of a tube and don't use it in one sitting, it won't work as well after it dries and you add water. This set addresses the problem with special packaging. Each color (mostly rich tints) is contained in its own little plastic pot like the individual jelly containers included with toast in cafes. The "jelly" cups sit in a special case with a removable palette that snuggles tightly down on top. The lid's fastener is the weak spot of this design. It fits closely, but it's hard to snap shut.

The paints are amazing. I peeled the lids back just enough to dip my brush in and pushed them back down. I left the colors I didn't use sealed. So far, the paint has remained moist and viable. This is a beautiful set and I will use it to modify my sketchbook watercolors, but designers, illustrators and others who us this underestimated medium will enjoy using them.

Many of us used pipe cleaners and other improvised bits of wire to make masks when nothing else was available and they didn't work very well. These sturdy, flexible nosepieces will make your homemade masks fit better and last longer. They are like the ones you see on the professional versions.

Smooth on one side and textured on the other, they are ready to use in your favorite pattern. Lightweight but strong, they'll allow wearers of your mask to fit it to their face for less slippage and steaming up of glasses. I hope this won't last much longer, but chances are we'll need masks for a while. This package will make or upgrade more masks than your family (and probably all the neighbors) will need for the duration.(100 pieces!)

You'll have a blast exploring alcohol inks with this set of 22 gorgeous colors.

There are lights, midtones and darks. All dilute with "blender" solution or 91% alcohol (hard to find right now).

Each vial has a fine spout tip. Snip just the very tip or prick with a pin for the finest application. The inks are very fluid and the vials are squeezable. I did the loose painting on Yupo with a nylon brush, mostly by dripping. I diluted the ink and cleaned the brush with alcohol.

You could also fill a water brush with one of these colors and use it for calligraphy or drawing. There are endless possiblities.

Draw or paint with these saturated alcohol inks (for use on nonporous surfaces such as Yupo, metal, plastic, or glass.) This set is a great way to get acquainted with the medium. There are many tutorials available online if these amazing colors are as new to you as they were to me.

I did the abstract painting above with the three included colors and 91% alcohol on a sheet of Yupo I'd used for loose watercolors many times. It was lightly stained with blue which shows through in spots, The inks didn't pick up the watercolor, but there wasn't much paint left on the surface so I can't guarantee they wouldn't. Experiment!

I used a nylon round brush for this one, but if you want to draw, these are classic colors for that. A refillable brush (like a water brush) with a fine point is included in the set.

For a beginner/explorer like me this set is a great introduction to the world of alcohol markers. Professional illustrators use Copecs. (They didn't invent alcohol markers, so these aren't rip-offs.) That very expensive brand offers more colors and their pens are refillable, which these aren't. Theirs also offer a brush tip. These have a chisel tip on one end and a bullet tip on the other. That said, these markers are gorgeous, and a fraction of the cost of their professional cousins.

They have a triangular shape so they won't roll off your drafting table and the caps are easy to take off and replace.

Buy some Bristol and check out one of the great instructional videos available from illustrators online. Then enter the exciting world of illustration. These 60 markers come in a handy canvas case that makes it easy to find the colors you want. There are light, medium, and dark shades in every hue for exciting blends.

For the price, these are amazing. They'd make a great splurge for yourself or a gift for your favorite high school comic artist.

When I pulled my Singer out of the closet after 20 years to make masks, I found I had given away some essential accessories, like needles and bobbins. This little kit provides both in a handy little plastic box.

If you have the same problem, this might solve it for you.

It's easy to create thick and thin hand-written messages with this set of brush pens for lettering artists and hobbyists. The long, firm fiber tips deliver an even flow of colorful ink. There are darks, lights, brights, and even fluorescents.

They are a lot easier to use than conventional calligraphy pens, at least for me. Control line weight by pressing down, letting up, or even laying the brush tip on its side.

If you (or your kids) are bored these hot summer afternoons, cue up a tutorial on calligraphy and practice. If you want to show off your skills, order a Bristol pad and make cards for friends. Write up favorite quotations, add flourishes such as hearts, flowers, or rainbows, photograph them with your phone and share on social media.

(Also great for a creative entertainment box if children visit your house. Note: best for kids 8+ Younger children do better with crayons and ordinary markers.)

I love acrylics because they dry quickly, can be used on almost any surface, are water-soluble wet, archival when dry, and have almost no odor, They are magic. Put an easy-flowing fluid version of these great paints in a medium-fine pointed set of pens and you have this product.

To use, choose a color, press down on a hard surface to let the paint flow down onto the pen's tip, and then draw. That's it. You can mix colors if you are fast enough to add color from another pen on your drawing before it dries, but these pens are best for bold designs with bright primary and secondary hues.

Use them for stunning results on black/dark-colored paper or poster board, rocks, glass, plastic, wood, or metal. Make hand-lettered plaques or signs. Label anything. You'll find dozens of ways to use them.

Have fun, I have.

These great pens with waterproof ink can be used alone or with watercolor, They don't run. There are a number of different tips and the ink flows smoothly from all of them.

They are good for sketching and illustration as well as calligraphy and other lettering projects. They are great for making cards, plaques and more.

These are great for artists, crafters, students and more. If your teens and tweens are going nuts being inside this summer, buy them a set and find calligraphy lessons online. They'll love making cards for their friends.

Each of these beautiful fine point pens is a different color. That's a hundred different colors! They come with a set of stencils you can use in a scrapbook, journal, or to make your own stationery.

Organize your life. These pens make keeping a bullet journal a lot more fun. The points are firm and metal reinforced, not felt tip, so they allow you to add fine detail or write in small spaces.

Add a gridded spiral notebook for a great back-to-school gift, or add a dash of inspiration to your home office.

I love nylon brushes for acrylic. Unfortunately, the texture of canvas and the medium itself eat them up, so I have to buy many, especially small ones.

These are springy enough to handle tube paints. The set includes a variety of shapes and sizes (from medium to small.) Most are flats. There's only one round, a #2. The flexible tapered fan is great for grasses in watercolor.

With short handles, they fit perfectly into sketch boxes.

These brushes are soft, but have enough body to handle tube acrylics as well as watercolors. The flats and angles make sharp, clear edges. The rounds hold a surprising amount of water and come to reasonable points. They are sized to lay in basic shapes as well as capture fine details. You could use them to add details to larger works, but I will tuck them into my sketch box to use on canvases and sketch sheets not larger than 8 x 10 or 11 x 14.

I haven't used them long, but the ferrules seem tight and no hairs have shed yet. This set is a great bargain, especially for students and beginners.

This handy, lightweight set includes 36 gorgeous colors in square pans, a lift-out five-section palette, and two water brushes, one flat and the other round. The plastic case snaps shut securely and fits in one hand. All you need for a trip to the wilds is a watercolor sketchbook, a few paper towels (two small sponges are included, but I like paper towels). and a folding stool. If you like to make color notes outdoors and develop paintings in your studio, you'll love this handy, inexpensive kit. (BTW, the case alone is worth the price. If you are a pro, consider refilling it with your favorite colors.)

The set is good for older children, adult beginners, hand-crafted greeting cards, and artist trading cards, as well as plein air sketchers. It includes everything you need to get started. There are so many colors, most projects will require very little mixing, resulting in fresh, bright, satisfying hues. Choose quality watercolor or mixed media paper for best results.

I'm just getting started with these beautiful acrylic paint pens. So far, I've used them on black paper and river stones. They've performed beautifully. The paint flows on smoothly and dries quickly. It creates even, opaque lines on multiple surfaces. It glides on over other media. The bold, bright colors include primary and secondary hues along with white, black, pink, and silver.

The first time you use a marker, you have to press down hard to move paint into the stiff, fibrous tip. Do not perform this step on your artwork! The first time, it will probably make a little puddle on the surface. Use a palette. Then you can dip a brush into that paint so it doesn't go to waste. Once paint flows onto the tip, the pen stays ready to use. Be sure to replace the cap to keep the paint from drying out.

This round, soft elastic is similar to that used on surgical masks I bought before the pandemic hit our shores. It comes in a bulk pack that will make a huge number of masks.

It is very stretchy, soft, and comfortable, especially for masks that slip over the top of your head. It would certainly work for ear loops too, but I like the slip-over ones better.

The bulk pack provides a continuous strand (in a plastic bag) ready to be cut into custom lengths.

This elastic will make staying safe this year easier. Pick your favorite pattern online, cut up an old pillowcase, and stitch up a few washable masks for your family.

Packed in a gorgeous, sturdy aluminum case, you'll find everything needed for months of art fun. There are rainbows of colored pencils, markers, oil pastels, watercolors, and tempera. Two palettes, four small brushes (two flat and two round), a pencil sharpener, and two erasers are also included. Wax crayons are represented by six color-changing blocks (chunks of several colors smashed together for random fun.)

If you are taking a camping trip this summer this is the perfect thing to take along. All your child will need is some water and several pads of mixed media paper for hours of entertainment. No electricity required. The neat case makes the set perfect for the back seat or a relative's home.

Aunties and nanas, attention: keep this in a drawer for times when kids visit. It would also be a great holiday or birthday gift.

When I opened this up, I felt like I was eight with the BEST present I ever got in my life! Sometimes, all of us need something like this no matter how old we are. (These are real art materials and not just for kids anyway.)

These beautiful pencils don't come in a fancy metal case. (The cardboard one with a plastic insert is fine, but my point is, all the manufacturing resources have been focused on the pencils themselves.) The hardness range is broader than usual (4H-12B along with B, H, and HB). The softer leads lay down rich darks and the harder ones sharpen to fine points for the most delicate applications. The ones I have used sharpen evenly in a hand-held pencil sharpener (they come pre-sharpened and ready to use).

With schools cutting arts budgets in the fall, these pencils would make a great gift for students in grades 5 and above. Pair them with the terrific drawing course from The Great Courses and you will have delivered the gift of seeing for life. If you don't want to invest in the course, there are many great drawing lessons online or in books from your local library. (Some libraries even have the drawing course.)

These pencils aren't just for students, though. This is a real professional set. Use them to restock those metal cases I was talking about. They're great for sketching, planning, and fine art drawing.

This paper is heavyweight and beautiful The description says "canvas," which it isn't. It is high quality paper that's black all the way through. You can paint on both sides and it won't soak through. It comes in a gum-bound pad with the edges of the foldable framing smoothly perforated. (I would advise having a craft knife handy to keep the edges extra-clean.

I painted compositions based on my Saddlehorn Series in white acrylic marker (Tavolozza) on both sides. I prefer the effect on what I call the front (second photo) because when you fold it, there are holes in the "back" so you can slip it over a nail and it will sit against the wall like a mini gallery-wrap.

In the first photo you see the effect of the "back." That's where the craft knife comes in. If you plan to create a frame for the fridge (you'll need to add an adhesive magnet to the back), be extra-careful punching out the art from the pad. Pay special attention to the small tabs and the slots they will insert into. My corners look very rough because I was experimenting and had a little trouble figuring it out. I painted the top edges gray with acrylic. I'm not thrilled with my craftmanship on this side, but it gives you the idea. I did another "Saddlehorn Series" sketch in white acrylic, but I could have affixed a photograph or other small graphic to this side.

You can paint the "frame" sections of either or both sides. I recommend doing so before punching it out of the pad. It's easier to mask and paint when it's flat.

Folding the "frame" took a little thought and experimentation, but it wasn't hard. The Origami (winks) is very well engineered and comes out perfectly if you are careful when punching it out.

This product is fun for all ability levels. The youngest artists will need help folding the frame, but the results are extremely lightweight and handsome. They would look great in a grouping in a hallway or small room, The side I call the "back" would be perfect for the fridge door. A stick-on magnet would hold it up with power to spare.

I look forward to experimenting more with this innovative product.

It's listed for middle-schoolers, and they would certainly find it useful. Anybody would. College students, crafters and artists will love it too. The outside is elegantly simple and the inside beautifully lined. It's even slightly padded.

There's an inside net pocket and below it a smaller pocket with a velcro flap. The latter could hold membership or credit cards. There are five pen or pencil holding elastics sewn on a flap inside that flips over to reveal a large compartment with a removable divider.

I put art supplies in it. It holds two water brushes, a long narrow palette and a 6 X 4" sketch diary. This would make a nice gift, especially if stocked with the recipient's favorite craft supplies. It's handsome and well-made.
Nice case for home, school, office, studio, or even travel.

This is a great gift for yourself, or any aspiring artist. The pencils sharpen up beautifully. There is even a knife included to do the job properly, but you can use a portable sharpener (not a good idea with the softer leads or charcoal, but you can do it, as with all pencil sets.) The case with a strap and snap closure opens out flat to lie across your table and keep everything handy.

The set includes graphite pencils in a full range from hard to soft and even charcoal pencils. Elastic straps are tight enough to hold all but loose enough not to impede access.

It's suggested for beginners, but it's good for anyone who likes working in graphite. I know I have several pencil sets stashed around the house and in my car. Treat yourself.

I live in western Colorado. It's a land of red rock cliffs, vineyards, ranches, and orchards. Springtime is great for painting outdoors, but changeable weather, including sudden rain, gusting wind, and even snow makes it difficult to set up and finish a painting.

Different artists here have different solutions. I've developed a method that works for me. I sketch the scene in pencil, go over the lines I like with waterproof artist pens, erase the pencil lines, then, very quickly, suggest the color with a portable watercolor box and water pens. I can do a painting in a few minutes sitting on a folding stool or in my car if the weather turns.

I have a lot of water pens. They are wonderful. You fill them at home, drop a couple in your kit, and go. You don't need a bulky water bottle (unless you want something to drink.) You just wet your color pans, and use the brushes build into the pen.

A few things are different with this set of pens. The water reservoirs (you could put ink or paint in them if you wish. Some people do. I've never done that) are rigid. The other pens I've used have squeezable water compartments. To deliver water to the brushes in this set, you compress a soft green button on the rigid body of the pen.

Another thing I've never seen before is the ability to make a fuller wash brush for larger paper. There's a removable plastic collar on the flat brushes in the set that allows you to use a small flat (which I do, because I work small) or a larger brush with much longer bristles.

Here's a hint. The reservoir opens near the top. You can see the plastic threading above the green button. You will probably try to turn it the wrong way and wonder why it doesn't open. Most brushes I've used open by turning to the right. These turn the other way. If you have trouble opening the brush, turn it the other way. The green piece at the end of the brush could, theoretically, be used to clean the reservoir if you use ink or paint, but be careful, it fits very tightly. (on purpose, you don't want the brush leaking all over your bag.)

The synthetic bristles are firm but flexible. Use a light touch and they'll deliver the paint where you want it. Wash the brush with mild soap and water when you get home, if necessary. Most paint just wipes off with a rag, paper towel, tissue, or even your jeans (winks.)

My main medium is acrylic on canvas, but I like to go outside to paint when the weather is nice here in Western Colorado. Some other artists here use pastels or oil en Plein Air, but I've been more successful with watercolor and in my sketchbook, I have lots of little sets of paints I've picked up here and there over the years, but this is one of the nicest. It's much larger than most travel sets, which could be a problem if you're really limited for space in an airline bag, but it's very flat. It has tons of gorgeous colors and a nice water brush (I love these things) is included. It fits right inside and the whole thing snaps tight. (I never trust these latches. On a trip, even though I've had no problem with this, I'd bring a heavy-duty elastic or rubber band just in case.)

This would make an amazing gift for a beginner, but experienced artists might want to add it to their kit.

My husband needed white pastels to add a realistic weathered touch to his HO scale model brick and wood (plastic) buildings. Most sets of soft pastels contain only one stick of white. Hard pastels for drawing are easier to find in white, but they are too waxy for this purpose. These sticks are perfect. They are just the right hardness--not as soft as fine art brands such as Rembrandt, but not as hard as drawing sticks. I use pastels too. I often go to drawing sessions with a model. I like to work on mid-toned paper such as Canson MiTientes[[ASIN:B000IG2OYG Canson Mi-Teintes Pastel Assorted Pad 12 in. x 16 in.]] I use charcoal for the darks and white pastels such as these for the lights. It's great to know I can buy a set with nothing but white.

I love Fiskars products. They perform well and they really last. These new scissors for crafters are no exception. The orange you see on the handle is actually a kind of cushioning that really helps when you clamp down on thick materials. Speaking of thick materials, I cut through eight sheets of colored bond in one clean slice. I also cut heavy corrugated cardboard without bending it or making my hand cramp up. My husband works with polystyrene sheets when he builds models for his railroad layout, and we tried these scissors on some scrap. They work fine. I even used them to cut an expired credit card into a curvy abstract design I could use as a stencil. They work so well because they are very very sharp. Be sure to use the included guard sleeve and keep them out of the reach of young children. The sleeve comes off by lifting up on the orange tab near the top. Then, when you slip it back on, the hole in the sleeve fits right back over the raised white button on the side of the scissors. Note: You will need a craft knife, a razor blade, or heavy scissors to cut the plastic band that holds the scissors in their packaging. It is really strong. You will have fun thinking of ways to use this exciting new tool.

I can never go back to normal resin curing again. This mat helps to cure my resin pieces in record time so no more waiting a day before I can actually unmold my pieces and then having to let them set up longer to harden. Okay, I use a lot of UV resin but when it comes to the colored UV resin I haven't used many of them. This UV resin works perfectly and just as I was hoping it would work. The biggest con I have with these tho is they smell absolutely horrendous! I mean they smell horrible and the smell is right from the moment I opened the box they were shipped to me in. What's worse, is the smell doesn't go away until after the resin is cured. Honestly, I probably shouldn't keep these in my house but I do have them in their own container and that seems to keep the smell just to that area.
When it comes to resin you should always use the proper equipment when you're using it but with this resin, I feel like you should use the equipment even before you start using it. The smell is super strong so just be aware of that when you buy it and then receive this product.

What's worse, is the smell doesn't go away until after the resin is cured. Honestly, I probably shouldn't keep these in my house but I do have them in their own container and that seems to keep the smell just to that area.
When it comes to resin you should always use the proper equipment when you're using it but with this resin, I feel like you should use the equipment even before you start using it. The smell is super strong so just be aware of that when you buy it and then receive this product.

I am in love with this overlay paper. I have my own small business and make my own business cards. I wanted to add that extra something to them and this was perfect. It's easy to lay over the sheet after it's been printed and then cut the cards out. It's thick plastic so it makes the cards much more durable. This overlay is great and I am in love with it. These make great keychains and also pendants too. I can make them however I want and they always pop out of the mold. These are great and instead of buying acrylic blanks, now I can just make my own personalized blank to put whatever vinyl I want on them.

Great cardstock that I've already used to make super cute roses for a shadowbox and the cutter is honestly awesome too. It cuts paper so smoothly but also cuts through vinyl without any problems. Very happy with this set.

Very happy with this kit.

I have been wanting to try scorch pens for the longest time so I was so happy to try these out. I am not disappointed. These work just as intended and make the most beautiful designs in wood. They are super easy to use, just color in the places you want burned or scorched and use a heat gun on the places and it burns right into the wood. It works beautifully and honestly, it works so much easier than using actual wood burning tool. Have already bought more and use them to make some of the most beautiful wood pieces.
These are awesome for my dried flowers, beads, charms, whatever you want to use them for. They lock tight and I don't worry about them opening and spilling everything around if I have them in my bag.
Love this mold. I've had no problems from it and it has worked great for a few pieces now. Easy to use, makes the perfect sized tray and looks great.

These are great for making all kinds of keychains. These work great for vinyl work but also if you wanted to paint on these, they work great for that too. People are loving these and I am loving making so many awesome and beautiful keychains.

This is a great carrying case and storage case for my Cricut. It makes transporting it so much easier and protects it too. It's super well made and is just awesome to have for when I do need to take my Cricut somewhere.

I have been seeing these storage boxes online for the longest time and have wanted to make my own since. These are so super fun and I am just loving them.

Great chain that is working great for my craft pieces. It makes a great chain for hanging the different things that I make. It's nice and thick and isn't dainty so I know it's going to hold up. It's easy to use and I can make it whatever length I want. Very happy with this chain.
These wood slices are great and have been amazing on so many of my projects. These take paint beautifully and are easy to use and paint on. I am so happy with these and they make beautiful projects.

I want so many more of these large silicone measuring cups. This cup is different from all of the other ones I have. First, it isn't just a normal round shape but instead, it's oval and is thicker towards the back of the cup while it is thinner towards the front which totally helps when pouring the resin and even mixing the resin. These cups are easy to clean because they are silicone and are just awesome and I totally love them. Totally going to get more soon because as I said the bigger one is the best.

Loving this resin and it's working great in all my resin projects. There is very little odor when mixing it and totally has a great 40 min work time. Pigments and glitter mix in with no problems and cures within about 12 hrs or so and is rock hard in about 24 hrs. Also cures crystal clear with very few bubbles. Very good resin and I am happy with it.

This is a great mold and honestly such a beautiful way to memorialize your pet. I've just started making pet memorials with these and my customers are loving them. They love the beautiful message that comes on this but also that I can either add a photo of their pet or they can use it as a tea light holder as well.
The mold itself is very well made and is thick enough that it can sit up on its own without problems. Just a great mold that I am very happy with.

These are such cute earring molds. I've already been making all kinds of different colors and people are loving them. They are pretty thick though but I can easily change the thickness by how much resin I put into the molds. Love these though and am so happy with them.

These molds are beautiful but also easy to use. They make the most beautiful pieces and I love using them. I have made all kinds of things from these and they are working beautifully. Great molds for sure.

Loving this beautiful glow-in-the-dark glitter. It works awesome in resin and the glow is beautiful and intense. I love using it in earrings and it gives them that extra something that you don't often see in jewelry. This hobby knife set is really great and super sharp. The scissors are great and cut through things like butter. The only problem I have with this set is the way they sent the mat along with everything else. The way everything is packaged, the mat is wrapped and bent around the other items. This wouldn't be so much of a problem if it would actually bend back to flat. I've put this thing under heavy books, under the mattress, under bricks, you name it and it's still wavy and not flat at all. It's a great set but I really wish the mat would have been packaged better.

These bezels are great and are working awesomely for UV resin pieces but also for resin pieces too. They are very well made and finished beautifully. These are perfect for making all kinds of beautiful jewelry pieces. Very happy with these.

These shimmer markers are great and are so juicy. They work great for drawing or writing but they also work great on your resin projects too. They add a beautiful sparkle to anything you are working on. Very happy with these. They are beautiful.

I have just started making faux leather jewelry such as earrings and bracelets and this is exactly what I have been looking for. I ended up getting this leather hole punch and it has been amazing. I can change out the size of the hole with just a turn of the top and it's nice and sharp and goes through the faux leather with no problems. I do pour painting and have been looking for something different to use as canvases and these work great. This UV resin is great. I have used it for plenty of projects and every time it dries within 2 mins under a UV lamp and doesn't yellow or anything else. It holds strong and is beautifully crystal clear. It works amazing at doming pieces as well and just looks beautiful. Very happy with this resin.

At first, I wasn't sure how much I would like this mold but now that I have used it and made something cool from it, I have to say that I don't hate it. First, you can not use this as an actual bow, it is not kid-sized by any means, and is more for decoration than anything else. I hate false advertising and that is exactly what those photos are doing. So don't be fooled by the pictures used on here. However, the mold itself is good, very detailed, and something fun and unique to make. I am a Sagittarius so that was another reason this appealed to me so much. I ended up making it into a wall hanging and I am loving it and have already been making more.

I got this to airbrush canvases before I put vinyl pieces onto them and so far it has worked great. I've been able to use the actual airbrush with this and so far it's been working just fine. I use a thin type of paint and always clean it once I am finished using it so nothing has a chance to dry inside the actual device. The compressor though is where this machine really shines because it's one of the best I've had, and I've gone through a few. I can switch out the airbrushes with other ones and this compressor handles them like a champ. Totally worth the money just for the compressor to be honest.
